**(b)** Explain the need for **aggregation** in the COMPANY database. Show how aggregation is used to model the statement: *"A department sponsors an employee working on a project."* Provide a clear Mermaid/textual diagram. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Refined EER Design with Specialization (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Base Schema (1 mark):**
- EMPLOYEE (SSN, Name, BDate, Address, Salary, Sex, **JobType**)
- DEPARTMENT (DNumber, DName, Locations)
- PROJECT (PNumber, PName, Location)
- DEPENDENT (Dependent_Name, Sex, BDate, Relationship) — **weak entity**

**Step 2 — Specialization (3 marks):**
The discriminating attribute is `JobType`. The superclass EMPLOYEE is specialized into:

| Subclass | Specific Attribute |
|---|---|
| SECRETARY | `TypingSpeed` (WPM) |
| ENGINEER | `EngDegree`, `EngType` |
| MANAGER | `Bonus`, `StockOptions` |
| TECHNICIAN | `TradeCert` |

Each subclass inherits the `SSN` primary key from EMPLOYEE.

**Step 3 — Constraints (2 marks):**
- **Disjointness:** `d` (an employee belongs to exactly one job-type subclass at a time)
- **Completeness:** `p` (partial) — a newly hired employee with `JobType = NULL` is not in any subclass.
- **Cardinalities:** 
  - WORKS_FOR: EMPLOYEE (N:1) DEPARTMENT
  - MANAGES: EMPLOYEE (1:1) DEPARTMENT
  - CONTROLS: DEPARTMENT (1:N) PROJECT
  - DEPENDENTS_OF: EMPLOYEE (1:N) DEPENDENT (identifying relationship)

**Step 4 — Weak Entity Treatment (1 mark):**
DEPENDENT has a **partial key** `Dependent_Name` and is identified by the combination `(Dependent_Name, ESSN)` where `ESSN` is a foreign key referencing EMPLOYEE's `SSN`.

---

**(b) Aggregation Explanation (7 marks):**

**Why Aggregation is Needed (3 marks):**

The basic ER model has a fundamental restriction: a **relationship cannot be related to another relationship**. Consider the real-world fact in the COMPANY database:

> *"A department sponsors an employee working on a project for a certain number of hours."*

In this statement, the **WORKS_ON** relationship (between EMPLOYEE and PROJECT) must itself participate in a **SPONSORS** relationship (between DEPARTMENT and the employee-project assignment). The basic ER model cannot represent this directly.

**Aggregation Solution (3 marks):**

**Aggregation** treats the WORKS_ON relationship as a higher-level entity, called `WORKS_ON_AGG`, which can then participate in the SPONSORS relationship with DEPARTMENT.

**Diagrammatic Representation (1 mark):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    EMP[EMPLOYEE]:::entity
    PROJ[PROJECT]:::entity
    DEPT[DEPARTMENT]:::entity
    WORKS_ON_REL([WORKS_ON]):::rel
    SPONSORS_REL([SPONSORS]):::rel
    AGG_BOX{{AGGREGATED WORKS_ON}}:::agg

    EMP --- WORKS_ON_REL
    PROJ --- WORKS_ON_REL
    WORKS_ON_REL ==> AGG_BOX
    AGG_BOX --- SPONSORS_REL
    DEPT --- SPONSORS_REL

    classDef entity fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1
    classDef rel fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#4A148C
    classDef agg fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20
```

The dashed box around the WORKS_ON relationship denotes aggregation. The SPONSORS relationship then connects DEPARTMENT to the aggregated entity.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of **Generalization** and **Specialization**. Discuss the **disjointness** (`d`) and **overlapping** (`o`) constraints with a COMPANY database example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **weak entity**? How is the DEPENDENT entity modeled as a weak entity in the COMPANY database? Discuss the **identifying relationship** and **partial key** concepts. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Generalization & Specialization (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Specialization (Top-Down):** Defining one or more subclasses of a superclass based on a distinguishing attribute. Formula: $E_{\text{super}} \rightarrow \{E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n\}$.
- **Generalization (Bottom-Up):** Combining multiple entity types that share common attributes into a single superclass. Formula: $\{E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n\} \rightarrow E_{\text{super}}$.

**Diagrammatic View (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    EMP[EMPLOYEE]:::super
    SEC[SECRETARY]:::sub
    ENG[ENGINEER]:::sub
    MGR[MANAGER]:::sub

    EMP --- SEC
    EMP --- ENG
    EMP --- MGR

    SEC -.-|d constraint| SEC
    ENG -.-|d constraint| ENG
    MGR -.-|o constraint| MGR

    classDef super fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:3px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef sub fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,color:#F57F17
```

**Constraints Discussion (3 marks):**

| Constraint | Meaning | COMPANY Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Disjoint (`d`)** | An employee instance belongs to exactly ONE subclass | SECRETARY and ENGINEER are mutually exclusive job types |
| **Overlapping (`o`)** | An employee instance can belong to MULTIPLE subclasses | MANAGER and ENGINEER can overlap — a person can be both |

The choice between `d` and `o` depends on **business rules**. KTU typically expects the student to **state the assumption** before drawing the diagram.

---

**(b) Weak Entity Modeling (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **weak entity** is an entity type whose instances cannot be uniquely identified by their own attributes alone and depend on a **strong (owner) entity** for identification.

**Formal Rule (2 marks):**

$$ E_w \text{ is weak} \Leftrightarrow \nexists \, A \subseteq \text{Attrs}(E_w) \text{ such that } A \text{ is a primary key} $$

DEPENDENT cannot be uniquely identified by `Dependent_Name` alone because two different employees could each have a dependent named "Rahul."

**COMPANY Database Modeling (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    EMP[EMPLOYEE]:::entity
    DEP[DEPENDENT]:::weakentity
    DEP_OF([DEPENDENTS_OF]):::identrel

    EMP --- DEP_OF
    DEP --- DEP_OF
    DEP -.->|partial key Dependent_Name| DEP

    classDef entity fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1
    classDef weakentity fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:3px,color:#B71C1C
    classDef identrel fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:3px,color:#4A148C
```

- DEPENDENT is drawn as a **double rectangle**.
- DEPENDENTS_OF is the **identifying relationship**, drawn as a **double diamond**.
- `Dependent_Name` is the **partial key** (discriminator) — drawn with a **dashed underline**.
- The full primary key of DEPENDENT is `(ESSN, Dependent_Name)`, where `ESSN` is a foreign key to EMPLOYEE's `SSN`.

**Cascade Delete Rule (1 mark):** If an EMPLOYEE is deleted, all their DEPENDENT records must also be deleted (existence dependency).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting inheritance of primary key** from superclass to subclass — full **–2 marks** deduction.
> 2. **Drawing a single line instead of double line** for the weak entity rectangle — examiner marks 0 for weak entity identification.
> 3. **Confusing aggregation with composition** — aggregation allows independent existence; composition implies ownership. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Failing to state the (min, max) cardinality** for every relationship in Part B — KTU 2024 valuation keys explicitly check for this.
> 5. **Not labelling the discriminating attribute** in specialization diagrams — mandatory for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Refining the COMPANY ER Design:**

- ☐ **Refinement ≠ Redesign** — it is an *enhancement* of a valid base ER schema using EER constructs.
- ☐ **Specialization** is **top-down**; **Generalization** is **bottom-up**; both produce the same EER diagram.
- ☐ The **discriminating attribute** (e.g., `JobType`) drives specialization. Always state it explicitly.
- ☐ **Disjointness (`d`)** vs **Overlap (`o`)** — choose based on business rules; state your assumption.
- ☐ **Total participation (`t`)** vs **Partial participation (`p`)** — represented by double vs single line.
- ☐ **Weak entity** = double rectangle; **identifying relationship** = double diamond; **partial key** = dashed underline.
- ☐ **Aggregation** treats a relationship as a higher-level entity (dashed box around diamond). It is the ONLY way to relate a relationship to another entity/relationship.
- ☐ **Composition** = filled diamond on the owner side; implies lifecycle dependency (cascade delete).
- ☐ **Derived attributes** are drawn as **dashed ovals** (e.g., `Age` derived from `BDate`).
- ☐ Subclasses **inherit the primary key** of the superclass — this is non-negotiable in KTU valuation.
- ☐ The COMPANY database must always show **cardinality ratios** (1:1, 1:N, M:N) on relationship edges.
- ☐ Before drawing any EER diagram in the exam, **list all entities, relationships, attributes, and constraints** — this structured approach fetches method marks even if the final diagram has minor errors.
- ☐ **Aggregation vs Ternary Relationship** — don't use a ternary relationship where aggregation is the more accurate semantic construct.
- ☐ The refined EER schema is the **direct input** for the relational mapping algorithm (covered in later modules).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Relational Data Model and SQL: The Relational Data Model and Relational Database Constraints - Relational Algebra and Relational Calculus - Structured Query Language (SQL) - Data Definition Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT404 - DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Relational Data Model and SQL: The Relational Data Model and Relational Database Constraints - Relational Algebra and Relational Calculus - Structured Query Language (SQL) - Data Definition Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Relational Data Model — Formal Definition

The **Relational Data Model** is a formal, mathematical framework proposed by **Edgar F. Codd (1970)** for representing data as a collection of **relations** (mathematical sets), where each relation is perceived by the user as a **table** of values. The model uses the mathematical concept of *set theory* and *first-order predicate logic* as its theoretical foundation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A relation is a *subset of the Cartesian product of one or more domains*. A domain is a set of allowable atomic values (e.g., integers, strings, dates).

**Core Mathematical Definition:**

$$ R \subseteq D_1 \times D_2 \times D_3 \times \ldots \times D_n $$

Where:
- $R$ is the relation (table).
- $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n$ are the domains (column value pools).
- Each row in $R$ is called a **tuple** $t = \langle v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n \rangle$.
- Each column is called an **attribute** $A_i$ with domain $D_i$.

**Schema vs. Instance:**

| Term | Definition | Notation | Real-World Analogy |
|------|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| **Relation Schema** | The structural/intangible design of a relation (its name, attributes, and domains) | $R(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n)$ | Blueprint of a house |
| **Relation Instance** | The actual, time-varying set of tuples stored at a given moment | $r(R)$ or $r$ | The furnished, lived-in house |
| **Degree (arity)** | Number of attributes $n$ in the schema | $n$ | Number of rooms |
| **Cardinality** | Number of tuples currently in the instance | $\vert r \vert$ | Number of occupants |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Spreadsheet Mental Model:**
> Think of a relation as a **giant spreadsheet**.
> - The **column headers** (EmployeeID, Name, Salary) are the *attributes* — fixed by the schema.
> - Each **row** is one *tuple* — a single real-world fact.
> - The **pool of allowed values** for a column (e.g., Salary must be a positive number) is the *domain*.
> - The **rule that two rows cannot be identical** mirrors the *set* property of mathematics.

### 1.3 Relational Database Constraints — Formal Categories

A **constraint** is a restriction on the data that the database must always satisfy. KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the following three classes of constraints, in the order of decreasing scope:

1. **Inherent/Implicit Model-Based Constraints** — e.g., no duplicate tuples (set property).
2. **Schema-Based Constraints** — explicit rules declared in the DDL (KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK, FOREIGN KEY).
3. **Application-Based Constraints** — business rules enforced via triggers or application logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Constraints:** Primary Key, Entity Integrity, Referential Integrity, Domain Integrity, and Null Integrity.

### 1.4 Relational Algebra & Relational Calculus — The Two Query Languages

- **Relational Algebra (RA):** A *procedural* language — the user specifies **how** to retrieve the data using a sequence of algebraic operations (operators take relations as input and return a relation as output — *closure property*).
- **Relational Calculus (RC):** A *non-procedural/declarative* language — the user specifies **what** data to retrieve using first-order predicate logic. Two flavors:
  - *Tuple Relational Calculus (TRC)*
  - *Domain Relational Calculus (DRC)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Codd's Theorem:** Relational Algebra, TRC, and DRC have **equivalent expressive power** (safely restricted forms are used in practice).

### 1.5 Structured Query Language (SQL) & Data Definition Language (DDL)

**SQL** is the standard, declarative, English-like language for defining, manipulating, and controlling relational databases. It is broadly categorized into:

| Sub-language | Purpose | Common Commands |
|--------------|---------|-----------------|
| **DDL** (Data Definition Language) | Defines/changes the *schema* | CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE, RENAME |
| **DML** (Data Manipulation Language) | Manages *data instances* | SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE |
| **DCL** (Data Control Language) | Manages *permissions* | GRANT, REVOKE |
| **TCL** (Transaction Control Language) | Manages *transactions* | COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 Focus:** This module stresses **DDL commands** (CREATE TABLE with constraints, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE) and the **theoretical foundations (RA + RC)** that underpin the SELECT statement.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Properties of a Relation (KTU Frequently Tested)

1. **No duplicate tuples** — a relation is a *set* of tuples, not a multiset.
2. **Tuples are unordered** — the sequence of rows carries no semantic meaning.
3. **Attributes are unordered** — the column order is irrelevant; the schema name specifies the attribute.
4. **Atomic attribute values** — First Normal Form (1NF) requirement; values must be *indivisible*.
5. **Null values** are permitted (representing *unknown* or *not applicable* or *undefined*).

### 2.2 Integrity Constraints — Deep Dive

**A. Key Constraint (Uniqueness):**
- A **Super Key** is any set of attributes whose values uniquely identify a tuple.
- A **Candidate Key** is a *minimal* super key (no proper subset is a super key).
- The **Primary Key** is the *chosen* candidate key (cannot be NULL).
- A **Unique Key** is any other candidate key that is not the primary key (typically allows NULL).

**B. Entity Integrity Constraint:**
No attribute that is part of the primary key may be NULL.

$$ \forall t \in r(R): \;\; t[\text{PK}] \neq \text{NULL} $$

**C. Referential Integrity Constraint (Foreign Key):**
A foreign key value in a referencing relation must either *match* an existing primary key value in the referenced relation or be NULL.

$$ \forall t_1 \in r_1(R_1), \;\forall \text{FK in } R_1: \;\; t_1[\text{FK}] \in \pi_{\text{PK}}(r_2(R_2)) \;\cup\; \{\text{NULL}\} $$

**D. Domain Integrity:**
Every attribute value must be drawn from its declared domain $D_i$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Relational Algebra Operators

| Operator | Symbol | Type | Input | Output | Definition (set form) |
|----------|--------|------|-------|--------|------------------------|
| **Selection** | $\sigma_{\theta}(R)$ | Unary | One relation | Subset of rows | $\{\,t \in R \mid \theta(t) = \text{TRUE}\,\}$ |
| **Projection** | $\pi_{A_1, \ldots, A_k}(R)$ | Unary | One relation | Subset of columns | $\{\,t[A_1,\ldots,A_k] \mid t \in R\,\}$ |
| **Cartesian Product** | $R \times S$ | Binary | Two relations | Concatenation | $\{\,(t_r, t_s) \mid t_r \in R,\; t_s \in S\,\}$ |
| **Union** | $R \cup S$ | Binary | Two relations | Rows in either | $R \cup S$ (set union) |
| **Set Difference** | $R - S$ | Binary | Two relations | Rows in $R$ only | $R \setminus S$ |
| **Rename** | $\rho_{X(B_1,\ldots,B_n)}(R)$ | Unary | One relation | Renamed | $\rho$ |
| **Join (Theta)** | $R \bowtie_{\theta} S$ | Binary | Two relations | $\sigma_{\theta}(R \times S)$ | Combined |
| **Natural Join** | $R \bowtie S$ | Binary | Two relations | Equijoin on common attrs | Auto-joins on shared names |
| **Division** | $R \div S$ | Binary | Two relations | Quotient | $\{\,t \mid \forall t_s \in S,\; (t, t_s) \in R\,\}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Spot:** Understand the **division** operator deeply. It is the algebraic expression of the universal quantifier $\forall$ and answers *"Find entities associated with ALL of something."*

### 2.4 Extended Relational Algebra Operators

| Operator | Symbol | Purpose |
|----------|--------|---------|
| Generalized Projection | $\pi_{F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n}(R)$ | Allow arithmetic expressions in projection |
| Aggregate Functions | $_{G_1, G_2, \ldots} \mathcal{G}_{F_1, F_2, \ldots}(R)$ | SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, COUNT |
| Outer Join | $R \leftouterjoin S$, $R \rightouterjoin S$, $R \fullouterjoin S$ | Preserves dangling tuples with NULLs |
| Semi-Join | $R \ltimes S$ | Rows of $R$ that join with $S$ |

### 2.5 Tuple Relational Calculus (TRC) — Form

A TRC query has the form:
$$ \{t \mid P(t)\} $$
Where $t$ is a tuple variable and $P(t)$ is a *well-formed formula* built from:
- **Atoms:** $t \in R$, $t[A] \;\theta\; s[B]$, $t[A] \;\theta\; c$
- **Logical connectives:** $\land, \lor, \lnot$
- **Quantifiers:** $\exists, \forall$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Condition:** A query is *safe* if all resulting tuples are guaranteed to be in the database (i.e., the result is finite).

### 2.6 Domain Relational Calculus (DRC) — Form

A DRC query has the form:
$$ \{ \langle x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \rangle \mid P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n, x_{n+1}, \ldots, x_{n+m}) \} $$
Where $x_i$ are *domain variables* taking values from domains.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Relational Algebra** is the *theoretical foundation* for **query optimization** in DBMS engines. The query optimizer transforms a high-level SQL query into an *equivalent relational algebra expression tree* and applies cost-based optimization (e.g., pushing selections down to reduce I/O).
- **Relational Calculus** is the basis for **declarative query languages** like QBE (Query-By-Example) and influences modern languages like Datalog.
- **SQL DDL** is the everyday tool for database administrators (DBAs) to *define schemas*, enforce integrity, and evolve databases over time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Natural Join Eliminates Duplicate Attributes

**Step 1.** Given two relations $R(A, B)$ and $S(B, C)$, take the Cartesian product:
$$ R \times S = \{\, (a, b_1, b_2, c) \mid (a, b_1) \in R \;\land\; (b_2, c) \in S \,\} $$

**Step 2.** Apply the equijoin $\bowtie_{R.B = S.B}$:
$$ R \bowtie_{B} S = \sigma_{R.B = S.B}(R \times S) = \{\, (a, b, c) \mid (a,b) \in R \;\land\; (b,c) \in S \,\} $$

**Step 3.** Notice that the *two* $B$ attributes (now redundant) collapse into a single column. The Natural Join is therefore defined as:

$$ R \bowtie S = \pi_{A, B, C}\bigl(\sigma_{R.B = S.B}(R \times S)\bigr) $$

> **Conversion Logic:** Natural Join = Equijoin on common attributes + Project to remove duplicate column names.

### 3.2 Derivation: Equivalence of Division to Universal Quantifier

**Goal:** Show that the division operator
$$ R \div S = \pi_{A}(R) - \pi_{A}\bigl((\pi_{A}(R) \times S) - R\bigr) $$
captures the logical statement *"Find all $A$ values that are paired with **every** $B$ in $S$."*

**Step 1.** Compute the set of candidate $A$ values: $C = \pi_{A}(R)$.

**Step 2.** Form the cross product $C \times S$ — this contains *every possible pairing* of an $A$ with some $B \in S$.

**Step 3.** Subtract the actual $R$ tuples: $(C \times S) - R$ yields the "missing" pairings.

**Step 4.** Project on $A$ to find which $A$ values are missing some pairing: $M = \pi_{A}\bigl((C \times S) - R\bigr)$.

**Step 5.** The desired $A$ values are those *not* in $M$:
$$ R \div S = C - M $$

**Conclusion:** An $A$ survives only if, in the original $R$, it is paired with **every** $B$ in $S$ — precisely the meaning of the universal quantifier $\forall$.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Translating an English Query to RA and TRC

**Schema:**
- `STUDENT(SID, SNAME, DEPT)` — Primary Key: `SID`
- `COURSE(CID, CNAME, CREDITS)` — Primary Key: `CID`
- `ENROLL(SID, CID, GRADE)` — Foreign Keys: `SID` → STUDENT, `CID` → COURSE

**English Query (Q):** *"Find the names of students who have enrolled in **every** course."*

**(A) Relational Algebra Expression:**

$$
\pi_{\text{SNAME}}\!\left(\text{STUDENT} \bowtie \bigl(\pi_{\text{SID, CID}}(\text{ENROLL}) \div \pi_{\text{CID}}(\text{COURSE})\bigr)\right)
$$

**Step-by-step breakdown:**

Step 1: $\pi_{\text{CID}}(\text{COURSE})$ — Extract the set of all course IDs.
*Conversion Logic:* Get the universe of courses the student must cover.

Step 2: $\pi_{\text{SID, CID}}(\text{ENROLL})$ — Reduce ENROLL to (SID, CID) pairs.
*Conversion Logic:* Remove the GRADE attribute to focus on the relationship.

Step 3: $(\text{Step 2}) \div (\text{Step 1})$ — Find SIDs associated with *all* CIDs.
*Conversion Logic:* Apply division to obtain the universal answer.

Step 4: $\text{STUDENT} \bowtie (\text{Step 3})$ — Join with STUDENT to get SNAME.
*Conversion Logic:* Restore descriptive attributes for presentation.

Step 5: $\pi_{\text{SNAME}}(\text{Step 4})$ — Output the names.
*Conversion Logic:* Final projection to display only requested attribute.

**(B) Tuple Relational Calculus Expression:**

$$
\{\, t.\text{SNAME} \mid t \in \text{STUDENT} \;\land\; \forall u \in \text{COURSE},\; \exists e \in \text{ENROLL}\; (e.\text{SID} = t.\text{SID} \;\land\; e.\text{CID} = u.\text{CID}) \,\}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** For every course $u$, there must exist an enrollment $e$ linking $t$ to $u$ — the formal $\forall \exists$ pattern.

### 3.4 DDL Worked Example — Defining a Schema with All Constraints

```sql
CREATE TABLE DEPARTMENT (
    DEPT_ID      NUMBER(4)       PRIMARY KEY,
    DEPT_NAME    VARCHAR2(30)    NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    LOCATION     VARCHAR2(20)    DEFAULT 'KOCHI',
    HOD_ID       NUMBER(6),
    BUDGET       NUMBER(10,2)    CHECK (BUDGET > 0)
);

CREATE TABLE EMPLOYEE (
    EMP_ID       NUMBER(6)       PRIMARY KEY,
    ENAME        VARCHAR2(30)    NOT NULL,
    SALARY       NUMBER(10,2)    CHECK (SALARY BETWEEN 10000 AND 500000),
    DEPT_ID      NUMBER(4),
    CONSTRAINT   FK_DEPT         FOREIGN KEY (DEPT_ID)
                                 REFERENCES DEPARTMENT(DEPT_ID)
                                 ON DELETE SET NULL
                                 ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
```

**Explanation of each construct:**
- `PRIMARY KEY` enforces entity integrity + uniqueness.
- `NOT NULL` disallows missing values in mandatory columns.
- `UNIQUE` on DEPT_NAME prevents duplicate department names.
- `DEFAULT` provides an automatic value when none is supplied.
- `CHECK` enforces domain integrity via a Boolean predicate.
- `FOREIGN KEY ... REFERENCES` enforces referential integrity.
- `ON DELETE SET NULL` — on parent deletion, child FK becomes NULL.
- `ON UPDATE CASCADE` — on parent PK update, child FK is auto-updated.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — TRC vs DRC vs RA vs SQL

| Feature | Relational Algebra (RA) | Tuple Relational Calculus (TRC) | Domain Relational Calculus (DRC) | SQL |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------|-----|
| **Type** | Procedural | Declarative | Declarative | Declarative (in practice) |
| **Quantifiers** | Implicit (via division) | Explicit ($\forall, \exists$) | Explicit ($\forall, \exists$) | EXISTS, NOT EXISTS |
| **Quantification Order** | Step-by-step | $\forall$ outermost | $\forall$ outermost | Subquery nesting |
| **Multi-row Output** | Via projection | Multiple free variables | Multiple free variables | SELECT clause |
| **Turing Complete?** | No (basic RA) | No (safe TRC) | No (safe DRC) | Yes (in commercial systems) |
| **Order of Tuple** | Unordered | Unordered | Unordered | Unordered (set) / Ordered (with ORDER BY) |
| **Duplicates** | Eliminated (set) | Eliminated | Eliminated | Allowed (multiset) unless DISTINCT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** When asked *"Show that RA, TRC, and DRC are equivalent,"* demonstrate by **translating** one example query into all three forms and showing the identical output schema and tuples.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — The Relational Data Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Relational Data Model] --> B[Domain]
    A --> C[Attribute]
    A --> D[Schema]
    A --> E[Instance]
    A --> F[Constraints]
    A --> G[Operations]

    B --> B1[D1, D2, ... Dn]
    B --> B2[Atomic Value Pools]

    C --> C1[Simple vs Composite]
    C --> C2[Single-valued vs Multi-valued]
    C --> C3[Derived]
    C --> C4[Key vs Non-key]

    D --> D1[Relation Name R]
    D --> D2[Attributes A1..An]
    D --> D3[Integrity Rules]

    E --> E1[Set of Tuples]
    E --> E2[Time-varying]

    F --> F1[Inherent Model Constraints]
    F --> F2[Schema-Based Constraints]
    F --> F3[Application-Based Constraints]
    F2 --> F21[KEY]
    F2 --> F22[NOT NULL]
    F2 --> F23[UNIQUE]
    F2 --> F24[CHECK]
    F2 --> F25[FOREIGN KEY]
    F21 --> F211[Primary Key]
    F21 --> F212[Candidate Key]
    F21 --> F213[Super Key]
    F21 --> F214[Foreign Key]

    G --> G1[Retrieval Operations]
    G --> G2[Update Operations]
    G1 --> G11[Relational Algebra]
    G1 --> G12[Relational Calculus]
    G2 --> G21[Insert]
    G2 --> G22[Delete]
    G2 --> G23[Modify]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — SQL Query Execution Pipeline (Theory Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[User SQL Query] --> P2[Parser and Syntax Tree]
    P2 --> P3[Query Preprocessor]
    P3 --> P4[Relational Algebra Expression]
    P4 --> P5[Query Optimizer]
    P5 --> P6[Optimized RA Plan]
    P6 --> P7[Execution Engine]
    P7 --> P8[Result Tuples]
    P4 -.uses.-> P41[Selection, Projection, Join]
    P4 -.uses.-> P42[Set Operators]
    P4 -.uses.-> P43[Aggregate Operators]
    P5 -.applies.-> P51[Predicate Pushdown]
    P5 -.applies.-> P52[Join Reordering]
    P5 -.applies.-> P53[Index Selection]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — DDL Sub-language Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[DDL Command Stream] --> R{CREATE}
    Q --> S{ALTER}
    Q --> T{DROP}
    Q --> U{TRUNCATE}
    Q --> V{RENAME}

    R --> R1[CREATE TABLE]
    R --> R2[CREATE VIEW]
    R --> R3[CREATE INDEX]
    R --> R4[CREATE SCHEMA]
    R --> R5[CREATE CONSTRAINT]

    S --> S1[ADD COLUMN]
    S --> S2[DROP COLUMN]
    S --> S3[MODIFY COLUMN]
    S --> S4[ADD CONSTRAINT]
    S --> S5[DROP CONSTRAINT]

    T --> T1[DROP TABLE]
    T --> T2[DROP VIEW]
    T --> T3[DROP INDEX]
    T --> T4[DROP SCHEMA]
    T --> T5[CASCADE vs RESTRICT]

    U --> U1[TRUNCATE TABLE]
    U --> U2[DDL vs DML semantics]

    V --> V1[RENAME old TO new]
    V --> V2[ALTER TABLE RENAME]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Map — Constraints Enforcement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Constraint Engine] --> L[Compile-Time Check]
    K --> M[Run-Time Check]

    L --> L1[Static Type Checking]
    L --> L2[Domain Validation]
    L --> L3[Key Cardinality Analysis]

    M --> M1[Trigger Before Insert]
    M --> M2[Trigger After Update]
    M --> M3[Referential Cascade]

    M1 --> R1[Entity Integrity]
    M1 --> R2[Domain Integrity]
    M2 --> R3[Key Uniqueness]
    M3 --> R4[Referential Integrity]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** Define the following with an example each:
(i) Super Key
(ii) Candidate Key
(iii) Primary Key

**Model Answer:**

- **Super Key:** A set of one or more attributes that, taken collectively, **uniquely identifies a tuple** in a relation. *Example:* In `STUDENT(SID, SSN, NAME, DOB)`, both `{SID}` and `{SSN}` and `{SID, SSN}` are super keys.
- **Candidate Key:** A *minimal* super key — no proper subset of it is a super key. *Example:* `{SID}` and `{SSN}` are candidate keys (singletons), but `{SID, SSN}` is not minimal.
- **Primary Key:** The **candidate key chosen by the designer** to uniquely identify tuples; it is NOT NULL. *Example:* `SID` is selected as the primary key.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often equate "Primary Key" with "Key." A primary key is *one* chosen candidate key. All candidate keys satisfy uniqueness; only the primary key has the NOT NULL guarantee.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** State the **closure property** of relational algebra. Why is it important?

**Model Answer:**

**Closure Property:** The output of any relational algebra operation is itself a *relation*. Hence, the result of one operation can be used as the input to another.

$$ \forall O \in \{\sigma, \pi, \times, \cup, -, \rho, \bowtie, \div, \ldots\}: \;\; O(R_1, R_2, \ldots) \; \text{is a relation} $$

**Importance:**
1. It enables **nesting and composition** of operations into complex queries.
2. It provides a **modular, algebraic** query construction.
3. It allows the **query optimizer** to apply algebraic transformations (e.g., $\sigma_{p}(R \bowtie S) \equiv \sigma_{p}(R) \bowtie S$ under certain conditions) to find efficient execution plans.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the following schema:
- `EMPLOYEE(ENO, ENAME, SALARY, DNO)`
- `DEPARTMENT(DNO, DNAME, LOCATION)`
- `PROJECT(PNO, PNAME, BUDGET, DNO)`
- `WORKS_ON(ENO, PNO, HOURS)`

Write **Relational Algebra** expressions for the following queries:
1. Find the names of employees working on project 'P101'.
2. Find the names and salaries of all employees in the 'Sales' department.
3. Find the project numbers of projects worked on by *all* employees of department D05.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Entity Integrity** and **Referential Integrity** constraints with suitable examples. How are they enforced in SQL?

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Convert the following English query into **Tuple Relational Calculus (TRC)** and **Domain Relational Calculus (DRC)**:
> *"Find the names of employees who earn a salary greater than the average salary of their department."*

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat syntax, explain the various **DDL commands** in SQL. Write the SQL DDL statements to create the following tables with all relevant constraints:
- `STUDENT(REGNO PRIMARY KEY, SNAME NOT NULL, AGE CHECK > 17, DEPTNO FK → DEPARTMENT)`
- `DEPARTMENT(DEPTNO PRIMARY KEY, DNAME UNIQUE NOT NULL)`

---

### 5.3 COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A**

#### Part (a) — Relational Algebra Expressions

**Solution (1): Names of employees working on project 'P101'**

$$
\pi_{\text{ENAME}}\!\left(\text{EMPLOYEE} \bowtie \left( \sigma_{\text{PNO} = \text{'P101'}}(\text{WORKS\_ON}) \right)\right)
$$

**Step-by-step:**
- Step 1: $\sigma_{\text{PNO} = \text{'P101'}}(\text{WORKS\_ON})$ — Filter WORKS_ON to only those rows for project P101.
- Step 2: $\text{EMPLOYEE} \bowtie (\text{Step 1})$ — Natural join with EMPLOYEE on ENO.
- Step 3: $\pi_{\text{ENAME}}(\text{Step 2})$ — Project the names.

**Solution (2): Names and salaries of employees in 'Sales' department**

$$
\pi_{\text{ENAME, SALARY}}\!\left(\text{EMPLOYEE} \bowtie \left( \sigma_{\text{DNAME} = \text{'Sales'}}(\text{DEPARTMENT}) \right)\right)
$$

**Step-by-step:**
- Step 1: $\sigma_{\text{DNAME} = \text{'Sales'}}(\text{DEPARTMENT})$ — Filter DEPARTMENT.
- Step 2: $\text{EMPLOYEE} \bowtie (\text{Step 1})$ — Join on DNO.
- Step 3: $\pi_{\text{ENAME, SALARY}}(\text{Step 2})$ — Project the requested attributes.

**Solution (3): Project numbers of projects worked on by ALL employees of D05**

$$
\pi_{\text{PNO}}\!\left(\text{WORKS\_ON} \div \pi_{\text{ENO}}\!\left(\sigma_{\text{DNO} = \text{'D05'}}(\text{EMPLOYEE})\right)\right)
$$

**Step-by-step:**
- Step 1: $\sigma_{\text{DNO} = \text{'D05'}}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$ — Employees of D05.
- Step 2: $\pi_{\text{ENO}}(\text{Step 1})$ — Their ENO set.
- Step 3: $\text{WORKS\_ON} \div (\text{Step 2})$ — Division: projects paired with *every* such ENO.
- Step 4: $\pi_{\text{PNO}}(\text{Step 3})$ — Final projection.

#### Part (b) — Integrity Constraints

**1. Entity Integrity Constraint:**
- **Definition:** No attribute participating in the primary key of a relation is allowed to be NULL.
- **Reason:** A NULL primary key would mean we cannot uniquely identify the tuple, defeating the very purpose of the primary key.
- **Example:** In `STUDENT(REGNO, SNAME, AGE)`, `REGNO` is the primary key, so the DBMS rejects `INSERT` statements where `REGNO IS NULL`.

**2. Referential Integrity Constraint:**
- **Definition:** If a relation $R_2$ has a foreign key FK referencing relation $R_1$'s primary key PK, then every value of FK in $R_2$ must either (a) match an existing value of PK in $R_1$, or (b) be NULL (subject to constraint declaration).
- **Example:** In `ENROLL(SID, CID)` referencing `STUDENT(SID)` and `COURSE(CID)`, inserting `(99, 201)` will be rejected if there is no `STUDENT` with `SID=99`.

**Enforcement in SQL:**

```sql
-- Entity Integrity (implicit when PRIMARY KEY is declared)
CREATE TABLE STUDENT (
    REGNO   NUMBER(8)   PRIMARY KEY,    -- REGNO cannot be NULL
    SNAME   VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL
);

-- Referential Integrity (via FOREIGN KEY)
CREATE TABLE ENROLL (
    SID     NUMBER(8),
    CID     NUMBER(6),
    CONSTRAINT FK_SID FOREIGN KEY (SID) REFERENCES STUDENT(REGNO)
                              ON DELETE CASCADE
);
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Relational Algebra):** When asked for queries involving **"all"** or **"every"**, students often write join + selection instead of *division*. Using division is mandatory; otherwise, the query is logically incorrect. Marks lost: 2–3 per such query.

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B**

#### Part (a) — TRC and DRC Conversion

**English Query:** *"Find the names of employees who earn a salary greater than the average salary of their department."*

**Tuple Relational Calculus (TRC):**

$$
\{t.\text{ENAME} \mid t \in \text{EMPLOYEE} \;\land\; t.\text{SALARY} > \text{AVG}_{d.\text{DNO} = t.\text{DNO}}(\text{EMPLOYEE}.\text{SALARY})\}
$$

Or written with explicit quantifiers (more formal):

$$
\{t.\text{ENAME} \mid t \in \text{EMPLOYEE} \;\land\; \exists u \in \text{EMPLOYEE} \; (u.\text{DNO} = t.\text{DNO} \;\land\; t.\text{SALARY} > u.\text{SALARY}) \}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A *necessary condition* (not equivalent without universal quantifier on "all pairs in the dept"). A common simplified form uses an aggregate.

**Domain Relational Calculus (DRC):**

$$
\{ \langle n \rangle \mid \exists e, s, d \;(\langle e, n, s, d \rangle \in \text{EMPLOYEE} \;\land\; \exists e_2, s_2 \;(\langle e_2, n_2, s_2, d \rangle \in \text{EMPLOYEE} \;\land\; s > s_2)) \}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** For every domain tuple $\langle e, n, s, d \rangle$ representing (ENO, ENAME, SALARY, DNO), assert the existence of a "lower-salary colleague" in the same department, ensuring the employee's salary is above the minimum (a simpler sufficient approximation in pure calculus; the *true* average requires aggregation not directly in pure relational calculus).

#### Part (b) — DDL Commands and Table Creation

**Overview of DDL Commands:**

| Command | Purpose | Syntax |
|---------|---------|--------|
| `CREATE` | Creates database objects (TABLE, VIEW, INDEX, SCHEMA) | `CREATE OBJECT_TYPE object_name (...);` |
| `ALTER` | Modifies structure of an existing object | `ALTER TABLE table_name action;` |
| `DROP` | Permanently deletes an object | `DROP TABLE table_name [CASCADE CONSTRAINTS];` |
| `TRUNCATE` | Removes all rows (faster than DELETE; DDL in Oracle) | `TRUNCATE TABLE table_name;` |
| `RENAME` | Renames an object | `RENAME old_name TO new_name;` |

**SQL DDL Statements for the Given Tables:**

```sql
-- Department table first (parent)
CREATE TABLE DEPARTMENT (
    DEPTNO   NUMBER(4)      PRIMARY KEY,
    DNAME    VARCHAR2(30)   UNIQUE NOT NULL
);

-- Student table (child)
CREATE TABLE STUDENT (
    REGNO    NUMBER(8)      PRIMARY KEY,
    SNAME    VARCHAR2(50)   NOT NULL,
    AGE      NUMBER(3)      CHECK (AGE > 17),
    DEPTNO   NUMBER(4),
    CONSTRAINT FK_STUD_DEPT
        FOREIGN KEY (DEPTNO)
        REFERENCES DEPARTMENT(DEPTNO)
        ON DELETE SET NULL
);
```

**ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE Examples:**

```sql
-- ALTER: add a column
ALTER TABLE STUDENT ADD (EMAIL VARCHAR2(50));

-- ALTER: drop a column
ALTER TABLE STUDENT DROP COLUMN EMAIL;

-- ALTER: add a constraint
ALTER TABLE STUDENT ADD CONSTRAINT CHK_AGE_MAX CHECK (AGE < 60);

-- TRUNCATE: remove all rows, keep structure
TRUNCATE TABLE STUDENT;

-- DROP: remove table entirely
DROP TABLE STUDENT CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (DDL):** Two recurring mistakes:
> 1. Forgetting that `TRUNCATE` is **DDL** (auto-committed, no rollback in some DBMS) versus `DELETE` (DML, transactional). 
> 2. Declaring `CHECK (AGE > 17)` without a constraint name and trying to drop it later — the system gives it a cryptic default name. Always provide a meaningful `CONSTRAINT` name for testability and portability.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Relational Data Model** is founded on **set theory + first-order logic**; relations are *sets of tuples*, not multisets (in theory).
- [x] **Schema** = structure (fixed); **Instance** = data (changes over time).
- [x] **Key types** in increasing restrictiveness: **Super Key → Candidate Key → Primary Key**.
- [x] **Entity Integrity** = PK ≠ NULL; **Referential Integrity** = FK must match parent PK or be NULL.
- [x] **Domain Integrity** = attribute values belong to the declared domain.
- [x] **Relational Algebra** is **procedural** with the **closure property** (output of any op is a relation).
- [x] **Six core RA operators** to memorize: $\sigma$ (select), $\pi$ (project), $\times$ (product), $\cup$ (union), $-$ (difference), $\rho$ (rename).
- [x] **Division** is the algebraic equivalent of the **universal quantifier**; use it for *"all/every"* queries.
- [x] **Natural Join** = equijoin on common attributes + projection to remove duplicate columns.
- [x] **TRC** and **DRC** are *declarative*; TRC uses tuple variables, DRC uses domain variables.
- [x] **Codd's Theorem:** RA ≡ TRC ≡ DRC (in their safe/relationally complete forms).
- [x] **SQL categories:** DDL (schema), DML (data), DCL (permissions), TCL (transactions).
- [x] **Key DDL commands:** `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`, `RENAME`.
- [x] **Constraint types in SQL DDL:** `PRIMARY KEY`, `FOREIGN KEY`, `UNIQUE`, `NOT NULL`, `CHECK`, `DEFAULT`.
- [x] **Referential actions:** `ON DELETE CASCADE / SET NULL / RESTRICT / NO ACTION`; same for `ON UPDATE`.
- [x] **TRUNCATE vs DELETE:** TRUNCATE is DDL (no WHERE, no trigger firing, auto-commit); DELETE is DML (transactional, supports WHERE).
- [x] **SQL is not strictly based on relational algebra** — it is a *superset* that includes multiset semantics, ordering, and aggregate functions beyond pure RA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Manipulation Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCMT404 - DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Data Manipulation Language ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Data Manipulation Language (DML)** is a subset of SQL (Structured Query Language) specifically designed to **retrieve, insert, update, and delete data** stored in the tables of a relational database. DML statements operate on the *data* (rows/tuples) inside tables, NOT on the table structure itself. The four canonical DML operations form the **CRUD** acronym: - **C** – Create (INSERT) - **R** – Read (SELECT) - **U** – Update (UPDATE) - **D** – Delete (DELETE) In the KTU 2024 Scheme, DML is positioned as the practical interface between an application/user and the relational engine, sitting *above* DDL (Data Definition Language) and *below* DCL (Data Control Language) in the SQL hierarchy. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for Board Exams:** > - **DML** → manipulates **data** (rows). > - **DDL** → manipulates **schema** (tables, columns, constraints). > - A frequent board question: *"Is `TRUNCATE` a DML command?"* — Answer: **No**, `TRUNCATE` is a DDL command because it removes all rows by deallocating the data page; it is auto-committed and cannot be rolled back. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Magic Library Imagine a **library catalogue system**: | SQL Command | Real-World Analogy | Action on Data | |-------------|--------------------|----------------| | `SELECT` | **Search the catalogue** | Read/find books without touching them | | `INSERT` | **Register a new book** | Add a brand new entry to the shelves | | `UPDATE` | **Edit a book's record** | Correct the price or author of a book | | `DELETE` | **Remove a book entry** | Strike a book off the registry | > **Plain English Summary:** If DDL is the *architect* who builds the library's rooms and shelves, then **DML is the librarian** who actually places books on shelves, changes their labels, searches for them, or removes obsolete ones. The architect never touches the books; the librarian never builds rooms. --- ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terms in DML > [!NOTE] > **Tuple / Row / Record** — a single horizontal entry in a table. All three terms are synonymous in KTU board exams. > > **Attribute / Column / Field** — a single vertical entry; defines the property of the entity. > > **Cardinality** — total number of tuples (rows) currently present in a table. > > **Arity / Degree** — total number of attributes (columns) in a table. > > **Atomicity of a Transaction** — A DML operation (or set of operations) is treated as a single logical unit. Either **all** sub-operations succeed (COMMIT) or **none** take effect (ROLLBACK). This is the **A** in the ACID properties. --- ### 1.4 Types of DML KTU recognises two sub-classifications: 1. **Procedural DML** — User specifies **what** to retrieve AND **how** to obtain it (e.g., relational algebra expressions, PL/SQL cursors, `PROCEDURE` blocks). 2. **Non-Procedural DML** (Declarative) — User specifies **only what** data is required; the DBMS optimizer decides the retrieval path. SQL's `SELECT` is the textbook example. SQL is primarily a **non-procedural / declarative** language, although extensions like PL/SQL embed procedural logic. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four DML Statements — Logic Breakdown #### A. `INSERT` — Adding Rows **Why** we use it: To persist new records into an existing table. **How** it works: The DBMS validates the row against column constraints (NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK, FOREIGN KEY, PRIMARY KEY) before storage. Three syntactic forms: 1. Insert with all columns (column list may be omitted if all values are provided in correct order). 2. Insert with explicit column list (recommended — robust against schema changes). 3. Insert via sub-query (copying bulk rows from another table). #### B. `SELECT` — Retrieving Rows **Why**: To project a subset of attributes and/or a subset of tuples based on a predicate. **How**: The query undergoes a logical pipeline: > `FROM` (cartesian product) → `WHERE` (selection/filter) → `GROUP BY` (partitioning) → `HAVING` (group filter) → `SELECT` (projection) → `ORDER BY` (sort) → `LIMIT/OFFSET` (pagination). #### C. `UPDATE` — Modifying Rows **Why**: To correct or alter existing records without recreating the row. **How**: The `WHERE` clause decides which rows are affected. **Omitting the WHERE clause updates EVERY row** — a notorious board-exam pitfall. #### D. `DELETE` — Removing Rows **Why**: To remove obsolete or invalid records. **How**: Works row-by-row (logical delete). For full table emptying, `TRUNCATE` is faster but is DDL. --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Below is the consolidated DML cheat sheet. Memorise the operator precedences — they appear in nearly every KTU 5/10-mark question. | # | Construct | Syntax Pattern | Logical Order of Evaluation | Common Board Trap | |---|-----------|---------------|----------------------------|-------------------| | 1 | `INSERT` | `INSERT INTO T [(c1,c2,...)] VALUES (v1,v2,...);` | 1. Constraint check → 2. Insert → 3. Index update | Omitting mandatory `NOT NULL` columns | | 2 | `INSERT … SELECT` | `INSERT INTO T1 SELECT * FROM T2 WHERE cond;` | Sub-query executes first | Data-type mismatch between source and target | | 3 | `SELECT` skeleton | `SELECT [DISTINCT] cols FROM T [JOIN …] [WHERE …] [GROUP BY …] [HAVING …] [ORDER BY …];` | FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY | Putting an aggregate in `WHERE` instead of `HAVING` | | 4 | `WHERE` operators | `=, <>, <, >, <=, >=, BETWEEN, IN, LIKE, IS NULL, AND, OR, NOT` | `NOT` > `AND` > `OR` | `BETWEEN x AND y` is **inclusive** of both ends | | 5 | `LIKE` wildcards | `%` (any string, including empty), `_` (exactly one char) | Matched character-by-character | `LIKE 'a%b'` matches `ab`, `axxb`, `a123b` — but NOT `ba` | | 6 | `UPDATE` | `UPDATE T SET c1=v1[, c2=v2] [WHERE cond];` | 1. Row identification → 2. Value assignment → 3. Constraint check | Omitting `WHERE` updates **all** rows | | 7 | `DELETE` | `DELETE FROM T [WHERE cond];` | 1. Row identification → 2. Cascade checks → 3. Delete | Foreign-key violation in child tables | | 8 | `ORDER BY` | `ORDER BY col [ASC\|DESC] [, col2 …];` | Final logical step (after SELECT) | Default order is **ASC**; NULLs sort as smallest in ASC | | 9 | Aggregate functions | `COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX` | Applied to each group | `COUNT(*)` counts NULLs; `COUNT(col)` ignores NULLs | | 10 | `DISTINCT` | `SELECT DISTINCT col FROM T;` | Applied after projection | Removes duplicate rows entirely, not per-column | --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > DML is the **transactional heart** of nearly every modern software stack: > - **Banking**: Every `UPDATE Accounts SET balance = balance - 1000 WHERE …` is a DML transaction. > - **E-Commerce**: `INSERT INTO orders …` fires on every checkout. > - **IoT/Embedded Telemetry**: Sensor data is bulk-`INSERT`-ed into time-series tables. > - **Analytics**: `SELECT` with `GROUP BY` powers BI dashboards. > - **Healthcare (HIMS)**: `DELETE` is heavily guarded because of legal retention rules — hence the prevalence of *soft-deletes* (a status flag) instead of hard `DELETE`. --- ### 2.4 Operator Precedence — The "NOT-AND-OR" Ladder $$ \text{Precedence (Highest to Lowest): } \textbf{NOT} \;>\; \textbf{AND} \;>\; \textbf{OR} $$ This means the expression: $$ \texttt{A = 1 OR B = 2 AND NOT C = 3} $$ is evaluated by the engine as: $$ \texttt{A = 1 OR (B = 2 AND (NOT C = 3))} $$ **Board Exam Tip:** Whenever mixing AND/OR, **always parenthesize** to avoid losing marks. The KTU evaluator looks for explicit clarity. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — The Classic `Library` Schema Let us consider the canonical KTU example schema: $$ \text{BOOK}(\underline{\text{BID}},\ \text{Title},\ \text{Author},\ \text{Price},\ \text{Category}) $$ $$ \text{MEMBER}(\underline{\text{MID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{JoinDate}) $$ $$ \text{ISSUE}(\underline{\text{IID}},\ \text{BID}_{FK},\ \text{MID}_{FK},\ \text{IssueDate}) $$ Each underline denotes the **primary key**; subscript `FK` denotes **foreign key**. --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive DML Walkthrough #### Step 1 — `INSERT` a new book **Plain English Goal:** Add a book titled *"Database System Concepts"* by *Silberschatz*, priced **950**, in category **'CS'**, with `BID = 101`. **Step 1.1 — Identify mandatory columns.** We check the table definition. Assuming `BID` is `NOT NULL` and is the PK, it must be supplied. **Step 1.2 — Construct the SQL.** ```sql INSERT INTO BOOK (BID, Title, Author, Price, Category) VALUES (101, 'Database System Concepts', 'Silberschatz', 950, 'CS'); ``` **Step 1.3 — Validation chain executed by the engine:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{(i) Parse and resolve column names} \\ &\text{(ii) Type-check values — e.g., 950 must be numeric for Price} \\ &\text{(iii) Constraint enforcement:} \\ &\qquad \text{(a) NOT NULL} \Rightarrow \text{each supplied value must be non-null} \\ &\qquad \text{(b) UNIQUE / PRIMARY KEY} \Rightarrow \text{BID 101 must not exist} \\ &\qquad \text{(c) CHECK} \Rightarrow \text{Price > 0} \\ &\qquad \text{(d) FOREIGN KEY} \Rightarrow \text{book must reference a valid category} \\ &\text{(iv) Write to data page and update indexes} \\ &\text{(v) Log to transaction log for rollback capability} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1.4 — Bulk insert variant using a sub-query.** Suppose we have a `STAGING_BOOKS` table holding the same columns plus a `Discontinued = 'N'` flag, and we want to load only non-discontinued books: ```sql INSERT INTO BOOK (BID, Title, Author, Price, Category) SELECT BID, Title, Author, Price, Category FROM STAGING_BOOKS WHERE Discontinued = 'N'; ``` **Logical Evaluation Order of the Sub-Query:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{(a) FROM STAGING\_BOOKS} &&\rightarrow \text{read the entire staging table} \\ &\text{(b) WHERE Discontinued = 'N'} &&\rightarrow \text{filter rows} \\ &\text{(c) SELECT projection} &&\rightarrow \text{shape the result rows} \\ &\text{(d) INSERT INTO BOOK} &&\rightarrow \text{apply validation on every row} \end{aligned} $$ If **any** row in the bulk violates a constraint, the **entire** `INSERT … SELECT` aborts in default mode. To allow skipping offenders, use: ```sql INSERT IGNORE INTO BOOK (...) SELECT ...; -- or INSERT INTO BOOK (...) SELECT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE Price = VALUES(Price); ``` --- #### Step 2 — `SELECT` with filtering, ordering, and aggregation **Plain English Goal:** *"Find the titles and prices of CS-category books priced above 500, sorted by price descending, and also show the total count and average price of those books."* The query has two logical parts — a row-list and a summary. We will treat them separately to satisfy the KTU marking rubric. **Sub-step 2.1 — Detailed row list:** ```sql SELECT Title, Price FROM BOOK WHERE Category = 'CS' AND Price > 500 ORDER BY Price DESC; ``` **Evaluation pipeline:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Step A: } \text{FROM BOOK} &&\rightarrow \text{load all rows of BOOK} \\ &\textbf{Step B: } \text{WHERE Category = 'CS' AND Price > 500} &&\rightarrow \text{retain qualifying rows} \\ &\textbf{Step C: } \text{SELECT Title, Price} &&\rightarrow \text{project only these two columns} \\ &\textbf{Step D: } \text{ORDER BY Price DESC} &&\rightarrow \text{sort in descending order} \end{aligned} $$ **Sub-step 2.2 — Aggregate summary:** ```sql SELECT COUNT(*) AS BookCount, AVG(Price) AS AvgPrice FROM BOOK WHERE Category = 'CS' AND Price > 500; ``` Notice that `COUNT(*)` returns the **number of rows that passed the filter**, and `AVG(Price)` ignores NULL prices automatically. **Sub-step 2.3 — Combined via `GROUP BY` (a more advanced, commonly tested pattern):** ```sql SELECT Category, COUNT(*) AS Cnt, AVG(Price) AS AvgP FROM BOOK WHERE Price > 300 GROUP BY Category HAVING COUNT(*) > 2 ORDER BY AvgP DESC; ``` The full logical pipeline: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{1. FROM BOOK} \\ &\text{2. WHERE Price > 300} \\ &\text{3. GROUP BY Category} \\ &\text{4. HAVING COUNT(*) > 2} \\ &\text{5. SELECT Category, COUNT(*), AVG(Price)} \\ &\text{6. ORDER BY AvgP DESC} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why can't we use `WHERE COUNT(*) > 2`?** Because `WHERE` filters rows *before* aggregation, and `COUNT(*)` is **not yet defined** at that point. The `HAVING` clause is the only place where aggregate predicates are legal. This is a **guaranteed 2-mark question** in KTU exams. --- #### Step 3 — `UPDATE` with a calculated expression **Plain English Goal:** *"Raise the price of every CS book by 10%, but cap the new price at 1000."* ```sql UPDATE BOOK SET Price = LEAST(Price * 1.10, 1000) WHERE Category = 'CS'; ``` **Step-by-step reasoning:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{NewPrice} &= \text{Price} \times 1.10 \\ \text{FinalPrice} &= \min(\text{NewPrice},\ 1000) \end{aligned} $$ The `LEAST(a, b)` function returns the smaller of the two values. It is SQL-standard; some dialects use `IIF` or `CASE`. Equivalent, more portable form using `CASE`: ```sql UPDATE BOOK SET Price = CASE WHEN Price * 1.10 > 1000 THEN 1000 ELSE Price * 1.10 END WHERE Category = 'CS'; ``` --- #### Step 4 — `DELETE` with a join-aware pattern **Plain English Goal:** *"Delete every book whose category is 'Old' and which has never been issued."* ```sql DELETE FROM BOOK WHERE Category = 'Old' AND BID NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT BID FROM ISSUE); ``` **Evaluation:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{(i) The sub-query produces a set of BIDs that appear in ISSUE.} \\ &\text{(ii) The outer query keeps BOOK rows where Category = 'Old' AND BID is NOT in that set.} \\ &\text{(iii) FK constraint check: ISSUE.BID still references rows being deleted? } \\ &\quad \text{If yes, the engine raises a foreign-key violation error.} \end{aligned} $$ If the schema declares `ON DELETE CASCADE` on `ISSUE.BID`, the referencing rows in `ISSUE` are removed automatically. Otherwise the `DELETE` is rejected. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DML vs DDL vs DCL vs TCL | Feature | DML | DDL | DCL | TCL | |---------|-----|-----|-----|-----| | Purpose | Manipulate data | Define schema | Control permissions | Manage transactions | | Commands | `SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE` | `CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE` | `GRANT, REVOKE` | `COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT` | | Auto-commit? | No (default) | Yes | Yes | N/A | | Rollback? | Yes (until commit) | No | No | Yes (its job) | | Affects rows? | Yes | No (rows affected indirectly via TRUNCATE) | No | No | | KTU weightage | **Highest** in Module 2 | High | Low | Medium | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Logical Pipeline of a SQL `SELECT` (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Start: User issues SELECT] --> nodeB[FROM clause
Identify source tables] nodeB --> nodeC[JOIN if multiple tables
Cartesian product + ON predicate] nodeC --> nodeD[WHERE clause
Row-wise filter on tuples] nodeD --> nodeE[GROUP BY clause
Partition rows into groups] nodeE --> nodeF[HAVING clause
Filter on aggregate values] nodeF --> nodeG[SELECT clause
Project columns and compute expressions] nodeG --> nodeH[DISTINCT keyword
Eliminate duplicate result rows] nodeH --> nodeI[ORDER BY clause
Sort result set ASC or DESC] nodeI --> nodeJ[LIMIT and OFFSET
Paginate output] nodeJ --> nodeK[Final Result Set
Returned to user] ``` ### 4.2 CRUD Operation Map (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph UserSpace [User Application Layer] nodeUA1[Form Submission] nodeUA2[Search Bar] nodeUA3[Edit Record] nodeUA4[Delete Request] end subgraph SQLEngine [SQL DML Engine] nodeSE1[INSERT] nodeSE2[SELECT] nodeSE3[UPDATE] nodeSE4[DELETE] end subgraph DBStore [Persistent Storage] nodeDB1[Row added] nodeDB2[Rows read] nodeDB3[Row modified] nodeDB4[Row removed] end nodeUA1 --> nodeSE1 --> nodeDB1 nodeUA2 --> nodeSE2 --> nodeDB2 nodeUA3 --> nodeSE3 --> nodeDB3 nodeUA4 --> nodeSE4 --> nodeDB4 ``` ### 4.3 Transaction Lifecycle Around DML (Mermaid) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Active: BEGIN or implicit start Active --> Dirty: INSERT or UPDATE or DELETE Dirty --> Active: SELECT or ROLLBACK Dirty --> Persisted: COMMIT Persisted --> Idle Active --> Aborted: ROLLBACK to SAVEPOINT Aborted --> Active: ROLLBACK or new statement Persisted --> [*] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** A DML statement can only leave the *Active* state via `COMMIT` (data permanently persisted) or `ROLLBACK` (data discarded). Until one of these happens, the modifications are visible only inside the current transaction — a property called **isolation**. ### 4.4 Decision Tree: When to Use `WHERE`, `HAVING`, or Both (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeQ1{Predicate involves
an aggregate function?} nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeH[HAVING clause] nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeQ2{Predicate filters
individual rows
before grouping?} nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeW[WHERE clause] nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeQ3{Do you need
both row filter
and group filter?} nodeQ3 -- Yes --> nodeB[Use BOTH
WHERE then HAVING] nodeQ3 -- No --> nodeA[Pick one] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > > **Q1.** Differentiate between DDL and DML with suitable examples. > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Aspect | DDL (Data Definition Language) | DML (Data Manipulation Language) | > |--------|--------------------------------|----------------------------------| > | Purpose | Defines or alters the **schema** of database objects (tables, views, indexes). | Manipulates the **data** stored inside those objects. | > | Operations | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE` | `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` | > | Effect | Structural change to the database itself. | Affects rows (tuples) only. | > | Auto-commit | Yes — DDL statements are auto-committed. | No — DML changes are committed only on `COMMIT`. | > | Example | `CREATE TABLE Student (Roll INT PRIMARY KEY, Name VARCHAR(50));` | `INSERT INTO Student VALUES (1, 'Anu');` | > > **Examiner's note (1 mark):** State clearly that DML operates on **rows** whereas DDL operates on the **table definition**, and that DDL changes cannot be rolled back whereas DML changes can. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > > **Q2.** Explain the difference between `DELETE`, `TRUNCATE`, and `DROP` statements. > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > 1. **`DELETE`** — A DML command. Removes **specific rows** identified by the `WHERE` clause. Can be rolled back. Fires `DELETE` triggers. Slower for large tables. > ```sql > DELETE FROM Student WHERE Roll = 5; > ``` > > 2. **`TRUNCATE`** — A DDL command. Removes **all rows** of a table by deallocating the data pages. **Cannot be rolled back**. Resets identity/auto-increment counters. Faster than `DELETE`. > ```sql > TRUNCATE TABLE Student; > ``` > > 3. **`DROP`** — A DDL command. Removes the **entire table** including its structure, data, indexes, and constraints. **Cannot be rolled back**. > ```sql > DROP TABLE Student; > ``` > > **One-line mnemonic for board exams:** **D**elete = **D**ata only, **T**runcate = **T**able emptied, **D**rop = **D**emolished entirely. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** > > ### Question A (14 Marks) > > Consider the following schema for a college library system. Write SQL DML statements for the given sub-questions. > > $$ > \text{BOOK}(\underline{\text{BID}},\ \text{Title},\ \text{Author},\ \text{Price},\ \text{Category}) > $$ > > $$ > \text{MEMBER}(\underline{\text{MID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{JoinDate},\ \text{MembershipType}) > $$ > > $$ > \text{ISSUE}(\underline{\text{IID}},\ \text{BID}_{FK},\ \text{MID}_{FK},\ \text{IssueDate},\ \text{ReturnDate}) > $$ > > **a)** *(7 marks)* Write SQL queries to: > (i) Insert a new book `BID = 201, Title = 'Operating Systems', Author = 'Galvin', Price = 750, Category = 'CS'`. *(2 marks)* > (ii) Display the titles and prices of all books in the `'CS'` category, sorted by price in descending order. *(2 marks)* > (iii) Find the number of books issued per category. *(3 marks)* > > **b)** *(7 marks)* Write SQL queries to: > (i) Increase the price of all books in the `'CS'` category by 15%. *(2 marks)* > (ii) Delete all members whose `MembershipType = 'Expired'` and who have not issued any book in the last 1 year. *(3 marks)* > (iii) Display the names of members who have issued more than 3 books, along with the total count. *(2 marks)* > > --- > > **Model Solution A:** > > **a) (i) — INSERT a new book (2 marks):** > ```sql > INSERT INTO BOOK (BID, Title, Author, Price, Category) > VALUES (201, 'Operating Systems', 'Galvin', 750, 'CS'); > ``` > *Marking tip:* Award 1 mark for correct syntax, 1 mark for correct value-to-column mapping. If the student omits the column list but the value order is right, give 1 mark only. > > **a) (ii) — Sorted SELECT (2 marks):** > ```sql > SELECT Title, Price > FROM BOOK > WHERE Category = 'CS' > ORDER BY Price DESC; > ``` > *Marking tip:* Common error — students write `ORDER BY Price` without `DESC`. Give 1 mark only. > > **a) (iii) — Books per category with aggregation (3 marks):** > ```sql > SELECT Category, COUNT(*) AS TotalBooks > FROM BOOK > GROUP BY Category; > ``` > *Marking tip:* Many students forget `GROUP BY`. Without it, the query is invalid because `Category` is in the `SELECT` and is non-aggregated. Deduct 1 mark if `GROUP BY` is missing. > > **b) (i) — Bulk price increase (2 marks):** > ```sql > UPDATE BOOK > SET Price = Price * 1.15 > WHERE Category = 'CS'; > ``` > *Marking tip:* Award 1 mark for `UPDATE … SET` pattern, 1 mark for the correct `WHERE` filter. Omitting `WHERE` updates all books — deduct 1 mark. > > **b) (ii) — Conditional DELETE (3 marks):** > ```sql > DELETE FROM MEMBER > WHERE MembershipType = 'Expired' > AND MID NOT IN ( > SELECT MID > FROM ISSUE > WHERE IssueDate >= DATE_SUB(CURDATE(), INTERVAL 1 YEAR) > ); > ``` > *Alternative using standard SQL:* > ```sql > DELETE FROM MEMBER > WHERE MembershipType = 'Expired' > AND MID NOT IN ( > SELECT MID FROM ISSUE > WHERE IssueDate >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1' YEAR > ); > ``` > *Marking tip:* Sub-query correctness carries 2 marks; outer `WHERE` logic carries 1 mark. Board exam solutions must show the sub-query explicitly. > > **b) (iii) — Members with more than 3 issues (2 marks):** > ```sql > SELECT M.Name, COUNT(*) AS IssueCount > FROM MEMBER M > JOIN ISSUE I ON M.MID = I.MID > GROUP BY M.MID, M.Name > HAVING COUNT(*) > 3; > ``` > *Marking tip:* A common mistake is to put `COUNT(*) > 3` in `WHERE`. This is logically wrong because `WHERE` executes before grouping. Deduct full 2 marks if this error appears. > > --- > > ### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks) > > Consider the same library schema. Write SQL DML statements for the following: > > **a)** *(7 marks)* > (i) Insert three new members in a single `INSERT` statement with values `(1, 'Anu', '2024-01-15', 'Gold'), (2, 'Ravi', '2024-02-20', 'Silver'), (3, 'Meera', '2024-03-05', 'Gold')`. *(2 marks)* > (ii) Display the average price of books in each category, but only for categories with more than 2 books, sorted by average price descending. *(3 marks)* > (iii) List the names of authors whose books have an average price greater than 600. *(2 marks)* > > **b)** *(7 marks)* > (i) Update the `ReturnDate` of all issues where the book belongs to the `'Reference'` category to `NULL` (i.e., mark them as not yet returned). *(2 marks)* > (ii) Delete from `ISSUE` all records where the issue happened before `'2023-01-01'`. *(2 marks)* > (iii) Display the top 3 most issued books (by `BID` and count). *(3 marks)* > > --- > > **Model Solution B:** > > **a) (i) — Multi-row INSERT (2 marks):** > ```sql > INSERT INTO MEMBER (MID, Name, JoinDate, MembershipType) > VALUES > (1, 'Anu', '2024-01-15', 'Gold'), > (2, 'Ravi', '2024-02-20', 'Silver'), > (3, 'Meera', '2024-03-05', 'Gold'); > ``` > *Marking tip:* Comma-separated `VALUES` lists are accepted in standard SQL. Award 1 mark for syntax, 1 mark for correct data. > > **a) (ii) — Conditional aggregation (3 marks):** > ```sql > SELECT Category, AVG(Price) AS AvgPrice > FROM BOOK > GROUP BY Category > HAVING COUNT(*) > 2 > ORDER BY AvgPrice DESC; > ``` > *Marking tip:* Order of clauses is critical. The correct order is `WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → ORDER BY`. Mixing them up loses 1 mark. > > **a) (iii) — Grouped filter (2 marks):** > ```sql > SELECT Author > FROM BOOK > GROUP BY Author > HAVING AVG(Price) > 600; > ``` > *Marking tip:* 1 mark for `GROUP BY Author`, 1 mark for `HAVING AVG(Price) > 600`. > > **b) (i) — Conditional UPDATE (2 marks):** > ```sql > UPDATE ISSUE I > JOIN BOOK B ON I.BID = B.BID > SET I.ReturnDate = NULL > WHERE B.Category = 'Reference'; > ``` > *MySQL-specific syntax shown; for standard SQL use:* > ```sql > UPDATE ISSUE > SET ReturnDate = NULL > WHERE BID IN (SELECT BID FROM BOOK WHERE Category = 'Reference'); > ``` > > **b) (ii) — Historical DELETE (2 marks):** > ```sql > DELETE FROM ISSUE > WHERE IssueDate < '2023-01-01'; > ``` > *Marking tip:* Watch the date format. `DATE` literal must be a quoted string in standard SQL. > > **b) (iii) — Top-N query (3 marks):** > ```sql > SELECT BID, COUNT(*) AS IssueCount > FROM ISSUE > GROUP BY BID > ORDER BY IssueCount DESC > LIMIT 3; > ``` > *Standard SQL alternative using window functions:* > ```sql > SELECT BID, IssueCount > FROM ( > SELECT BID, COUNT(*) AS IssueCount, > DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) AS rnk > FROM ISSUE > GROUP BY BID > ) AS ranked > WHERE rnk <= 3; > ``` > *Marking tip:* `LIMIT` is widely accepted. Award full marks for either pattern. Deduct 1 mark if `ORDER BY` is missing. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 ways KTU students lose marks in DML questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the `WHERE` clause in `UPDATE` / `DELETE`** — silently modifies the entire table. The examiner will deduct 1–2 marks *and* the query is logically wrong. > > 2. **Putting an aggregate inside `WHERE`** — `WHERE SUM(Price) > 1000` is a **syntax error** in standard SQL. Always use `HAVING` for aggregate predicates. > > 3. **Confusing `COUNT(*)` with `COUNT(column)`** — `COUNT(*)` counts rows including those with NULLs in the column. `COUNT(col)` ignores NULLs. Worth 1 mark in board exams. > > 4. **Forgetting `GROUP BY` when mixing aggregates with non-aggregates in `SELECT`** — strictly invalid in standard SQL and in most KTU-graded dialects. > > 5. **Mis-ordering SQL clauses** — `SELECT … FROM … WHERE … GROUP BY … HAVING … ORDER BY` is the canonical order. Reversing `WHERE` and `HAVING` is a guaranteed mark loss. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — DML (Module 2, PBCMT404)** - **DML = Data Manipulation Language.** Four commands: `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` — collectively CRUD. - **DML affects rows; DDL affects structure.** This is the single most important distinction in board exams. - **`TRUNCATE` and `DROP` are DDL, not DML.** `TRUNCATE` empties all rows; `DROP` deletes the table itself. - **Operator precedence in `WHERE`:** `NOT` > `AND` > `OR`. Always parenthesize for clarity. - **`LIKE` wildcards:** `%` = any string, `_` = exactly one character. - **`BETWEEN x AND y` is inclusive** of both `x` and `y`. - **Aggregate functions** (`COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX`) **cannot appear in `WHERE`**. They belong in `HAVING` or in the `SELECT` list. - **`GROUP BY` clause** is mandatory whenever `SELECT` mixes aggregated and non-aggregated columns. - **Logical evaluation order of `SELECT`:** `FROM` → `WHERE` → `GROUP BY` → `HAVING` → `SELECT` → `ORDER BY` → `LIMIT`. - **`DISTINCT`** is applied **after** projection, on the entire row, not on individual columns. - **`ORDER BY` default is `ASC`**. `NULL` values sort smallest in `ASC`, largest in `DESC` (engine-dependent). - **DML statements are transactional by default** — they can be `COMMIT`-ted or `ROLLBACK`-ed. DDL statements auto-commit. - **Bulk INSERT** via `INSERT INTO … SELECT … FROM …` is the most efficient way to copy rows between tables. - **Foreign-key awareness** in `UPDATE`/`DELETE` — if child rows reference a parent row being modified/removed, the engine may reject the operation unless `ON DELETE CASCADE` / `ON UPDATE CASCADE` is defined. - **Soft-delete pattern**: rather than `DELETE`, many production systems use an `is_active BOOLEAN` flag to preserve audit trails. - **`LIKE` is case-insensitive in MySQL** (default collation) but **case-sensitive in PostgreSQL/Oracle** — flag matters for KTU practical exams. - **Two NULLs are not equal**: `NULL = NULL` evaluates to `NULL` (i.e., unknown), not `TRUE`. Use `IS NULL` or `IS NOT NULL`. - **`EXPLAIN` / `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`** is a diagnostic tool (not a DML command) that shows the query execution plan — useful for performance questions.

topics.title: Assertions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT404 - DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assertions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Relational Model** and **SQL DDL (Data Definition Language)**, an **Assertion** is a declarative integrity constraint that is specified using the `CREATE ASSERTION` statement in SQL. Unlike a constraint attached to a single table (such as a `PRIMARY KEY`, `FOREIGN KEY`, or a table-level `CHECK` clause), an assertion is a **schema-level (catalog-level) predicate** that the DBMS is mandated to keep true at *all times* — across one or more tables simultaneously.

According to the SQL standard (ISO/IEC 9075), an assertion expresses a condition that the database state must always satisfy. If any transaction causes the assertion's predicate to evaluate to `FALSE`, the transaction is rejected (rolled back) and the database is left in its previous valid state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Assertions are part of **Module 2: Integrity Constraints and Relational Algebra** in the KTU 2024 Scheme. They fall under the SQL constraint mechanism family along with `NOT NULL`, `UNIQUE`, `PRIMARY KEY`, `FOREIGN KEY`, and `CHECK`. Assertions are the most general of these — they are *table-independent* and can reference any number of tables.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Rules" of a Database

Imagine a large office building with many rooms (tables) and people moving things between rooms (transactions). The building manager wants to enforce a **House Rule**:

> *"At no point in time may the total number of people inside Room A and Room B combined exceed 100."*

This rule:
- Is **not** tied to just one room (it spans multiple tables).
- Must hold **all the time**, whether people are entering, leaving, or rearranging themselves.
- Is **automatically checked** by the building's access system — if a movement would break the rule, the doors are locked and the action is refused.

In this analogy:
- **Rooms** = SQL tables
- **People moving** = `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` operations
- **The House Rule** = an SQL **Assertion**
- **The automatic door lock** = the DBMS's constraint enforcement engine

### 1.3 Where Assertions Fit in the SQL Constraint Hierarchy

| Level | Scope | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Column** | Single column, single row | `NOT NULL`, `UNIQUE` |
| **Table** | All rows of one table | `PRIMARY KEY`, `FOREIGN KEY`, `CHECK` |
| **Domain** | All columns of a given data type | `CREATE DOMAIN` |
| **Assertion** | Multiple tables, entire database | `CREATE ASSERTION` |

> [!NOTE]
> A `CHECK` clause is a **table-bound** constraint — it cannot reference other tables. The moment your constraint needs to look at *another* table to make a decision, you graduate from `CHECK` to `ASSERTION`.

### 1.4 Why Assertions Exist — The "Why"

Most real-world business rules are **multi-table** in nature:
- *"The salary of any manager must be greater than the salary of any employee in that manager's department."*
- *"The total number of registered students in any course must not exceed the course's room capacity."*
- *"Every project must be assigned to at least one department."*

These cannot be expressed using a single-table `CHECK`. They require SQL's most powerful declarative integrity mechanism: the **Assertion**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the `CREATE ASSERTION` Statement

The standard SQL syntax is:

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION <assertion_name>
    CHECK <search_condition>;
```

Where:
- **`<assertion_name>`** — A unique identifier within the schema. Naming convention: usually suffixed with `_A` or `_CK` to distinguish it from table constraints.
- **`<search_condition>`** — Any valid boolean SQL expression. It is the **predicate** that the DBMS must keep `TRUE` for all time. If it evaluates to `FALSE` for any database state, the modifying transaction is aborted.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — How the DBMS Handles an Assertion

The enforcement of an assertion can be broken down into the following steps:

1. **Parsing & Catalog Registration** — When the `CREATE ASSERTION` statement is executed, the DBMS parses the predicate, validates it against the current schema, and stores it in the system catalog under a globally unique name.
2. **Constraint Activation** — From this moment onward, the assertion is treated as an *active integrity constraint*. The DBMS internally registers it with its constraint manager.
3. **Lazy / Deferred Checking on Modification** — Whenever a transaction performs an `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE` that touches *any* table referenced (directly or indirectly) by the assertion, the DBMS re-evaluates the predicate.
4. **Validation Gate**:
   - If the predicate is `TRUE` → the modification is allowed to commit.
   - If the predicate is `FALSE` → the entire transaction is rolled back; an error (`SQLSTATE 23000 — Integrity Constraint Violation`) is raised.
   - If the predicate is `UNKNOWN` (due to `NULL`s) → the constraint is considered **satisfied** (because SQL uses three-valued logic, and `NOT FALSE` allows `UNKNOWN`).
5. **Catalog Update** — The assertion remains in the catalog until explicitly dropped via `DROP ASSERTION <assertion_name>;`.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Aspect | Detail / Rule |
|---|---|
| **Statement keyword** | `CREATE ASSERTION` |
| **Mandatory clause** | `CHECK <predicate>` |
| **Predicate scope** | Can reference any number of tables, views, or subqueries |
| **Drop syntax** | `DROP ASSERTION <name>` |
| **Trigger timing** | `AFTER` (default, post-modification validation) |
| **Deferability** | May be `DEFERRABLE` / `NOT DEFERRABLE` (per SQL standard) |
| **Three-valued logic** | `FALSE` → reject ; `TRUE` or `UNKNOWN` → accept |
| **Error class on violation** | `SQLSTATE 23000` (Integrity Constraint Violation) |
| **Cardinality limit** | One assertion per schema name (uniquely named) |
| **Comparison with `CHECK`** | `CHECK` = intra-table only ; Assertion = inter-table |
| **Comparison with Trigger** | Trigger = procedural & event-driven ; Assertion = declarative & continuous |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse **`CHECK` constraint** with **`ASSERTION`**. In the KTU valuation key, full marks for an "Assertion" question require explicitly mentioning that the predicate is **schema-level** and **multi-table**. Writing `ALTER TABLE … ADD CHECK …` when an inter-table rule is needed = guaranteed 0 marks for the constraint part.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Component

- **Why a `CHECK` clause inside the assertion?** Because SQL re-uses its most expressive boolean expression syntax for predicates. A `CHECK` clause, when promoted to schema level, becomes a universal constraint language.
- **Why does the predicate allow subqueries?** Because business rules often need to *aggregate* (e.g., `SUM`, `COUNT`) across rows or compare rows *between* tables. Subqueries are the natural way to express these.
- **Why can the assertion be dropped independently?** Because it is a *first-class schema object* — it does not belong to any one table, so it must be manageable as a separate entity.
- **Why is `UNKNOWN` treated as valid?** Because SQL's three-valued logic ensures that a constraint is only violated when its negation is demonstrably `TRUE` (i.e., when the original predicate is concretely `FALSE`). A row of `NULL`s cannot "break" an assertion.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In enterprise systems (banking, ERP, hospital information systems), assertions model the **invariants** of the business domain:
- In a **banking schema**: *"The total balance of all loan accounts of a customer must not exceed 3× their monthly income."*
- In a **university schema**: *"No faculty member can supervise more than 5 M.Tech students concurrently."*
- In a **manufacturing schema**: *"Sum of all components issued to the assembly line must never exceed the warehouse stock."*

These are not "row rules" — they are **organizational laws** that span the entire database, and SQL `ASSERTION` is the only declarative mechanism that captures them at the schema level.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> In this section, we move from textual concepts to **exhaustive worked examples**. Each example is a classic KTU textbook pattern, fully solved.

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Assertion vs. Other Constraint Types

| Constraint Type | Scope | Cross-Table? | Procedural? | Active By Default? | Standard SQL Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `NOT NULL` | Column | No | No | Yes | Implicit in column def |
| `UNIQUE` | Column / Table | No | No | Yes | `UNIQUE` |
| `PRIMARY KEY` | Table | No | No | Yes | `PRIMARY KEY` |
| `FOREIGN KEY` | Inter-table | Yes (referential) | No | Yes | `FOREIGN KEY ... REFERENCES` |
| `CHECK` | Table | **No** | No | Yes | `CHECK (...)` |
| **Assertion** | **Schema** | **Yes** | No | Yes | `CREATE ASSERTION ... CHECK` |
| Trigger | Schema | Yes | **Yes (PL/SQL code)** | Yes | `CREATE TRIGGER` |

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — The "Sum-Constraint" Pattern

**Schema Context (University DB):**
- `STUDENT(SID, SNAME, DEPT_ID, CREDITS_EARNED)`
- `DEPARTMENT(DEPT_ID, DEPT_NAME, MAX_CAPACITY)`

**Business Rule:** *"The total number of students in any department must never exceed that department's `MAX_CAPACITY`."*

**Why `CHECK` fails:** A `CHECK` on `STUDENT` cannot look at the `MAX_CAPACITY` of the corresponding `DEPARTMENT` row.

**Assertion Construction — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Identify the predicate's subject.**
The rule is: $\forall d \in \text{DEPARTMENT}, \quad \text{COUNT}(s \in \text{STUDENT} : s.\text{DEPT\_ID} = d.\text{DEPT\_ID}) \leq d.\text{MAX\_CAPACITY}$.

**Step 2 — Express the negative using a subquery.**
SQL assertions are most naturally written by negating the violation:

> "There must not exist a department where the count of its students exceeds its capacity."

Mathematically:
$$
\neg \exists \, d \in \text{DEPARTMENT} \;\Big( \text{COUNT}_{\text{STUDENT}}(s.\text{DEPT\_ID} = d.\text{DEPT\_ID}) \;>\; d.\text{MAX\_CAPACITY} \Big)
$$
