**Step 3 — Translate to SQL.**

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION DEPT_CAPACITY_RULE
    CHECK (
        NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT d.DEPT_ID
            FROM DEPARTMENT d
            WHERE (
                SELECT COUNT(*)
                FROM STUDENT s
                WHERE s.DEPT_ID = d.DEPT_ID
            ) > d.MAX_CAPACITY
        )
    );
```

**Step 4 — Verification (the DBMS's mental check).**
For every row in `DEPARTMENT`, the inner correlated subquery counts the matching students. The outer `WHERE` filters only the violating departments. The `NOT EXISTS` then asserts that the set of violating departments is **empty**. If any transaction causes a new `STUDENT` insert that pushes a department over capacity, the inner subquery returns a non-empty set, the `NOT EXISTS` evaluates to `FALSE`, and the assertion fails — the transaction is aborted.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — The "Forall Comparison" Pattern

**Business Rule:** *"Every professor's salary must be strictly greater than the salary of every lecturer in the same department."*

**Mathematical Predicate:**
$$
\forall p \in \text{PROF}, \, \forall l \in \text{LECT} \;:\; (p.\text{DEPT} = l.\text{DEPT}) \;\Rightarrow\; (p.\text{SALARY} > l.\text{SALARY})
$$

**Negated Form (to plug into `NOT EXISTS`):**
$$
\neg \exists \, p, l \;:\; (p.\text{DEPT} = l.\text{DEPT}) \;\land\; (p.\text{SALARY} \leq l.\text{SALARY})
$$

**SQL Translation — Step by Step:**

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION PROF_SALARY_DOMINANCE
    CHECK (
        NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT *
            FROM PROFESSOR p, LECTURER l
            WHERE p.DEPT = l.DEPT
              AND p.SALARY <= l.SALARY
        )
    );
```

**Validation Walk-through:**
- Inner join on `DEPT` pairs each professor with same-department lecturers.
- The `<=` filter selects only pairs where the professor does **not** earn strictly more.
- `NOT EXISTS` ensures **no such pair** exists. If an `UPDATE PROFESSOR SET SALARY = SALARY - 1000` would create such a pair, the assertion fails and the update is rejected.

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — The "Aggregate Over Join" Pattern

**Business Rule:** *"For every course, the sum of credits of all enrolled students' prior completed courses must not exceed 240."*

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION COURSE_CREDIT_LIMIT
    CHECK (
        NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT c.COURSE_ID
            FROM COURSE c
            JOIN ENROLLMENT e ON c.COURSE_ID = e.COURSE_ID
            JOIN STUDENT s ON e.SID = s.SID
            GROUP BY c.COURSE_ID
            HAVING SUM(s.CREDITS_EARNED) > 240
        )
    );
```

**Step-by-step explanation of the logic:**

1. `JOIN` connects the three relevant tables: course, enrollment, student.
2. `GROUP BY c.COURSE_ID` partitions the joined rows by course.
3. `SUM(s.CREDITS_EARNED)` aggregates, per course, the credits of enrolled students.
4. `HAVING SUM(...) > 240` keeps only the *violating* courses.
5. `NOT EXISTS` wraps it: "There must not be any course whose aggregate exceeds 240."

### 3.5 Dropping and Modifying Assertions

Because assertions are first-class schema objects, they cannot be altered in place. The standard idiom is:

```sql
DROP ASSERTION PROF_SALARY_DOMINANCE;
-- (then re-create with the new predicate)
CREATE ASSERTION PROF_SALARY_DOMINANCE
    CHECK ( ... new predicate ... );
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The lack of an `ALTER ASSERTION` statement (in the SQL standard) is a deliberate design choice. Assertions are treated as atomic, version-controlled schema artifacts — drop-and-recreate is the canonical workflow.

### 3.6 The "Deferred vs. Immediate" Subtlety

The SQL standard allows assertions to be declared:

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION MY_RULE
    CHECK ( ... )
    [ INITIALLY DEFERRED | INITIALLY IMMEDIATE ]
    [ [ NOT ] DEFERRABLE ];
```

- **`IMMEDIATE`** (default) — The predicate is checked at the end of **every** statement.
- **`DEFERRED`** — The predicate is checked only at transaction commit time. This is useful when a multi-statement transaction temporarily violates a constraint but the final committed state is valid.

This is the same deferability mechanism that applies to `FOREIGN KEY` constraints, and it is a frequent **KTU 2-mark question** in the integrity constraints module.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Lifecycle of an SQL Assertion (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer issues CREATE ASSERTION stmt] --> B[DBMS Parser]
    B --> C{Valid SQL predicate?}
    C -- No --> D[Return Syntax Error]
    C -- Yes --> E[Register in System Catalog as schema object]
    E --> F[Assertion is now ACTIVE]
    F --> G[Transaction begins: INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE]
    G --> H[DBMS identifies tables touched by the assertion]
    H --> I[Re-evaluate CHECK predicate on new state]
    I --> J{Predicate result?}
    J -- TRUE --> K[Modification permitted]
    J -- UNKNOWN --> K
    J -- FALSE --> L[Rollback transaction]
    L --> M[Raise SQLSTATE 23000 Integrity Violation]
    K --> N[Transaction may continue or commit]
    M --> O[Database remains in previous valid state]
```

### 4.2 Assertion vs. CHECK vs. Trigger — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Need an integrity rule?] --> Q2{Does it reference only one table?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is it a simple value condition e.g. SALARY > 0?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R1[Use CHECK clause]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4[Use CHECK with subquery on same table]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5{Does it require procedural logic e.g. auditing?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> R2[Use TRIGGER]
    Q5 -- No --> R3[Use CREATE ASSERTION]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How an Assertion Sits in the DBMS Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UserLayer["User / Application Layer"]
        U1[SQL DML: INSERT UPDATE DELETE]
    end

    subgraph EngineLayer["DBMS Engine"]
        P[SQL Parser]
        O[Query Optimizer]
        EX[Execution Engine]
        CI[Constraint Manager]
        AC[Assertion Catalog]
    end

    subgraph StorageLayer["Storage Layer"]
        TT[Tables: STUDENT, DEPT, ...]
    end

    U1 --> P
    P --> O
    O --> EX
    EX --> CI
    CI <--> AC
    EX <--> TT
    CI -- re-evaluates predicate --> EX
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Map — Integrity Constraints in SQL (Hierarchical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SQL[SQL Integrity Constraints] --> A[Column-Level]
    SQL --> B[Table-Level]
    SQL --> C[Schema-Level]

    A --> A1[NOT NULL]
    A --> A2[UNIQUE on column]
    A --> A3[PRIMARY KEY on column]
    A --> A4[CHECK on column]

    B --> B1[PRIMARY KEY composite]
    B --> B2[FOREIGN KEY]
    B --> B3[UNIQUE composite]
    B --> B4[CHECK on table]

    C --> C1[CREATE ASSERTION]
    C --> C2[CREATE DOMAIN with CHECK]
    C --> C3[CREATE TRIGGER]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that `ASSERTION` sits in the **Schema-Level** tier — the only declarative constraint that is truly *table-independent* in its definition.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define an SQL assertion. How is it different from a `CHECK` constraint? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
An SQL **assertion** is a schema-level integrity constraint declared using the `CREATE ASSERTION` statement, which specifies a boolean predicate that the database must satisfy at all times. The syntax is:
```sql
CREATE ASSERTION <name> CHECK (<predicate>);
```
The key difference from a `CHECK` constraint is **scope**. A `CHECK` constraint is **table-bound** — it can only reference columns of the table to which it is attached and cannot access other tables. An **assertion**, on the other hand, is a **schema-level** object that may reference any number of tables and is enforced across the entire database. Consequently, assertions are used for cross-table business rules, while `CHECK` is reserved for single-table value validations. Assertions also have their own independent lifecycle (`DROP ASSERTION`) and are not tied to a `CREATE TABLE` statement.

---

**Q2. List any three characteristics of SQL assertions. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Schema-level visibility** — Assertions are independent schema objects, not sub-ordinate to any table.
2. **Multi-table scope** — Their predicate may reference one or more tables, including aggregates, joins, and correlated subqueries.
3. **Continuous enforcement** — The DBMS automatically validates the predicate on every relevant `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE`, aborting any transaction that causes the predicate to evaluate to `FALSE`.
4. **Three-valued logic semantics** — `UNKNOWN` is treated as *satisfied*; only a concretely `FALSE` predicate causes a violation.
5. **First-class lifecycle** — They are created with `CREATE ASSERTION` and removed with `DROP ASSERTION`; there is no `ALTER ASSERTION` in the SQL standard.

*(Any three of the above with a one-line explanation each earn full marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Question (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Consider the following schema for a library database:
- `BOOK(BookID, Title, Price, PublisherID, Year)`
- `AUTHOR(AID, AName, BookID)`
- `PUBLISHER(PublisherID, PName, City)`

Write SQL `CREATE ASSERTION` statements to enforce the following business rules: **(7 Marks)**
1. The total price of all books published by any single publisher must not exceed ₹10,00,000.
2. Every book must have at least one author.
3. No publisher located in the city "Kochi" may publish a book priced below ₹500.

**(b)** Explain with examples the difference between an SQL assertion, a `CHECK` constraint, and a trigger. Why are assertions sometimes considered the "cleanest" way to express integrity rules? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

```sql
-- Rule 1: Per-publisher total price cap
CREATE ASSERTION PUBLISHER_PRICE_CAP
    CHECK (
        NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT PublisherID
            FROM BOOK
            GROUP BY PublisherID
            HAVING SUM(Price) > 1000000
        )
    );

-- Rule 2: Every book must have at least one author
CREATE ASSERTION BOOK_MUST_HAVE_AUTHOR
    CHECK (
        NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT b.BookID
            FROM BOOK b
            LEFT JOIN AUTHOR a ON b.BookID = a.BookID
            WHERE a.AID IS NULL
        )
    );

-- Rule 3: Kochi publishers cannot publish low-priced books
CREATE ASSERTION KOCHI_PUBLISHER_PRICE_FLOOR
    CHECK (
        NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT *
            FROM BOOK b
            JOIN PUBLISHER p ON b.PublisherID = p.PublisherID
            WHERE p.City = 'Kochi' AND b.Price < 500
        )
    );
```

**Explanation of each assertion:**
- **Rule 1** uses `GROUP BY ... HAVING SUM(Price) > 1000000` to find violating publishers; `NOT EXISTS` then enforces that no such publisher exists.
- **Rule 2** uses a `LEFT JOIN` to identify books with no matching author (`a.AID IS NULL`), then asserts that this orphan set is empty.
- **Rule 3** joins `BOOK` and `PUBLISHER` and filters the city and price, again negating with `NOT EXISTS`.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

| Mechanism | Example | Nature | Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| `CHECK` | `CHECK (Price > 0)` on `BOOK` | Declarative | Single table |
| `ASSERTION` | `CREATE ASSERTION ... CHECK (subquery over BOOK + PUBLISHER)` | Declarative | Multi-table |
| `TRIGGER` | `AFTER INSERT ON BOOK … FOR EACH ROW CALL update_publisher_stats()` | Procedural | Multi-table + side effects |

Assertions are considered the "cleanest" mechanism because:
1. **Declarative purity** — You state *what* must be true, not *how* to enforce it. The DBMS optimizer is free to evaluate the predicate in the most efficient way.
2. **No procedural bugs** — Unlike triggers, there is no `IF/THEN/ELSE` control flow to mis-code.
3. **Centralized rule definition** — A business rule lives in one named schema object, easy to audit, version, and document.
4. **Automatic and continuous** — No risk of "forgetting" to fire the rule, as can happen with mis-configured triggers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Consider a university database with the following tables:
- `FACULTY(FID, FName, DeptID, Salary, Designation)`
- `STUDENT(SID, SName, DeptID, Credits)`
- `PROJECT(PID, Title, FID, DeptID, Budget)`

Formulate SQL assertions for: **(7 Marks)**
1. The total budget of all projects in any department must not exceed ₹50,00,000.
2. The number of projects supervised by any professor must not exceed 5.
3. Every project must be supervised by a faculty member belonging to the same department as the project.

**(b)** Discuss the role of the SQL system catalog in storing and managing assertions. What happens during `DROP ASSERTION`? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

```sql
-- Rule 1: Per-department total project budget cap
CREATE ASSERTION DEPT_PROJECT_BUDGET_CAP
    CHECK (
        NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT DeptID
            FROM PROJECT
            GROUP BY DeptID
            HAVING SUM(Budget) > 5000000
        )
    );

-- Rule 2: Max 5 projects per faculty
CREATE ASSERTION FACULTY_PROJECT_LIMIT
    CHECK (
        NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT FID
            FROM PROJECT
            GROUP BY FID
            HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
        )
    );

-- Rule 3: Project's faculty must belong to same department as project
CREATE ASSERTION PROJECT_DEPT_MATCH
    CHECK (
        NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT p.PID
            FROM PROJECT p
            JOIN FACULTY f ON p.FID = f.FID
            WHERE p.DeptID <> f.DeptID
        )
    );
```

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The **system catalog** (also called the **data dictionary**) is a special set of system tables that the DBMS maintains to describe every schema object in the database — tables, views, indexes, users, privileges, *and assertions*.

For each `CREATE ASSERTION` statement, the DBMS stores in the catalog:
- The assertion's **name** and **owner schema**.
- The **text of the predicate** (the `CHECK` expression).
- A list of **referenced tables and columns** (so the constraint manager knows which DML operations to monitor).
- **Deferability attributes** (`DEFERRABLE`, `INITIALLY DEFERRED`, etc.).
- The **state** of the assertion (enabled / disabled).

When `DROP ASSERTION <name>` is executed:
1. The DBMS locates the row in the catalog describing the assertion.
2. It removes the row and any auxiliary metadata (e.g., dependency tracking entries).
3. The constraint manager deregisters the assertion, so it will no longer be re-evaluated on subsequent DML.
4. The assertion is **immediately forgotten** — there is no "soft delete" or recovery (unless the DBMS supports transactional DDL, in which case the drop is part of the current transaction and can be rolled back).

The catalog's role is therefore to be the **single source of truth** for all schema-level rules, enabling the DBMS to perform efficient, dependency-aware constraint management.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Assertion Questions):**
> 1. **Forgetting `NOT EXISTS` wrappers** — A raw `SELECT ... HAVING SUM(...) > X` inside `CHECK (...)` is **not** a boolean predicate. Students often lose 2–3 marks here.
> 2. **Using `CHECK` instead of `ASSERTION` for cross-table rules** — A `CHECK` clause with a subquery on another table is *illegal* in standard SQL. Examiners mark this as a fundamental concept error.
> 3. **Confusing `DROP ASSERTION` with `DROP CONSTRAINT`** — The former removes a schema-level object; the latter removes a table-bound constraint. They are not interchangeable.
> 4. **Omitting the trailing semicolon** — KTU's valuation key is strict about syntactic completeness in the final SQL block.
> 5. **Forgetting to negate the predicate** — Assertions must keep the predicate `TRUE`. Writing the violating condition *without* a `NOT` makes the assertion **inverted** and the database will reject *all* valid updates.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Definition:** An assertion is a *schema-level* SQL constraint declared via `CREATE ASSERTION … CHECK (…)` whose predicate must evaluate to `TRUE` (or `UNKNOWN`) at all times.
- [x] **Scope:** Unlike `CHECK`, an assertion may reference **any number of tables** and may use subqueries, joins, and aggregates.
- [x] **Lifecycle:** Created with `CREATE ASSERTION` ; Removed with `DROP ASSERTION` ; No `ALTER` in the standard.
- [x] **Enforcement:** The DBMS re-evaluates the predicate on every modifying transaction; a `FALSE` result rolls back the transaction (`SQLSTATE 23000`).
- [x] **Three-Valued Logic:** `FALSE` ⇒ violation ; `TRUE` or `UNKNOWN` ⇒ satisfied.
- [x] **Deferability:** `DEFERRABLE` / `INITIALLY DEFERRED` is supported in the standard, allowing transaction-end validation.
- [x] **Idiom:** Always write the *violating* condition inside a subquery and wrap with `NOT EXISTS` to express the rule declaratively.
- [x] **Position in Hierarchy:** `NOT NULL` ⊂ `CHECK` (table) ⊂ **`ASSERTION`** (schema) — assertions are the most general declarative constraint.
- [x] **Vs. Triggers:** Triggers are *procedural* and event-driven; assertions are *declarative* and continuous — assertions are cleaner for pure invariant rules, triggers are necessary when procedural side-effects are required.
- [x] **Common KTU Pattern:** `NOT EXISTS ( SELECT … FROM T1 JOIN T2 … WHERE <violating_condition> )` — memorize this template; it appears in nearly every KTU assertion question.
- [x] **One-line Mantra for Exams:** *"A `CHECK` is to a table what an `ASSERTION` is to the schema — same declarative nature, vastly broader scope."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triggers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT404 - DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Triggers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triggers in SQL (PL/SQL / T-SQL Standard Definition)**
> A **Trigger** is a named, persistent, server-side database object (a special form of **Stored Procedure**) that is **automatically executed (fired/implicitly invoked)** by the DBMS engine in response to a specified **Data Manipulation Language (DML)** event — `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE` — occurring on a specified **base table** or **view**. Unlike stored procedures, triggers cannot be invoked explicitly by the user; they are tightly bound to a table and an event, and the act of modifying data is the *only* way they execute.

A trigger embodies a **declarative event–condition–action (ECA) rule**:
- **Event** → A DML operation (`INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`)
- **Condition** → An optional `WHEN` clause predicate
- **Action** → The procedural body (usually SQL + control flow)

The **SQL:1999 / SQL:2003 / SQL:2011 standards** define triggers as a core part of the SQL language, while vendors like **Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL, and Microsoft SQL Server** extend the standard with proprietary features (e.g., Oracle's `INSTEAD OF` triggers on views, SQL Server's `INSTEAD OF` and `AFTER` triggers).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bank Vault Motion Sensor**
> Imagine a bank's vault. You don't have a security guard standing at the door 24/7 asking "Should we lock down?" The vault itself has a **motion sensor hardwired to it**. The moment someone *tries to open* the door, the sensor detects this *event* automatically and *fires* the action — lights turn on, alarm sounds, manager is notified.
> - The **door opening** = DML event (`INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE`)
> - The **motion sensor** = the Trigger (declared once, bound to the table)
> - The **alarm response** = the trigger's action body
> - The user (you) does not call the alarm — **it just happens automatically** the moment the data changes.

## 1.3 Why Triggers Exist — The Engineering Motivation

1. **Enforce complex business rules** that cannot be expressed via `CHECK` constraints (e.g., cross-table validations).
2. **Maintain derived/audit data automatically** (logging, history tables, denormalized counters).
3. **Preserve referential integrity** beyond simple `FOREIGN KEY` rules (cascading audit trails).
4. **Implement security policies** (e.g., prevent salary column from being viewed/modified by certain roles).

## 1.4 Core Distinguishing Properties (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Properties of Every Trigger**
> 1. **Bound to a Table/View** — Every trigger belongs to exactly one base relation.
> 2. **Event-Driven (Implicit Invocation)** — Never called explicitly by the user; the DBMS engine fires it.
> 3. **Transactional by Default** — A trigger executes within the transaction of the triggering DML statement. If the trigger body fails, the entire DML operation is rolled back.

## 1.5 Standard Trigger Vocabulary (KTU Board-Exam Keywords)

| Term | Meaning | Database Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Firing** | The act of a trigger being executed automatically by the DBMS | Engine-level concept |
| **Triggering Statement** | The SQL `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` that caused the trigger to fire | The DML that the user typed |
| **Trigger Granularity** | Row-level vs. Statement-level — how many times the trigger fires per DML | Per-row or per-statement |
| **OLD / NEW Pseudo-records** | References to the row's data *before* and *after* the change | Row-level triggers only |
| **DML Trigger vs DDL Trigger** | DML fires on data changes; DDL fires on schema changes | Most DBs distinguish these |
| **BEFORE / AFTER** | Timing — whether action runs *before* the row is committed or *after* | Standard SQL |
| **INSTEAD OF** | Replaces the triggering DML entirely (mostly on views) | SQL Server, Oracle |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Trigger — The Six Design Dimensions

Every trigger in SQL is characterized along **six orthogonal axes** (these dimensions are frequent KTU 2-mark and 7-mark questions):

### Dimension 1 — **Timing** (When does the action run?)

- **BEFORE Trigger** → Action executes *before* the DML is applied to the table.
  - Use case: **Data validation**, **auto-filling timestamps**, **normalizing values** before storage.
- **AFTER Trigger** → Action executes *after* the DML is successfully applied.
  - Use case: **Auditing**, **logging**, **propagating changes** to other tables.
- **INSTEAD OF Trigger** → The DML itself is *replaced* by the trigger's action. Originally for **updatable views** that reference multiple base tables.
  - Use case: Making complex views updatable.

### Dimension 2 — **Event** (What DML operation fires it?)

Three possibilities: `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`. Some DBMSs allow `UPDATE OF <column_name>` for fine-grained firing (Oracle, PostgreSQL).

### Dimension 3 — **Granularity** (How many times does it fire?)

- **Statement-Level Trigger** (Default in many DBs) → Fires **once per DML statement**, regardless of how many rows are affected. Suitable for aggregate logging.
- **Row-Level Trigger** → Fires **once for every row** that the DML statement inserts/updates/deletes. Required if you need to inspect `OLD`/`NEW` row values.
  - In Oracle: `FOR EACH ROW` clause.
  - In SQL Server: the trigger is *always* row-level (the `inserted`/`deleted` pseudo-tables may contain multiple rows).

### Dimension 4 — **Triggering Table / View**

The base relation to which the trigger is bound. A trigger can only monitor events on its own table.

### Dimension 5 — **Trigger Body**

The procedural code (SQL + control flow) executed when fired. In standard SQL:2003 the body is an **SQL/PSM** routine (Procedural-SQL Module). Vendor variants:
- Oracle → PL/SQL block
- PostgreSQL → PL/pgSQL
- SQL Server → T-SQL
- MySQL → limited SQL with `IF`, `CASE`, `SIGNAL`

### Dimension 6 — **Triggering Action's Visibility to User**

A trigger runs with the **privileges of its definer** (`CURRENT_USER` / `DEFINER`), not the invoking user — by default. This is critical for **security contexts**.

## 2.2 The OLD and NEW Pseudo-Records (Row-Level Triggers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OLD and NEW — Data Context of a Firing Trigger**
> In row-level triggers, the DBMS exposes two virtual row-images representing the row's state *before* and *after* the DML:
> - **OLD** → the value of the row *prior* to the change.
> - **NEW** → the value of the row *after* the change.

| Event | OLD available? | NEW available? | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| `INSERT` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Row did not exist before. |
| `UPDATE` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Both before- and after-images are meaningful. |
| `DELETE` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Row will not exist after. |

### Vendor-Specific Syntax for OLD/NEW

| DBMS | OLD reference | NEW reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Oracle (PL/SQL)** | `:OLD.column_name` | `:NEW.column_name` |
| **PostgreSQL (PL/pgSQL)** | `OLD.column_name` | `NEW.column_name` |
| **MySQL** | `OLD.column_name` | `NEW.column_name` |
| **SQL Server** | `deleted.column` (pseudo-table) | `inserted.column` (pseudo-table) |
| **Standard SQL:2003** | `OLD ROW AS <var>` | `NEW ROW AS <var>` |

## 2.3 WHEN Clause — The Optional Condition

Some DBMSs (notably **PostgreSQL** and **Oracle 10g+**) allow a **`WHEN` condition** on row-level triggers. The trigger's action runs **only if the WHEN predicate is TRUE** for the affected row. This saves execution time when triggers would otherwise fire pointlessly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** `CREATE TRIGGER ... AFTER UPDATE ON Employee FOR EACH ROW WHEN (OLD.salary <> NEW.salary) ...`
> This trigger fires only when the salary *actually changes*, avoiding redundant logging.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Trigger Design Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Comparison Table (Board-Exam Favourite)**

| Feature | BEFORE | AFTER | INSTEAD OF |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Execution order** | Before DML applied | After DML applied | DML is *replaced* |
| **Can modify NEW row before insert?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | N/A |
| **Can abort the DML?** | ✅ Yes (e.g., `RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR` / `SIGNAL SQLSTATE`) | ✅ Yes (raising an error rolls back) | ✅ Yes |
| **OLD row available?** | ✅ (on UPDATE/DELETE) | ✅ (on UPDATE/DELETE) | ✅ |
| **NEW row available?** | ✅ | ✅ (on INSERT/UPDATE) | ✅ |
| **Can query other tables?** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Allowed on Views?** | ❌ Rarely | ❌ Rarely | ✅ Yes (primary use) |
| **Allowed on Tables?** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ SQL Server forbids on tables |
| **Common use case** | Validation, defaulting | Auditing, replication | Making complex views updatable |

## 2.5 Trigger Execution Order (Multi-Trigger Case)

When **multiple triggers exist on the same table for the same event**, the DBMS uses a deterministic but vendor-specific order:

1. **BEFORE statement-level** triggers
2. For each affected row:
   a. **BEFORE row-level** triggers
   b. The actual DML row mutation
   c. **AFTER row-level** triggers
3. **AFTER statement-level** triggers

> [!WARNING]
> **Oracle** lets you specify ordering via `FOLLOWS` / `PRECEDES` clauses (11g+). **SQL Server** uses `sp_settriggerorder`. Knowing this ordering is a **frequent 7-mark question**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Trigger Use |
|---|---|
| **Banking** | Audit every transaction ≥ ₹50,000 into a regulatory log table. |
| **E-Commerce** | Decrement `stock_qty` automatically when an `order_items` row is inserted. |
| **HR Systems** | Maintain a `salary_history` table on every UPDATE of `Employee.salary`. |
| **Healthcare** | Prevent deletion of patient records (soft-delete flag instead). |
| **Gaming** | Auto-update a leaderboard on score changes. |

## 2.7 Common Pitfalls (Pre-Exam Checklist)

> [!WARNING]
> **Why Triggers Are Used Cautiously in Production**
> 1. **Hidden Logic** — Logic buried in triggers is invisible to application code → debugging nightmare.
> 2. **Cascading Triggers** — Trigger A modifies Table B → fires Trigger B → modifies Table A → infinite loop.
> 3. **Performance** — Row-level triggers on bulk `UPDATE` of 1M rows fire 1,000,000 times.
> 4. **Mutating-Table Errors (Oracle)** — A row-level trigger cannot read the *same* table it is modifying.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> **Methodology Note:** Since the topic "Triggers" is a declarative SQL-DDL + DML construct (not a physics/calculus derivation), the "derivations" below are **exhaustive SQL syntax constructions and worked example walk-throughs**. Every clause, every keyword, and every transition is explained line-by-line — equivalent in rigor to an algebraic derivation.

## 3.1 Standard SQL:2003 Trigger Syntax (The Canonical Form)

The SQL:2003 standard defines triggers via the `CREATE TRIGGER` statement. Below is the **full canonical grammar**, broken down step by step.

### Step 1 — The Trigger Header

$$ \text{CREATE TRIGGER } \langle \text{trigger\_name} \rangle $$

> **Logic:** Every trigger must have a unique name within its schema. Naming convention: `<table>_<event>_<timing>` (e.g., `EMP_AUDIT_AFTER_UPDATE`).

### Step 2 — The Timing Clause

$$ \text{BEFORE} \mid \text{AFTER} $$

> **Logic:** Determines *when* the trigger body fires relative to the DML.

### Step 3 — The Event Clause

$$ \text{INSERT} \mid \text{DELETE} \mid \text{UPDATE [ OF } \langle \text{column\_list} \rangle \text{ ]} $$

> **Logic:** Specifies the DML operation that fires the trigger. The optional `OF column_list` restricts the firing to updates of *those specific columns* (supported in Oracle, PostgreSQL, DB2).

### Step 4 — The Table Reference

$$ \text{ON } \langle \text{table\_name} \rangle $$

> **Logic:** Names the base table or view the trigger is bound to.

### Step 5 — Optional Referencing Clause (Standard SQL Form)

$$ \text{REFERENCING } \begin{cases} \text{OLD [ ROW ] [ AS ] } \langle \text{old\_var} \rangle \\ \text{NEW [ ROW ] [ AS ] } \langle \text{new\_var} \rangle \\ \text{OLD TABLE [ AS ] } \langle \text{old\_tbl} \rangle \\ \text{NEW TABLE [ AS ] } \langle \text{new\_tbl} \rangle \end{cases} $$

> **Logic:** Maps the OLD/NEW pseudo-records to user-defined variable names for use in the trigger body. **Statement-level triggers** use the `OLD TABLE` / `NEW TABLE` forms to expose the *entire set* of affected rows.

### Step 6 — Granularity (FOR EACH ROW / FOR EACH STATEMENT)

$$ \text{FOR EACH } \{\text{ROW} \mid \text{STATEMENT}\} $$

> **Logic:** Determines whether the trigger fires once per row or once per SQL statement. **In SQL Server, triggers are always row-level (effectively) and expose `inserted`/`deleted` pseudo-tables.**

### Step 7 — Optional WHEN Condition

$$ \text{WHEN } \langle \text{predicate} \rangle $$

> **Logic:** Filter — the trigger body executes only when the predicate is TRUE for the row.

### Step 8 — The Triggered Action

$$ \text{BEGIN ATOMIC} \ \langle \text{SQL statements} \rangle \ \text{END} $$

> **Logic:** The procedural body. In standard SQL, the body is wrapped in `BEGIN ATOMIC ... END` to enforce that either the entire body succeeds or none of it does (atomic execution).

### The Complete Canonical Form (Recombined)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE TRIGGER } & \langle \text{name} \rangle \\
& \text{BEFORE} \mid \text{AFTER} \quad \text{INSERT} \mid \text{UPDATE} \mid \text{DELETE} \\
& \text{ON } \langle \text{table} \rangle \\
& [\text{REFERENCING OLD [ROW] AS } \langle o \rangle \text{ NEW [ROW] AS } \langle n \rangle] \\
& \text{FOR EACH ROW} \mid \text{FOR EACH STATEMENT} \\
& [\text{WHEN } \langle \text{predicate} \rangle] \\
& \text{BEGIN ATOMIC} \ \langle \text{body} \rangle \ \text{END};
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Oracle BEFORE INSERT Trigger (Auto-Timestamp)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** Every new row in `Orders` should automatically receive a `created_at` timestamp *if* the application forgot to set one.

### Step 1 — Examine the Base Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE TABLE Orders (} & \\
\text{order\_id} \ & \text{NUMBER PRIMARY KEY,} \\
\text{customer\_id} \ & \text{NUMBER NOT NULL,} \\
\text{amount} \ & \text{NUMBER(10,2),} \\
\text{created\_at} \ & \text{DATE} \\
\text{);} &
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Write the Trigger

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg\_orders\_bi} \\
\text{BEFORE INSERT ON Orders} \\
\text{FOR EACH ROW} \\
\text{BEGIN} \\
& \text{IF :NEW.created\_at IS NULL THEN} \\
& \quad \text{:NEW.created\_at := SYSDATE;} \\
& \text{END IF;} \\
\text{END trg\_orders\_bi;}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Line-by-Line Logic:**
> - Line 1 → Trigger name; `OR REPLACE` allows recompilation.
> - Line 2 → `BEFORE` + `INSERT` + `ON Orders` = fires *before* each insert into `Orders`.
> - Line 3 → `FOR EACH ROW` = row-level granularity.
> - Line 5 → Guard clause: only set timestamp if the application did not already supply one.
> - Line 6 → `:NEW.created_at` is the *new row's* created_at column. Assigning to it *mutates the value before it is stored*.

### Step 3 — Test the Trigger

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{INSERT INTO Orders (order\_id, customer\_id, amount) VALUES (1, 101, 5000.00);} \\
\text{INSERT INTO Orders (order\_id, customer\_id, amount, created\_at) VALUES (2, 102, 7500.00, DATE '2024-01-15');}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Row 1's `created_at` is auto-set to `SYSDATE`; Row 2's `created_at` remains `2024-01-15` because the `IF` check skipped the assignment.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Oracle AFTER UPDATE Trigger (Audit Trail)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** Maintain an `emp_audit` log that records every salary change in the `Employee` table.

### Step 1 — The Audit Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE TABLE emp\_audit (} & \\
\text{audit\_id} \ & \text{NUMBER PRIMARY KEY,} \\
\text{emp\_id} \ & \text{NUMBER,} \\
\text{old\_salary} \ & \text{NUMBER(10,2),} \\
\text{new\_salary} \ & \text{NUMBER(10,2),} \\
\text{changed\_by} \ & \text{VARCHAR2(50),} \\
\text{change\_date} \ & \text{DATE} \\
\text{);} &
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — An Auto-Increment Sequence for audit_id

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE SEQUENCE seq\_audit START WITH 1 INCREMENT BY 1;}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — The Trigger

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg\_emp\_audit} \\
\text{AFTER UPDATE OF salary ON Employee} \\
\text{FOR EACH ROW} \\
\text{WHEN (OLD.salary <> NEW.salary)} \\
\text{BEGIN} \\
& \text{INSERT INTO emp\_audit (audit\_id, emp\_id, old\_salary, new\_salary, changed\_by, change\_date)} \\
& \text{VALUES (seq\_audit.NEXTVAL, :OLD.emp\_id, :OLD.salary, :NEW.salary, USER, SYSDATE);} \\
\text{END trg\_emp\_audit;}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Line-by-Line Logic:**
> - `AFTER UPDATE OF salary` → Trigger fires *only* when the `salary` column is updated (not when other columns change).
> - `WHEN (OLD.salary <> NEW.salary)` → Optimization: skip the insert when salary was not actually different (e.g., set to the same value).
> - `:OLD.salary` and `:NEW.salary` → both row images are available since this is an `UPDATE` event.
> - `USER` → built-in function returning the current Oracle username.
> - `SYSDATE` → current date-time.
> - The audit insert is *itself* a DML, but on a *different* table (no mutating-table error).

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — INSTEAD OF Trigger on a View (SQL Server)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A `CustomerOrdersView` joins `Customers` and `Orders`. By default, this view is **not updatable** because it spans two base tables. We use an `INSTEAD OF` trigger to make `INSERT` on the view redirect to both base tables correctly.

### Step 1 — The View

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE VIEW CustomerOrdersView AS} \\
\text{SELECT c.cust\_id, c.cust\_name, o.order\_id, o.amount, o.order\_date} \\
\text{FROM Customers c JOIN Orders o ON c.cust\_id = o.customer\_id;}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — The INSTEAD OF Trigger

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE TRIGGER trg\_cust\_orders\_insert ON CustomerOrdersView} \\
\text{INSTEAD OF INSERT} \\
\text{AS} \\
\text{BEGIN} \\
& \text{-- Insert into Customers if the customer does not yet exist} \\
& \text{IF NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM Customers WHERE cust\_id = (SELECT cust\_id FROM inserted))} \\
& \text{BEGIN} \\
& \quad \text{INSERT INTO Customers (cust\_id, cust\_name)} \\
& \quad \text{SELECT cust\_id, cust\_name FROM inserted;} \\
& \text{END} \\
& \text{-- Always insert into Orders} \\
& \text{INSERT INTO Orders (order\_id, customer\_id, amount, order\_date)} \\
& \text{SELECT order\_id, cust\_id, amount, order\_date FROM inserted;} \\
\text{END;}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Walk-Through:**
> - The user attempts `INSERT INTO CustomerOrdersView VALUES (...)`.
> - SQL Server's engine normally **rejects** this DML (view is not updatable).
> - But the `INSTEAD OF` trigger **replaces** the user's insert with the two-table logic inside.
> - The `inserted` pseudo-table in SQL Server holds the rows the user tried to insert.
> - The trigger author chose to insert into `Customers` only if the customer is new, and always insert into `Orders`.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — PostgreSQL Statement-Level Trigger (Aggregate Logging)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** Log a single line per `DELETE` statement (not per row) showing the total number of rows deleted from `Products`.

### Step 1 — The Log Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE TABLE delete\_log (} & \\
\text{log\_id} \ & \text{SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,} \\
\text{table\_name} \ & \text{TEXT,} \\
\text{rows\_deleted} \ & \text{INTEGER,} \\
\text{log\_time} \ & \text{TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT\_TIMESTAMP} \\
\text{);} &
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — The Trigger Function

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION fn\_log\_delete() RETURNS TRIGGER AS \$\$} \\
\text{BEGIN} \\
& \text{INSERT INTO delete\_log (table\_name, rows\_deleted)} \\
& \text{VALUES (TG\_TABLE\_NAME, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM OLD\_TABLE));} \\
& \text{RETURN NULL;} \\
\text{END;} \\
\text{LANGUAGE plpgsql;}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — The Trigger Itself

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE TRIGGER trg\_products\_del\_stmt} \\
\text{AFTER DELETE ON Products} \\
\text{REFERENCING OLD TABLE AS old\_table} \\
\text{FOR EACH STATEMENT} \\
\text{EXECUTE FUNCTION fn\_log\_delete();}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:**
> - `FOR EACH STATEMENT` → fires once for the whole DELETE.
> - `OLD TABLE AS old_table` → PostgreSQL exposes the *entire set* of deleted rows as a virtual table.
> - `TG_TABLE_NAME` → built-in variable holding the name of the triggering table.
> - `RETURN NULL` in a statement-level trigger is the convention (row-level triggers return `NEW` or `OLD`).

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Granularity vs Use Case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix — Which Trigger Style to Choose**

| Need | Row-Level | Statement-Level | Vendor Support |
|---|---|---|---|
| Need to access OLD/NEW values of a specific row | ✅ Required | ❌ Cannot (no per-row context) | All major DBs |
| Need to compare before/after values | ✅ Required | ❌ | All major DBs |
| Need to perform aggregate over affected rows | ❌ Inefficient | ✅ Efficient (single call) | PostgreSQL, Oracle, DB2 |
| Need to abort the DML based on row condition | ✅ `WHEN` clause | ❌ | All major DBs |
| Need to set column defaults not handled by DEFAULT | ✅ `BEFORE INSERT` | ❌ | All major DBs |
| Auditing a bulk operation with a single log line | ❌ Fires N times | ✅ Fires once | PostgreSQL, Oracle, DB2 |
| Maintaining a denormalized counter | ✅ with `GROUP BY` outside | ✅ efficient | All major DBs |

## 3.7 Trigger Execution Algorithm (Pseudocode-Level Trace)

For an `AFTER FOR EACH ROW UPDATE` trigger, the engine's internal algorithm is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Algorithm: } \text{FireRowLevelTrigger}(T,\ DML) \\
&1. \ \text{For each row } r \ \text{affected by } DML: \\
&2. \quad \text{Compute } OLD \leftarrow r_{\text{before}},\ NEW \leftarrow r_{\text{after}} \\
&3. \quad \text{If } WHEN(OLD, NEW) \ \text{is FALSE: continue to next row} \\
&4. \quad \text{If timing is BEFORE: apply modifications from body to NEW} \\
&5. \quad \text{If timing is AFTER: write row to base table, then execute body} \\
&6. \quad \text{If body raises an error: roll back the entire transaction} \\
&7. \quad \text{If timing is INSTEAD OF: skip the engine's own DML, body handles it} \\
&8. \ \text{If statement-level: accumulate all affected rows in NEW\_TABLE / OLD\_TABLE, then run body once}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trigger Execution Flow (DML Lifecycle Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `n2`); no reserved keywords used; node labels contain raw uppercase text only — no markdown formatting inside quotes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[USER ISSUES DML STATEMENT] --> B[DBMS PARSES DML]
    B --> C{DOES TRIGGER EXIST ON TABLE FOR THIS EVENT}
    C -- NO --> Z[EXECUTE DML NORMALLY]
    C -- YES --> D[IDENTIFY TIMING BEFORE AFTER INSTEAD OF]
    D --> E[IDENTIFY GRANULARITY ROW LEVEL OR STATEMENT LEVEL]
    E --> F{IS TIMING BEFORE}
    F -- YES --> G[FIRE BEFORE TRIGGER BODY]
    G --> G1[TRIGGER CAN MODIFY NEW VALUES]
    G1 --> G2[TRIGGER CAN ABORT BY RAISING ERROR]
    G2 --> H[APPLY DML TO BASE TABLE]
    F -- NO --> H
    H --> I{IS TIMING AFTER}
    I -- YES --> J[FIRE AFTER TRIGGER BODY]
    J --> J1[ACCESS OLD AND NEW VALUES]
    J1 --> J2[PERFORM AUDIT OR CASCADING UPDATES]
    J2 --> K[COMMIT TRANSACTION]
    I -- NO --> K
    K --> L[RETURN SUCCESS TO USER]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Z fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 BEFORE vs AFTER vs INSTEAD OF — Block Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Block Diagram** showing what data is available and what actions are permitted at each timing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["BEFORE TRIGGER BLOCK"]
        B1[NEW values available: YES] --> B2[OLD values available: YES on UPDATE and DELETE]
        B2 --> B3[Modify NEW before insert: YES]
        B3 --> B4[Common use: VALIDATION AND DEFAULTING]
    end

    subgraph S2["AFTER TRIGGER BLOCK"]
        A1[NEW values available: YES on INSERT and UPDATE] --> A2[OLD values available: YES on UPDATE and DELETE]
        A2 --> A3[Modify NEW before insert: NO already persisted]
        A3 --> A4[Common use: AUDIT LOGGING AND REPLICATION]
    end

    subgraph S3["INSTEAD OF TRIGGER BLOCK"]
        I1[Replaces the user DML entirely] --> I2[Allowed on VIEWS not standard tables in SQL Server]
        I2 --> I3[Common use: MAKING COMPLEX VIEWS UPDATABLE]
    end

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3

    style S1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S3 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Row-Level vs Statement-Level — Fan-Out Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Diagram** showing fan-out behavior. A single `DELETE FROM Products WHERE category = 'X'` deleting 5 rows fires 1 statement-level trigger, but 5 row-level triggers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[USER: DELETE FROM Products WHERE category = X] --> P[PARSER IDENTIFIES 5 AFFECTED ROWS]
    P --> Q{TRIGGER GRANULARITY}

    Q -- STATEMENT LEVEL --> R1[FIRE TRIGGER ONCE]
    R1 --> R2[OLD TABLE contains all 5 deleted rows as a virtual set]
    R2 --> R3[ONE SINGLE LOG ENTRY: deleted 5 rows from Products]

    Q -- ROW LEVEL --> S1[FIRE TRIGGER FOR ROW 1]
    S1 --> S2[FIRE TRIGGER FOR ROW 2]
    S2 --> S3[FIRE TRIGGER FOR ROW 3]
    S3 --> S4[FIRE TRIGGER FOR ROW 4]
    S4 --> S5[FIRE TRIGGER FOR ROW 5]
    S5 --> S6[FIVE INDIVIDUAL LOG ENTRIES: one per row]

    style R3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Multi-Trigger Execution Order (Temporal Stack)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Architecture** showing the temporal sequence of trigger firing for a single DML statement that has all three types defined.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STMT[USER EXECUTES DML STATEMENT] --> A1[STEP 1: BEFORE STATEMENT LEVEL TRIGGER]
    A1 --> B1[STEP 2: FOR EACH ROW LOOP BEGINS]
    B1 --> B2[STEP 3: BEFORE ROW LEVEL TRIGGER]
    B2 --> B3[STEP 4: ENGINE APPLIES DML TO ROW]
    B3 --> B4[STEP 5: AFTER ROW LEVEL TRIGGER]
    B4 --> B5{ANY MORE ROWS}
    B5 -- YES --> B2
    B5 -- NO --> C1[STEP 6: AFTER STATEMENT LEVEL TRIGGER]
    C1 --> C2[STEP 7: COMMIT TRANSACTION]

    style STMT fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Define a trigger in SQL. List any **four** differences between a trigger and a stored procedure.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition:** A trigger is a named, persistent database object that is **automatically executed (fired) by the DBMS** in response to a specified DML event (`INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE`) on a specified table or view. Triggers cannot be invoked explicitly by the user.

**Four Differences:**

| Aspect | Trigger | Stored Procedure |
|---|---|---|
| **Invocation** | Implicit (event-driven by DML) | Explicit (CALL/EXECUTE by user/app) |
| **Binding** | Bound to a specific table/view | Not bound to any table |
| **Parameters** | Cannot accept parameters | Accepts IN/OUT parameters |
| **Return Value** | Cannot return a value | Can return values via OUT params |
| **Execution** | Runs inside the DML's transaction | Runs in its own transaction context |
| **DML on own table** | Restricted (mutating-table error) | Allowed freely |

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *What is the difference between **BEFORE** and **AFTER** triggers? When would you prefer a BEFORE trigger over an AFTER trigger? Give one example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | BEFORE Trigger | AFTER Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution time** | Fires *before* the DML is applied | Fires *after* the DML is applied |
| **NEW mutation** | ✅ Can modify `:NEW` values | ❌ Cannot (row already persisted) |
| **DML success assumption** | ❌ DML may not yet be applied | ✅ DML has succeeded |

> **Preferred Use of BEFORE:** When you need to **validate or auto-fill values** *before* the row is written. For example, a `BEFORE INSERT` trigger on an `Orders` table that auto-sets `created_at = SYSDATE` if the application did not supply it — saving the row write if the value is invalid.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ONE)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** Explain the **six design dimensions** of an SQL trigger. Use neat diagrams to illustrate the BEFORE–AFTER–INSTEAD OF timing taxonomy. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the following schema for a library system:
>
> - `Book(book_id PK, title, price, stock_qty)`
> - `Issue(book_id FK, member_id, issue_date, return_date)`
>
> Write an **Oracle PL/SQL AFTER INSERT row-level trigger** on the `Issue` table that **decrements `Book.stock_qty` by 1** every time a new issue row is inserted. Also, write a **BEFORE INSERT trigger** that ensures `issue_date` is not in the future. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Six Design Dimensions of an SQL Trigger — 7 Marks**

1. **Timing (3 Marks):** BEFORE, AFTER, INSTEAD OF
2. **Event (1 Mark):** INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
3. **Granularity (1 Mark):** Row-level vs Statement-level
4. **Triggering Table (0.5 Mark):** Base table or view name
5. **Trigger Body (0.5 Mark):** SQL/PSM procedural block
6. **Definer's Rights (1 Mark):** Runs with `DEFINER` privileges

**Neat Diagram (already in SECTION 4.2 above — student should reproduce a simple 3-box diagram).**

**(b) Oracle Trigger Implementations — 7 Marks**

**Trigger 1: Decrement stock on issue insert (AFTER INSERT, row-level)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg\_issue\_after\_insert} \\
\text{AFTER INSERT ON Issue} \\
\text{FOR EACH ROW} \\
\text{BEGIN} \\
& \text{UPDATE Book} \\
& \text{SET stock\_qty = stock\_qty - 1} \\
& \text{WHERE book\_id = :NEW.book\_id;} \\
\text{END trg\_issue\_after\_insert;}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Walk-Through:** Trigger fires *after* a row is inserted into `Issue`. The `:NEW.book_id` references the book being issued. The trigger updates the corresponding `Book` row by subtracting 1 from `stock_qty`. Note: we used **AFTER** (not BEFORE) because stock decrement should reflect that the issue *actually* happened.

**Trigger 2: Validate issue_date not in future (BEFORE INSERT, row-level)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg\_issue\_before\_insert} \\
\text{BEFORE INSERT ON Issue} \\
\text{FOR EACH ROW} \\
\text{BEGIN} \\
& \text{IF :NEW.issue\_date > SYSDATE THEN} \\
& \quad \text{RAISE\_APPLICATION\_ERROR(-20001, 'Issue date cannot be in the future');} \\
& \text{END IF;} \\
\text{END trg\_issue\_before\_insert;}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Walk-Through:** Trigger fires *before* the row is inserted. It checks if the new `:NEW.issue_date` is greater than the current date. If so, it raises an application error with code `-20001`, which **rolls back the entire INSERT**, preventing invalid data from entering the table. The use of `BEFORE` is critical here because we want to *prevent* the insert entirely, not log it.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **(a)** With a **neat labeled diagram**, explain the **row-level vs. statement-level trigger** execution flow. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the schema: `Employee(emp_id PK, name, salary, dept_id)` and `Dept(dept_id PK, dept_name, budget)`. Write an Oracle **AFTER UPDATE** trigger on `Employee.salary` that:
>  - Inserts a row into a new table `salary\_history(emp\_id, old\_salary, new\_salary, change\_date)` **only when the salary actually changes** (use a `WHEN` clause).
>  - Ensures the new salary is **not less than the old salary**. If it is, raise an error.
>   **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Row-Level vs Statement-Level — 7 Marks**

**Diagram Reference:** See SECTION 4.3 (the fan-out mermaid diagram). Student should reproduce a simple flowchart showing:
- User issues `DELETE FROM T WHERE ...` (5 rows affected)
- Statement-level: 1 trigger execution, with `OLD TABLE` exposing all 5 rows
- Row-level: 5 trigger executions, each with `:OLD` row context

**Comparison Table (6 distinguishing points × 1 Mark each, capped at 7):**

| Aspect | Row-Level | Statement-Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Firing frequency** | Once per affected row | Once per SQL statement |
| **OLD/NEW access** | ✅ Direct `:OLD` and `:NEW` | ❌ Not available (only OLD/NEW TABLE) |
| **WHEN clause** | ✅ Supported | ❌ Not supported |
| **Mutating-table query** | ❌ Forbidden in Oracle | ✅ Allowed |
| **Bulk DML performance** | ❌ Slow (N firings) | ✅ Fast (1 firing) |
| **Granularity** | Per-row control | Per-statement control |
| **Use case** | Per-row audit, validation | Aggregate logging, summary updates |

**(b) Combined BEFORE/AFTER Update Trigger — 7 Marks**

**First, the history table:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE TABLE salary\_history (} & \\
\text{emp\_id} \ & \text{NUMBER,} \\
\text{old\_salary} \ & \text{NUMBER(10,2),} \\
\text{new\_salary} \ & \text{NUMBER(10,2),} \\
\text{change\_date} \ & \text{DATE} \\
\text{);} &
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trigger 1: BEFORE UPDATE — Validate salary is not decreasing (4 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg\_emp\_salary\_check} \\
\text{BEFORE UPDATE OF salary ON Employee} \\
\text{FOR EACH ROW} \\
\text{WHEN (OLD.salary > NEW.salary)} \\
\text{BEGIN} \\
& \text{RAISE\_APPLICATION\_ERROR(-20002, 'New salary cannot be less than old salary');} \\
\text{END trg\_emp\_salary\_check;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trigger 2: AFTER UPDATE — Log salary history (3 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg\_emp\_salary\_audit} \\
\text{AFTER UPDATE OF salary ON Employee} \\
\text{FOR EACH ROW} \\
\text{WHEN (OLD.salary <> NEW.salary)} \\
\text{BEGIN} \\
& \text{INSERT INTO salary\_history (emp\_id, old\_salary, new\_salary, change\_date)} \\
& \text{VALUES (:OLD.emp\_id, :OLD.salary, :NEW.salary, SYSDATE);} \\
\text{END trg\_emp\_salary\_audit;}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Combined Logic:** When the user issues `UPDATE Employee SET salary = ... WHERE emp_id = X`:
> 1. The `BEFORE UPDATE` trigger first checks the new salary. If it is less than the old, the trigger fires (because `WHEN` is true) and raises an error, **aborting the entire UPDATE**.
> 2. If validation passes, the DML applies the new salary.
> 3. The `AFTER UPDATE` trigger then inserts a row into `salary_history` because the `WHEN (OLD <> NEW)` predicate is true (salary actually changed).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Trigger Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the `:` (colon) in Oracle** — In Oracle PL/SQL, `:NEW.salary` (with colon) is correct; `NEW.salary` (no colon) will throw a compilation error. **−1 Mark.**
> 2. **Confusing BEFORE vs AFTER for validation** — A common mistake is to put a validation check in an `AFTER` trigger. While this *works* (the error rolls back the DML), it is **semantically wrong** — validation belongs in `BEFORE`. Examiners may deduct **1–2 marks** for "conceptual clarity".
> 3. **Not mentioning the `FOR EACH ROW` clause** in Oracle — Default in Oracle is **statement-level**, so row-level access to `:OLD`/`:NEW` will fail. **−2 Marks.**
> 4. **Ignoring mutating-table restrictions** — A row-level trigger that does `SELECT ... FROM Employee` (the same table) on the same row it is modifying causes the dreaded **ORA-04091 mutating-table error**. Examiners love this as a "what is wrong with this trigger?" sub-question. **−1 to −3 Marks.**
> 5. **Writing triggers without `WHEN` clauses** when they are needed — e.g., an `AFTER UPDATE OF salary` trigger that logs *every* update, even when salary did not change. Examiners expect you to optimize with `WHEN (OLD.salary <> NEW.salary)`. **−1 Mark.**
> 6. **Mixing vendor syntaxes** — Writing `:NEW.salary` (Oracle) inside a SQL Server trigger (where you should use `inserted.salary`) or vice versa. **−1 to −2 Marks.** Always state the target DBMS.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention transactional rollback** — A trigger's error automatically rolls back the DML. Forgetting this earns a partial mark deduction in "explain the consequence" type questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Triggers (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Definition** — A trigger is an *event-driven* database object that *implicitly* fires on `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE`; it cannot be called explicitly.
- ✅ **Six Design Dimensions** — Timing, Event, Granularity, Table, Body, Definer rights.
- ✅ **Three Timings** — `BEFORE` (validate/default), `AFTER` (audit/log), `INSTEAD OF` (views).
- ✅ **Two Granularities** — Row-level (`FOR EACH ROW`, uses `:OLD`/`:NEW`) and Statement-level (fires once, uses `OLD TABLE`/`NEW TABLE`).
- ✅ **OLD/NEW Availability Matrix** — INSERT→NEW only; DELETE→OLD only; UPDATE→both.
- ✅ **Vendor Syntax** — Oracle uses `:OLD.col`/`:NEW.col`; SQL Server uses `deleted.col`/`inserted.col`; PostgreSQL/MySQL use `OLD.col`/`NEW.col`.
- ✅ **Execution Order** — Before-Statement → Before-Row → DML → After-Row → After-Statement.
- ✅ **Mutating-Table Rule (Oracle)** — Row-level trigger **cannot** `SELECT` from the same table it is modifying.
- ✅ **Transactional Behavior** — Any error inside a trigger rolls back the *entire* DML transaction.
- ✅ **Standard SQL Syntax** — `CREATE TRIGGER name BEFORE/AFTER event ON table [REFERENCING ...] FOR EACH ROW/STATEMENT [WHEN pred] BEGIN ATOMIC body END;`
- ✅ **Practical Use Cases** — Audit trails, derived data maintenance, cross-table business rules, complex view updatability.
- ✅ **Performance Caveat** — Avoid row-level triggers on bulk DML; prefer statement-level for aggregate logging.
- ✅ **Common Exam Traps** — Wrong vendor syntax; missing `FOR EACH ROW`; placing validation in AFTER; ignoring `WHEN` optimization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT404 - DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Views

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of relational database systems, a **View** is formally defined as a **virtual relation** (or virtual table) that is derived from one or more base relations (or other views) through a query expression, but **does not store its own data physically on disk**. The data accessible through a view is dynamically computed or materialized at the time the view is referenced in a query.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A view is a named query stored in the database catalog (data dictionary). It behaves like a table to the user (it can be queried like a table), but its contents are produced on-demand by executing the defining query against the underlying base tables.

Mathematically, if $Q$ is a SQL query expression and $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$ are base relations, then the view $V$ is defined as:

$$V = Q(B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n)$$

The view $V$ exists as a **schema-level object** (its definition is stored), but its **instance is transient** — it is generated each time it is queried.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Window Analogy

Imagine a large office building with hundreds of rooms. The building is your **database** (the base tables). Now, suppose you are a security guard stationed at a specific window. From that window, you can only see certain rooms — say, the reception area and the conference hall. The window is your **view**.

- You cannot rearrange the furniture in the rooms just by changing your viewpoint (you can sometimes, with restrictions).
- Different guards at different windows see different subsets of the building.
- The building itself never changes location because of where the guards stand.

Similarly:
- A view presents a **subset or transformation** of the underlying tables.
- Multiple users can have different views over the same data.
- The base tables are unaffected by the existence of the view itself.

## 1.3 Physical vs Logical Storage of Views

| Property | Base Table | View |
|----------|-----------|------|
| Physical storage on disk | Yes | No (in most cases) |
| Contains its own data | Yes | No (data is derived) |
| Stored in data dictionary | Schema only | Schema + definition query |
| Updated independently | Yes | Limited (depends on updatability rules) |
| Occupies disk space | Proportional to rows | Only the definition (a few KB) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** In KTU exams, students frequently confuse **Simple (Non-Materialized) Views** with **Materialized Views**. A simple view is recomputed every time it is referenced. A materialized view **physically stores the result** and must be explicitly refreshed. This distinction is a guaranteed 3-mark question in Module 2.

## 1.4 Why Views Matter in Database Engineering

Views serve three critical engineering purposes in modern database systems:

1. **Data Abstraction & Logical Data Independence** — Users interact with simplified logical schemas without knowing physical storage details.
2. **Security & Access Control** — Users can be granted access to a view without being granted access to the underlying base tables.
3. **Query Simplification** — Complex multi-table joins can be pre-packaged as a view, allowing users to write simpler queries.

> [!NOTE]
> According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes, this topic maps to **CO2: Design and implement relational databases using SQL**. Views are assessed as an application of DDL (Data Definition Language) under the SQL umbrella.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Views

Views in SQL are classified along multiple dimensions. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following classification:

### 2.1.1 By Underlying Storage Mechanism

**(a) Simple Views (Non-Materialized / Virtual Views)**
- The defining query is executed every time the view is accessed.
- No physical storage of tuples.
- Always reflects the **current state** of the base tables.
- Used in the majority of real-world OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) systems.

**(b) Materialized Views (Snapshot Views)**
- The result of the defining query is **physically stored**.
- Must be refreshed periodically (`REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW` in PostgreSQL, `MATERIALIZED VIEW` in Oracle).
- Provides superior read performance for complex aggregations.
- Risks **stale data** if not refreshed.
- In KTU 2024, this is a high-weightage concept under the **Data Warehousing** applications of views.

### 2.1.2 By Updatability

**(a) Updatable Views** — A view is updatable if the DBMS can unambiguously translate an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE on the view into an operation on the base table(s). The conditions are:
- Defined over a **single table** (no joins).
- Contains **all NOT NULL columns** of the base table (or has default values).
- **No aggregate functions** (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX).
- **No DISTINCT, GROUP BY, HAVING, UNION**.
- **No subqueries** in the SELECT list.

**(b) Read-Only Views** — Views that violate any of the above conditions cannot be updated through standard SQL DML. Attempting an UPDATE on a read-only view results in a runtime error.

### 2.1.3 By Complexity of Defining Query

| View Type | Description | KTU Frequency |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Row-Subset View** | `WHERE` clause restricts rows | Very High |
| **Column-Subset View** | SELECT restricts columns | Very High |
| **Joined View** | Combines multiple tables | High |
| **Aggregate View** | Uses GROUP BY, aggregates | High |
| **Partitioned View** | UNION of horizontal table partitions | Moderate |
| **Inline View** | Subquery in FROM clause (not stored) | Moderate |

## 2.2 SQL Syntax for View Operations

### 2.2.1 Creating a View

```sql
CREATE VIEW view_name [(column_alias_list)]
AS subquery
[WITH CHECK OPTION];
```

The `WITH CHECK OPTION` clause ensures that any INSERT or UPDATE operation on the view is rejected if it would produce a row that is **not visible through the view itself** (i.e., violates the view's `WHERE` clause).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`WITH CHECK OPTION` is a guaranteed KTU question.** Always remember: it is **LOCAL** by default (in SQL standard) and **CASCADED** if specified explicitly. The cascading behavior propagates the check to underlying views.

### 2.2.2 Dropping a View

```sql
DROP VIEW view_name [RESTRICT | CASCADE];
```

- `RESTRICT` (default): Refuses to drop if other objects depend on the view.
- `CASCADE`: Drops the view and all dependent objects.

### 2.2.3 Updating Through a View

```sql
UPDATE view_name
SET column = value
WHERE condition;
```

This is permitted only if the view is updatable. The DBMS uses a **view resolution / view modification algorithm** to translate this to a base-table operation.

## 2.3 View Resolution Algorithms

When a query references a view, the DBMS has two strategies to process it:

### 2.3.1 Query Modification (Naive Substitution)
The defining query of the view is **textually substituted** into the referencing query. The combined query is then optimized and executed. This is the most common approach in modern systems (used in PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, etc.).

### 2.3.2 View Materialization (Eager Evaluation)
The view is computed and stored as a temporary table. The referencing query is then run against this temporary table. Used for materialized views or complex nested views.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often think the view stores data. In the query modification approach, **the view definition is the only thing stored**, and the actual rows are produced at query time.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Rule | Description |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| View Definition | $V = Q(B_1, \ldots, B_n)$ | View $V$ is the result of query $Q$ over base relations |
| Tuple Cardinality | $\vert V \vert \leq \min(\vert B_1 \vert, \ldots, \vert B_n \vert)$ for joins | View cannot have more rows than smallest base table in a join |
| Updatability Rule | Single table + No aggregates + No DISTINCT + All NOT NULL columns | Required conditions for an updatable view |
| `WITH CHECK OPTION` | $\forall r \in V_{new}, \, Q(r) = \text{true}$ | Every inserted/updated row must satisfy the view's defining predicate |
| Materialized View Space | $S_{MV} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert V_{i} \vert \cdot w$ | Space cost where $w$ = row width in bytes |
| Refresh Cost | $T_{refresh} = O(\vert B \vert \log \vert B \vert)$ | Incremental refresh is generally logarithmic in base size |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** The cardinality constraint is an inequality (≤) because joins can produce more rows than either input if not a key-foreign-key join (i.e., a **many-to-many** join). For a true key-based equi-join, equality holds: $\vert V \vert = \vert B_{\text{outer}} \vert$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Banking Systems** — A view `CustomerAccountSummary` aggregates customer details with account balances. Tellers see this view; they never directly access raw transaction tables.

2. **E-Commerce Platforms** — A view `ActiveProducts` filters out discontinued items (`WHERE status = 'active'`). The website queries this view for display, while the inventory team queries the full base table.

3. **Healthcare Information Systems** — `WITH CHECK OPTION` is used to enforce that doctors can only insert patient records matching their department (e.g., `WHERE department_id = current_dept()`).

4. **Data Warehousing (OLAP)** — Materialized views pre-compute expensive aggregations (monthly sales, regional totals), enabling sub-second response time for dashboards.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a Database Scenario

Consider a university database with the following schema:

$$\text{STUDENT}(\underline{\text{reg\_no}}, \text{name}, \text{dept}, \text{cgpa}, \text{dob})$$

$$\text{ENROLLMENT}(\underline{\text{reg\_no}}, \underline{\text{course\_id}}, \text{semester}, \text{grade})$$

$$\text{COURSE}(\underline{\text{course\_id}}, \text{course\_name}, \text{credits})$$

The primary keys are underlined. `reg_no` in `ENROLLMENT` is a foreign key referencing `STUDENT`, and `course_id` references `COURSE`.

## 3.2 Derivation: Creating and Verifying a Row-Subset View

**Problem:** Create a view named `CSE_Students` that displays all students from the Computer Science department with their registration number, name, and CGPA.

### Step 1 — Identify the columns required
- `reg_no` (identification)
- `name` (display)
- `cgpa` (display)
- `dept` is needed **only for filtering** (in WHERE), not for display.

### Step 2 — Formulate the base query (without the view)

$$
\text{Query}_1 = \pi_{\text{reg\_no}, \text{name}, \text{cgpa}} \left( \sigma_{\text{dept} = \text{'CSE'}} \, (\text{STUDENT}) \right)
$$

### Step 3 — Translate to SQL with CREATE VIEW

```sql
CREATE VIEW CSE_Students (reg_no, name, cgpa)
AS
    SELECT reg_no, name, cgpa
    FROM STUDENT
    WHERE dept = 'CSE';
```

### Step 4 — Verify by querying the view

```sql
SELECT * FROM CSE_Students WHERE cgpa > 8.5;
```

### Step 5 — View Resolution (Query Modification)

The DBMS internally rewrites this as:

$$
\pi_{*} \left( \sigma_{\text{cgpa} > 8.5} \left( \pi_{\text{reg\_no}, \text{name}, \text{cgpa}} \left( \sigma_{\text{dept} = \text{'CSE'}} \, (\text{STUDENT}) \right) \right) \right)
$$

By the **commutativity of selection and projection** (when projection preserves the selection attributes), this can be optimized to:

$$
\pi_{\text{reg\_no}, \text{name}, \text{cgpa}} \left( \sigma_{\text{dept} = \text{'CSE'} \land \text{cgpa} > 8.5} \, (\text{STUDENT}) \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This single-predicate-combination optimization is exactly why query modification is efficient. The DBMS pushes predicates into a single `WHERE` clause before execution.

### Step 6 — Verify Updatability

Checking the updatability rules:
- Single base table: **STUDENT** ✓
- No aggregates: ✓
- No DISTINCT, GROUP BY: ✓
- All NOT NULL columns of STUDENT included? — We omit `dept` and `dob`. If `dept` is `NOT NULL` and has no default, the view is **not updatable** for INSERT operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, **always explicitly state the updatability verdict and the reason**. This is worth 2 of 7 marks in a typical sub-question.

## 3.3 Derivation: Joined View with WITH CHECK OPTION

**Problem:** Create a view showing all students who scored grade 'A' in any course, along with course names. Then attempt an update that would violate the view's condition.

### Step 1 — Define the view

```sql
CREATE VIEW TopPerformers (reg_no, name, course_name, grade)
AS
    SELECT s.reg_no, s.name, c.course_name, e.grade
    FROM STUDENT s
    JOIN ENROLLMENT e ON s.reg_no = e.reg_no
    JOIN COURSE c ON e.course_id = c.course_id
    WHERE e.grade = 'A'
WITH CHECK OPTION;
```

### Step 2 — Test the WITH CHECK OPTION behavior

```sql
UPDATE TopPerformers
SET grade = 'B'
WHERE reg_no = 'S001';
```

**Result:** The DBMS rejects this update because after modification, the row would no longer satisfy `e.grade = 'A'`. The view's defining predicate is violated.

### Step 3 — Formal definition of the check condition

Let $Q_V$ be the view-defining query. The check option enforces:

$$
\forall r \in \text{modified}(V), \; Q_V(r) = \text{true}
$$

Equivalently, for an UPDATE on a single row $r$ to column $c$ with new value $v_{new}$:

$$
Q_V(r[c \leftarrow v_{new}]) = \text{true}
$$

If this predicate evaluates to FALSE, the DBMS raises an error (in PostgreSQL: `ERROR: new row violates check option for view "topperformers"`).

## 3.4 Derivation: Aggregate View (Read-Only Verification)

**Problem:** Create a view `DepartmentStats` that shows, for each department, the number of students and average CGPA.

### Step 1 — Define the view

```sql
CREATE VIEW DepartmentStats (dept, student_count, avg_cgpa)
AS
    SELECT dept, COUNT(*), AVG(cgpa)
    FROM STUDENT
    GROUP BY dept;
```

### Step 2 — Updatability check

| Rule | Status | Reason |
|------|--------|--------|
| Single base table | ✓ | Only STUDENT used |
| No aggregates | ✗ | Uses COUNT, AVG |
| No GROUP BY | ✗ | GROUP BY present |

**Verdict:** **Read-Only View.** Any DML operation against `DepartmentStats` will fail.

### Step 3 — Cardinality analysis

The view's row count is bounded by:

$$
\vert \text{DepartmentStats} \vert = \vert \pi_{\text{dept}}(\text{STUDENT}) \vert \leq \vert \text{STUDENT} \vert
$$

i.e., the number of distinct departments is at most the total number of students.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common View Scenarios in KTU Exams

| Scenario | View Type | Updatable? | WITH CHECK OPTION Useful? | Storage |
|----------|-----------|------------|---------------------------|---------|
| `WHERE dept = 'CSE'` on STUDENT | Row-Subset | Yes (if NOT NULL cols) | Yes | Virtual |
| `SELECT name, cgpa FROM STUDENT` | Column-Subset | Yes (if NOT NULL cols) | No | Virtual |
| 2-table JOIN | Joined | No (multi-table) | Yes (defensive) | Virtual |
| `GROUP BY dept, AVG(cgpa)` | Aggregate | No | No | Virtual |
| Materialized Monthly Sales | Aggregate | No | No | **Physical** |
| `WHERE status = 'active'` enforced | Row-Subset | Yes | **Yes (essential)** | Virtual |
| UNION of 3 regional tables | Partitioned | Limited | No | Virtual |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level View Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a view fits between the user, the SQL engine, and the base tables.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Application / SQL Client] --> B[SQL Query referencing VIEW_V]
    B --> C[Query Parser and Optimizer]
    C --> D{Is V a Materialized View?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Fetch from MV_Storage on Disk]
    D -- No --> F[Query Modification Engine]
    F --> G[Substitute V with its defining query]
    G --> H[Optimized Combined Query]
    H --> I[Execute against Base Tables]
    I --> J[STUDENT]
    I --> K[ENROLLMENT]
    I --> L[COURSE]
    E --> M[Return Result Set to User]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    N[Data Dictionary stores VIEW definition] -.-> C
    N -.-> F
```

## 4.2 View Resolution — Query Modification Flow

This diagram shows the textual substitution of a view's defining query into the user's outer query.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[User Query: SELECT FROM V WHERE cgpa>8.5] --> Q1[Step 1: Look up V in Data Dictionary]
    Q1 --> R1[Retrieve: V = SELECT reg_no, name, cgpa FROM STUDENT WHERE dept=CSE]
    R1 --> S1[Step 2: Textual Substitution]
    S1 --> T1[Combined: SELECT reg_no, name, cgpa FROM STUDENT WHERE dept=CSE AND cgpa>8.5]
    T1 --> U1[Step 3: Predicate Pushdown and Optimization]
    U1 --> V1[Step 4: Execute on Base Table STUDENT]
    V1 --> W1[Return Result Rows]
```

## 4.3 Updatability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New View V] --> B{Defined over multiple tables?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Read-Only View]
    B -- No --> D{Uses aggregate functions?}
    D -- Yes --> C
    D -- No --> E{Uses DISTINCT, GROUP BY, UNION?}
    E -- Yes --> C
    E -- No --> F{Uses subquery in SELECT?}
    F -- Yes --> C
    F -- No --> G{Omits any NOT NULL column?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Updatable for UPDATE/DELETE only]
    G -- No --> I[Fully Updatable View]
```

## 4.4 WITH CHECK OPTION Behavior Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[User executes UPDATE on View V] --> B0{New row satisfies V's WHERE clause?}
    B0 -- Yes --> C0[Propagate UPDATE to Base Table]
    B0 -- No --> D0[Rollback: Raise SQL Error]
    C0 --> E0[Commit transaction]
    D0 --> F0[Error: new row violates check option for view]
    G0[WITH CHECK OPTION LOCAL] -.-> B0
    H0[WITH CHECK OPTION CASCADED] -.-> B0
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, the dotted arrows represent the **scope of the check**: LOCAL checks the immediate view's predicate; CASCADED recursively checks all underlying views' predicates as well.
