# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define a view in SQL. State any two advantages of using views.**

**Model Answer:**

A view is a **virtual table** derived from one or more base tables (or other views) by a stored query. It does not store data physically; instead, the result is computed dynamically when the view is queried.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Data Security** — Sensitive columns (e.g., salary, password) can be hidden from unauthorized users by exposing only a view with selected columns.
2. **Query Simplification** — Complex joins and computations can be pre-defined once in a view, allowing users to query it as if it were a simple table.

*(Alternate advantages: Logical data independence, data consistency, customized user perspectives.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**What is the difference between a simple view and a materialized view?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Simple View | Materialized View |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| Data storage | Virtual, not stored on disk | Physically stored on disk |
| Freshness | Always current (live data) | May be stale; needs refresh |
| Performance | Re-executes query each time | Fast reads (pre-computed) |
| Maintenance | Automatic | Requires explicit REFRESH |
| Use case | OLTP, real-time data | OLAP, data warehousing, dashboards |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Consider the following schema for a library database: `BOOK(book_id, title, author, publisher, price, category)`, `MEMBER(member_id, name, join_date, membership_type)`, `ISSUE(issue_id, book_id, member_id, issue_date, return_date)`. Write SQL statements to create the following views: **(7 marks)**

**(i)** A view `PremiumMembers` that shows `member_id`, `name`, and `join_date` of all members whose `membership_type` is `'Premium'`. Include `WITH CHECK OPTION`.

**(ii)** A view `BookIssueStats` that shows, for each category, the total number of books issued and the average price of books in that category.

**(iii)** A view `RecentIssues` that displays the `issue_id`, `book title`, `member name`, and `issue_date` for issues made in the year 2024 (use JOIN).

**(b)** For each view created in part (a), determine whether it is updatable. Justify your answer with reference to the updatability rules. Also, explain what happens if a user tries to update the `membership_type` of a row in `PremiumMembers` to `'Regular'`. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a)(i):**

```sql
CREATE VIEW PremiumMembers (member_id, name, join_date)
AS
    SELECT member_id, name, join_date
    FROM MEMBER
    WHERE membership_type = 'Premium'
WITH CHECK OPTION;
```

**Part (a)(ii):**

```sql
CREATE VIEW BookIssueStats (category, total_issued, avg_price)
AS
    SELECT b.category,
           COUNT(i.issue_id),
           AVG(b.price)
    FROM BOOK b
    LEFT JOIN ISSUE i ON b.book_id = i.book_id
    GROUP BY b.category;
```

> The `LEFT JOIN` ensures categories with zero issues still appear in the view (with `total_issued = 0` and `avg_price` = category average).

**Part (a)(iii):**

```sql
CREATE VIEW RecentIssues (issue_id, book_title, member_name, issue_date)
AS
    SELECT i.issue_id, b.title, m.name, i.issue_date
    FROM ISSUE i
    JOIN BOOK b ON i.book_id = b.book_id
    JOIN MEMBER m ON i.member_id = m.member_id
    WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM i.issue_date) = 2024;
```

*(Alternative: `WHERE i.issue_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'`.)*

**Part (b): Updatability Analysis**

| View | Updatable? | Reason |
|------|------------|--------|
| `PremiumMembers` | **Updatable** (for UPDATE/DELETE) | Single base table `MEMBER`; no aggregates; no GROUP BY. However, it omits `membership_type` (potentially NOT NULL), so **INSERT is not allowed** without it being supplied through the view. |
| `BookIssueStats` | **Not Updatable** | Uses `COUNT`, `AVG`, and `GROUP BY` — all forbidden for updatability. |
| `RecentIssues` | **Not Updatable** | Multi-table JOIN (3 tables) — not updatable per SQL standard. |

**Effect of UPDATE on `PremiumMembers`:**

```sql
UPDATE PremiumMembers
SET membership_type = 'Regular'
WHERE member_id = 101;
```

**Result:** The DBMS rejects the UPDATE due to `WITH CHECK OPTION`. After modification, the row's `membership_type` would be `'Regular'`, which violates the view's defining predicate `membership_type = 'Premium'`. The DBMS rolls back and raises an integrity error (e.g., PostgreSQL: `ERROR: new row violates check option for view "premiummembers"`).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students often write `CREATE VIEW ... VALUES (...)` — this is invalid syntax. Use `CREATE VIEW ... AS SELECT ...`.
> 2. Forgetting that `WITH CHECK OPTION` only restricts updates that **change the row's visibility**; updates to other columns are allowed.
> 3. Confusing `LEFT JOIN` with `INNER JOIN` in aggregate views — this affects which categories appear.
> 4. Writing `EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date)` without parentheses — invalid.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define a view. Explain with a suitable example the **query modification** approach used by DBMSs to resolve view references. How does it differ from view materialization? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the `EMPLOYEE(emp_id, ename, salary, dept_id, join_date)` and `DEPARTMENT(dept_id, dept_name, location)` tables. Write SQL to: **(7 marks)**

**(i)** Create a view `HighEarners` showing employees earning more than ₹50,000, with `WITH CHECK OPTION`.
**(ii)** Create a view `DeptEmployeeCount` showing department name and number of employees per department (use `GROUP BY`).
**(iii)** Create a view `EmpDeptInfo` that combines employee name, department name, and location for employees in the 'CSE' department.
**(iv)** Drop the view `HighEarners` such that dependent objects are also removed.

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a): View Definition and Query Modification**

A **view** is a virtual table defined by a stored SQL query. It does not store data; only the query definition is kept in the data dictionary.

**Query Modification Approach:**

In this approach, the DBMS resolves a view reference by **textually substituting** the view's defining query into the user's outer query. Example:

Suppose a view is defined as:

```sql
CREATE VIEW CS_Employees
AS
    SELECT emp_id, ename, salary
    FROM EMPLOYEE
    WHERE dept_id = 'CSE';
```

The user submits:

```sql
SELECT ename FROM CS_Employees WHERE salary > 70000;
```

The DBMS rewrites this internally to:

```sql
SELECT ename FROM EMPLOYEE WHERE dept_id = 'CSE' AND salary > 70000;
```

This combined query is then optimized (predicates merged) and executed against the base table.

**Difference from Materialization:**

| Aspect | Query Modification | Materialization |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Execution | View query re-executed per reference | View tuples physically stored |
| Data freshness | Always current | May be stale |
| Storage cost | Negligible | High (proportional to view size) |
| Speed | Slower for complex views | Faster reads |
| Refresh needed? | No | Yes |

**Part (b): SQL Solutions**

**(i) HighEarners with Check Option:**

```sql
CREATE VIEW HighEarners (emp_id, ename, salary, dept_id)
AS
    SELECT emp_id, ename, salary, dept_id
    FROM EMPLOYEE
    WHERE salary > 50000
WITH CHECK OPTION;
```

**(ii) DeptEmployeeCount:**

```sql
CREATE VIEW DeptEmployeeCount (dept_name, num_employees)
AS
    SELECT d.dept_name, COUNT(e.emp_id)
    FROM DEPARTMENT d
    LEFT JOIN EMPLOYEE e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
    GROUP BY d.dept_name;
```

**(iii) EmpDeptInfo:**

```sql
CREATE VIEW EmpDeptInfo (ename, dept_name, location)
AS
    SELECT e.ename, d.dept_name, d.location
    FROM EMPLOYEE e
    JOIN DEPARTMENT d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
    WHERE d.dept_name = 'CSE';
```

**(iv) Drop with CASCADE:**

```sql
DROP VIEW HighEarners CASCADE;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — `CASCADE` vs `RESTRICT`:**
> - `CASCADE` is necessary when other views, triggers, or stored procedures reference this view.
> - `RESTRICT` (the default in many DBMS) will throw an error if any dependency exists.
> - In KTU answers, **always state which behavior you are choosing and why** to earn full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Views**

### Core Definitions
- **View** = Named stored query acting as a virtual table.
- **Base Table** = Table with physical storage.
- **Materialized View** = View with physical storage of result.

### Syntax Essentials
- `CREATE VIEW vname [(aliases)] AS subquery [WITH CHECK OPTION];`
- `DROP VIEW vname [RESTRICT | CASCADE];`

### Updatability Rules (must remember)
1. ✅ Single base table
2. ✅ No aggregate functions
3. ✅ No DISTINCT, GROUP BY, HAVING, UNION
4. ✅ No subqueries in the SELECT list
5. ✅ All NOT NULL columns of base table included (or with defaults)

### WITH CHECK OPTION
- Prevents updates that make a row **invisible** in the view.
- `LOCAL` — checks only the immediate view.
- `CASCADED` — checks all underlying views recursively (default strict behavior).

### View Resolution Strategies
- **Query Modification** — textually substitute; modern standard; always fresh.
- **Materialization** — store tuples; faster reads; needs refresh.

### Advantages (must list at least 3 in long answers)
1. Security (column/row-level access control)
2. Logical data independence
3. Query simplification
4. Data consistency (single definition, multiple consumers)
5. Customized user perspectives

### Disadvantages (must list at least 1 for completeness)
1. Performance overhead (re-execution on each access)
2. Update restrictions (most views are read-only)
3. Dependency management complexity (dropping base tables breaks views)

### KTU-Specific High-Yield Patterns
- **Joins** in views → typically not updatable.
- **Aggregates** in views → always not updatable.
- **`WITH CHECK OPTION`** + single-table row-subset view → classic exam scenario.
- **LEFT JOIN** in aggregate views → preserves categories with zero matches.

### Exam Strategy
- Always write the **CREATE VIEW** statement with explicit column aliases.
- After every view definition, **state whether it is updatable and justify**.
- For `WITH CHECK OPTION` questions, **demonstrate** with a violating UPDATE and explain the error.
- Use **set notation** (σ, π, ⋈) wherever possible in derivations for full marks in theory questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relational Database Design Using ER-to-Relational Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT404 - DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relational Database Design Using ER-to-Relational Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ER-to-Relational Mapping** (also called *ER-to-Relational Translation* or *Logical Schema Design*) is the systematic, algorithm-driven procedure used by database designers to convert the conceptual schema of an **Entity-Relationship (ER) diagram** into an equivalent **relational schema** consisting of a set of well-formed relations (tables) with their attributes, primary keys, and foreign key constraints.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, this mapping is governed by a **seven-step canonical algorithm** (Elmasri & Navathe / Korth-Silberschatz approach) that ensures **lossless-join** and **dependency-preservation** properties are maintained when the conceptual model is realized as physical relations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** A correct ER-to-relational mapping is *information-preserving* — every fact expressible in the ER diagram must be representable in the resulting relational schema, and no spurious tuples should be generated when the relations are joined.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Architectural Blueprint to Building"**
>
> Imagine an **architect's blueprint** (the ER diagram) for a housing colony. The blueprint shows houses, roads, parks, and water pipelines but uses architectural symbols — circles, rectangles, diamonds. Now, a **construction engineer** cannot lay bricks directly from the blueprint; they need a **Bill of Quantities (BoQ)** — a precise list with measurements, material counts, and structural specifications.
>
> The **ER-to-Relational Mapping Algorithm is the Engineer's translation rulebook** that converts every architectural symbol (entity, attribute, relationship) into a row in the BoQ (a relation/table). Just as the BoQ preserves every detail — number of bricks, cement bags, door dimensions — the mapping algorithm ensures no information is lost when moving from concept to implementation.

## 1.3 Input/Output of the Mapping Process

| Stage | Artifact | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | ER Diagram (Conceptual Schema) | E, R, A (Entities, Relationships, Attributes) |
| **Output** | Relational Schema (Logical Schema) | R₁, R₂, ..., Rₙ (Relations) |
| **Auxiliary** | Mapping Algorithm | **Seven canonical steps** |
| **Constraint** | ER Cardinality & Participation Constraints | 1:1, 1:N, M:N; Total / Partial |

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU 2024

This topic typically carries **14 marks** in the End Semester Examination (ESE) under Module 2, often combined with questions on **ER modeling** or **Functional Dependencies**. Mastering the mapping algorithm is essential because:
- It bridges the **conceptual** (ER) and **logical** (Relational) phases of database design.
- It is directly testable through **"Map the given ER diagram to relations"** questions.
- It underlies **Normalization** — a downstream Module 3 topic — because the relations produced here are then analyzed for BCNF/3NF compliance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven-Step ER-to-Relational Mapping Algorithm

Below is the **canonical algorithm** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 scheme. Each step must be applied in sequence.

### **Step 1: Mapping of Strong (Regular) Entities**

For every **strong entity type** $E$ in the ER diagram, create a relation $R$ that includes:
- All **simple attributes** of $E$.
- The **primary key attribute(s)** of $E$.

$$R(\underline{\text{PK}}, \text{simple\_attr}_1, \text{simple\_attr}_2, \ldots)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite attributes** are flattened — only their constituent simple components are included. **Multivalued attributes** and **derived attributes** are **deferred** to Steps 6 and 7 respectively.

### **Step 2: Mapping of Weak Entity Types**

For every **weak entity type** $W$ with **owner entity** $E$ (via identifying relationship $R_{id}$):
- Create relation $R_W$ containing all simple attributes of $W$.
- Include the **primary key of $E$** as a **foreign key** in $R_W$.
- The **primary key of $R_W$** is the **combination** of $W$'s partial key and the foreign key from $E$.

$$R_W(\underline{\text{PK\_of\_}E}, \text{partial\_key\_of\_}W, \text{other\_attrs}) \text{ ON DELETE CASCADE}$$

### **Step 3: Mapping of Binary 1:1 Relationship Types**

For each binary 1:1 relationship $R$ between entities $E_1$ and $E_2$:
- **Strategy A (Foreign Key in either side):** Choose one of the participating entity relations (preferably the one with **total participation**) and add the primary key of the other as a foreign key.
- **Strategy B (Separate Relation):** Create a new relation with the primary keys of both $E_1$ and $E_2$ as a composite primary key.

### **Step 4: Mapping of Binary 1:N Relationship Types**

For each binary 1:N relationship $R$ from $E_1$ (1-side) to $E_2$ (N-side):
- **No new relation is needed.**
- Add the **primary key of $E_1$** as a **foreign key** in the relation corresponding to $E_2$ (the N-side entity).

$$R_{E_2}(\underline{\text{PK\_of\_}E_2}, \ldots, \text{FK\_of\_}E_1, \ldots)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most common** mapping in real-world schemas (e.g., *Department* (1) — *Employee* (N)).

### **Step 5: Mapping of Binary M:N Relationship Types**

For each binary M:N relationship $R$ between $E_1$ and $E_2$:
- **Create a new relation** $R_M$.
- Include the **primary keys of both $E_1$ and $E_2$** as foreign keys.
- The **composite of both foreign keys** forms the **primary key** of $R_M$.
- Add any **descriptive attributes** of $R$.

$$R_M(\underline{\text{PK\_of\_}E_1}, \underline{\text{PK\_of\_}E_2}, \text{desc\_attrs})$$

### **Step 6: Mapping of Multivalued Attributes**

For each **multivalued attribute** $A$ of entity $E$:
- **Create a new relation** $R_A$.
- Include the **primary key of $E$** as a foreign key in $R_A$.
- The **primary key of $R_A$** is the **combination** of the primary key of $E$ and $A$ itself.

$$R_A(\underline{\text{PK\_of\_}E}, \underline{A})$$

### **Step 7: Mapping of N-ary Relationship Types**

For each **n-ary relationship** $R$ ($n \geq 3$):
- **Create a new relation** $R_n$.
- Include the **primary keys of all participating entities** as foreign keys.
- The **composite of all these foreign keys** is the **primary key** of $R_n$.
- Add any descriptive attributes of $R$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Mapping Cheat Sheet

| ER Construct | New Relation? | Primary Key | Foreign Keys | Special Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strong Entity $E$ | Yes ($R_E$) | PK of $E$ | None | Composite attrs flattened |
| Weak Entity $W$ | Yes ($R_W$) | PK(E) + partial_key(W) | PK(E) | ON DELETE CASCADE |
| Binary 1:1 | Usually No (FK in one side) | Unchanged | PK of opposite side | Pick side with total participation |
| Binary 1:N | No | Unchanged | PK of 1-side in N-side | Most efficient mapping |
| Binary M:N | Yes ($R_M$) | PK(E₁) + PK(E₂) | Both | Include descriptive attrs |
| Multivalued Attr $A$ | Yes ($R_A$) | PK(E) + A | PK(E) | Always separate table |
| Derived Attr | No | — | — | Not stored in DB |
| N-ary Relationship | Yes ($R_n$) | All participating PKs | All participating PKs | $n \geq 3$ |
| Specialization (ISA) | Multiple options | Depends on strategy | Superclass PK | Top-down / Bottom-up / Hybrid |
| Union / Category | Yes | PK of representative owner | All member PKs | For partial specialization |

> [!NOTE]
> The pipe symbol `|` is avoided in the table per formatting protocol; vertical separators are conceptual only.

## 2.3 Special Cases: Specialization/Generalization (ISA) Mapping

Three alternative strategies exist:

1. **Multiple Relations (Top-Down / One relation per subclass):**
   - One relation for superclass + one relation for each subclass.
   - Subclass PK = Superclass PK (also FK).
   - Used when most attributes are disjoint and subclasses are queried independently.

2. **Multiple Relations (Bottom-Up / One relation per superclass + union):**
   - All attributes (superclass + subclasses) merged into the superclass relation.
   - NULLs are prevalent; suitable for shallow hierarchies.

3. **Single Relation with Type Attribute (Hybrid / One relation with discriminator):**
   - One relation containing all attributes + a **type/discriminator** attribute.
   - NULLs for irrelevant subclass attributes.
   - Efficient when few subclasses and overlapping queries.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This mapping algorithm is the **foundation of every commercial database design tool** — Oracle Designer, IBM InfoSphere Data Architect, and open-source ERwin/DataGrip. The auto-generated DDL (CREATE TABLE statements) by these tools is essentially a direct execution of Steps 1–7. Mastery of this algorithm enables students to **validate**, **debug**, and **optimize** auto-generated schemas in industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example: University Database ER Diagram

Consider the following ER schema (a classic KTU-favored question):

**Entities & Attributes:**
- **STUDENT** (strong): `StudentID` (PK), `Name`, `DOB`, `Major`
- **COURSE** (strong): `CourseID` (PK), `Title`, `Credits`
- **SECTION** (weak, owner: COURSE): `SectionNo` (partial), `Room`
- **DEPARTMENT** (strong): `DeptID` (PK), `DeptName`
- **INSTRUCTOR** (strong): `InstID` (PK), `Name`, `Salary`

**Relationships:**
- `ENROLLS` (M:N) between STUDENT and SECTION — attribute: `Grade`
- `OFFERS` (1:N) from DEPARTMENT to COURSE
- `TEACHES` (1:1) between INSTRUCTOR and SECTION
- `ADVISES` (1:N) from INSTRUCTOR to STUDENT
- **Multivalued attribute:** STUDENT has `Hobby` (multivalued)

We now apply the **seven-step algorithm** rigorously.

---

### **Step 1: Map Strong Entities**

```
STUDENT(StudentID, Name, DOB, Major)
COURSE(CourseID, Title, Credits)
DEPARTMENT(DeptID, DeptName)
INSTRUCTOR(InstID, Name, Salary)
```

> *Logic:* Each strong entity becomes an independent relation with its PK underlined. Simple attributes are listed in full. No FKs are introduced at this stage.

---

### **Step 2: Map Weak Entity (SECTION)**

```
SECTION(CourseID, SectionNo, Room)
```

**Derivation of Primary Key:**

$$ \text{PK}(\text{SECTION}) = \text{PK}(\text{COURSE}) \cup \text{partial\_key}(\text{SECTION}) $$

$$ \text{PK}(\text{SECTION}) = \text{CourseID} \cup \text{SectionNo} $$

Foreign Key: `CourseID` REFERENCES `COURSE(CourseID)` **ON DELETE CASCADE**.

> *Logic:* A SECTION cannot exist without its parent COURSE; deleting a COURSE must cascade to its sections.

---

### **Step 3: Map Binary 1:1 Relationship (TEACHES)**

Since TEACHES is 1:1 between INSTRUCTOR and SECTION:
- **Strategy chosen:** Add FK in INSTRUCTOR (assuming an instructor always teaches exactly one section — total participation).

```
INSTRUCTOR(InstID, Name, Salary, SectionNo, CourseID)
```

> *Alternative:* Could also place FK in SECTION; the rule is to pick the side with **total participation** to avoid NULLs.

---

### **Step 4: Map Binary 1:N Relationships**

**(a) OFFERS (1:N) — DEPARTMENT (1) → COURSE (N):**

Add `DeptID` as FK in COURSE:

```
COURSE(CourseID, Title, Credits, DeptID)
```

**(b) ADVISES (1:N) — INSTRUCTOR (1) → STUDENT (N):**

Add `InstID` as FK in STUDENT:

```
STUDENT(StudentID, Name, DOB, Major, InstID)
```

> *Logic:* For 1:N, FK always goes on the **N-side** (the "many" side). This is the most efficient mapping because no new table is needed.

---

### **Step 5: Map Binary M:N Relationship (ENROLLS)**

Since ENROLLS is M:N between STUDENT and SECTION (a weak entity):

```
ENROLLS(StudentID, CourseID, SectionNo, Grade)
```

**Primary Key Derivation:**

$$ \text{PK}(\text{ENROLLS}) = \text{PK}(\text{SECTION}) \cup \text{PK}(\text{STUDENT}) $$

$$ \text{PK}(\text{ENROLLS}) = \{\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}, \text{SectionNo}\} $$

Note: Because SECTION is weak, we must use its **full composite key** (`CourseID + SectionNo`) as the FK, **not** just `SectionNo`.

> *Logic:* A new relation is mandatory for M:N. The descriptive attribute `Grade` is stored here, not in STUDENT or SECTION.

---

### **Step 6: Map Multivalued Attribute (Hobby of STUDENT)**

```
STUDENT_HOBBY(StudentID, Hobby)
```

**Primary Key:**

$$ \text{PK}(\text{STUDENT\_HOBBY}) = \text{StudentID} \cup \text{Hobby} $$

> *Logic:* Since a student can have many hobbies, hobby values cannot be atomic in STUDENT. A separate relation is needed, with (StudentID, Hobby) as a composite PK to prevent duplicate hobby entries per student.

---

### **Step 7: Map N-ary Relationships**

In this example, there are no n-ary relationships with $n \geq 3$. If a ternary `EXAM_SUPERVISION(INSTRUCTOR, STUDENT, ROOM)` existed, the mapping would be:

```
EXAM_SUPERVISION(InstID, StudentID, RoomID)
```

with composite PK = all three attributes.

---

## 3.2 Final Relational Schema Summary

```
STUDENT(StudentID, Name, DOB, Major, InstID)
COURSE(CourseID, Title, Credits, DeptID)
DEPARTMENT(DeptID, DeptName)
INSTRUCTOR(InstID, Name, Salary, CourseID, SectionNo)
SECTION(CourseID, SectionNo, Room)
ENROLLS(StudentID, CourseID, SectionNo, Grade)
STUDENT_HOBBY(StudentID, Hobby)
```

## 3.3 Verification of Mapping Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lossless-Join Check:** The decomposition $\{ \text{STUDENT}, \text{ENROLLS}, \text{SECTION} \}$ satisfies the lossless-join property because the common attributes between STUDENT and ENROLLS form a key of ENROLLS ($\text{StudentID}$ is a key of ENROLLS).

> **Dependency-Preservation Check:** All functional dependencies derivable from the ER diagram (e.g., StudentID → Name, (CourseID, SectionNo) → Room) are preserved within individual relations — no transitive dependencies cross relation boundaries.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Seven-Step Algorithm Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read ER Diagram] --> B{Step 1: Strong Entities}
    B --> C[Create relation for each strong entity E]
    C --> D{Step 2: Weak Entities}
    D --> E[Create relation R_W with composite PK]
    E --> F{Step 3: Binary 1:1}
    F --> G[Add FK to side with total participation]
    G --> H{Step 4: Binary 1:N}
    H --> I[Add FK of 1-side to N-side relation]
    I --> J{Step 5: Binary M:N}
    J --> K[Create new relation with composite PK]
    K --> L{Step 6: Multivalued Attributes}
    L --> M[Create separate relation with composite PK]
    M --> N{Step 7: N-ary Relationships}
    N --> O[Create new relation with all participating PKs]
    O --> P[FINAL: Set of well-formed relations]
    P --> Q[Apply Normalization in next phase]
```

## 4.2 Entity-to-Table Transformation Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph InputLayer["Conceptual Layer (ER)"]
        SE[Strong Entity E]
        WE[Weak Entity W]
        B11[Binary 1:1]
        B1N[Binary 1:N]
        BMN[Binary M:N]
        MVA[Multivalued Attribute A]
        NAR[N-ary Relationship]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["Logical Layer (Relations)"]
        R1[R_E: Single table]
        R2[R_W: Table with composite PK + cascade]
        R3[R with FK in one side]
        R4[R with FK on N-side]
        R5[R_MN: New junction table]
        R6[R_A: Separate auxiliary table]
        R7[R_n: New n-ary junction table]
    end

    SE --> R1
    WE --> R2
    B11 --> R3
    B1N --> R4
    BMN --> R5
    MVA --> R6
    NAR --> R7
```

## 4.3 Specialization/Generalization Mapping Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Superclass: VEHICLE] --> SA[Subclass: CAR]
    SP --> SB[Subclass: TRUCK]

    S1[Strategy 1: Top-Down] --> S1a[VEHICLE table]
    S1 --> S1b[CAR table inherits PK]
    S1 --> S1c[TRUCK table inherits PK]

    S2[Strategy 2: Bottom-Up] --> S2a[Single VEHICLE table with all attrs and NULLs]

    S3[Strategy 3: Hybrid] --> S3a[VEHICLE table with Type discriminator attr]
```

## 4.4 Cardinality-to-Mapping Decision Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{What is the relationship cardinality?}
    Q1 -->|1:1| Q2{Any total participation?}
    Q1 -->|1:N| R4[Add FK to N-side]
    Q1 -->|M:N| R5[Create new junction relation]
    Q2 -->|Yes| R3a[Place FK on total-participation side]
    Q2 -->|No| R3b[Create new relation or arbitrarily choose]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"What is ER-to-Relational mapping? List the steps involved in mapping an ER diagram to a relational schema."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** ER-to-Relational mapping is the process of converting the conceptual schema described by an Entity-Relationship (ER) diagram into an equivalent relational schema consisting of a set of relations, attributes, primary keys, and foreign key constraints, while preserving all semantic information.

**Steps (2 marks):**
1. Mapping of Strong (regular) Entity Types
2. Mapping of Weak Entity Types
3. Mapping of Binary 1:1 Relationship Types
4. Mapping of Binary 1:N Relationship Types
5. Mapping of Binary M:N Relationship Types
6. Mapping of Multivalued Attributes
7. Mapping of N-ary Relationship Types (n ≥ 3)

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"How are weak entities mapped to relations? Explain with an example."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A weak entity $W$ depends on an owner entity $E$ for its existence and has a **partial key** (discriminator) instead of a full primary key. To map $W$:
- Create a relation $R_W$ with all simple attributes of $W$.
- Include the **primary key of $E$** as a **foreign key** in $R_W$.
- The **primary key of $R_W$** is the **combination** of $E$'s PK and $W$'s partial key.
- Specify **ON DELETE CASCADE** on the foreign key.

**Example:** For weak entity `ROOM` (partial key: `RoomNo`) owned by `HOTEL` (PK: `HotelID`):

$$ \text{ROOM}(\underline{\text{HotelID}}, \underline{\text{RoomNo}}, \text{Rent}, \text{Type}) $$

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** in the following ways:
> 1. **Forgetting multivalued attributes** in Step 6 (very common omission).
> 2. **Using only the partial key of a weak entity** as PK — this is WRONG; you must include the owner's PK.
> 3. **Forgetting to add `ON DELETE CASCADE`** for weak entities (loses 1 mark).
> 4. **Creating a new relation for 1:N relationships** — this is incorrect and wastes marks.
> 5. **Not underlining primary keys** explicitly in the answer (presentation marks lost).
> 6. Confusing the FK direction in 1:1 — FK should be on the side with **total participation** to minimize NULLs.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Question:** Consider the following ER diagram for a **Library Management System**:
- **BOOK** (strong): `BookID` (PK), `Title`, `Price`, `Year`
- **PUBLISHER** (strong): `PubID` (PK), `PubName`, `Address`
- **AUTHOR** (strong): `AuthorID` (PK), `AuthorName`, `Country`
- **COPY** (weak, owner: BOOK): `CopyNo` (partial), `ShelfLocation`
- **Relationship `PUBLISHED_BY`**: 1:N from PUBLISHER to BOOK
- **Relationship `WRITTEN_BY`**: M:N between BOOK and AUTHOR (attribute: `Royalty`)
- **Multivalued attribute** of BOOK: `Genre`
- **Relationship `BORROWED`**: M:N between MEMBER (PK: `MemberID`, Name, Phone) and COPY

**(a)** Map the above ER diagram to a relational schema, showing all relations, primary keys (underlined), and foreign keys. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Justify why a new relation is necessary for M:N relationships but not for 1:N relationships. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 — Strong Entities:
```
PUBLISHER(PubID, PubName, Address)
BOOK(BookID, Title, Price, Year)
AUTHOR(AuthorID, AuthorName, Country)
MEMBER(MemberID, Name, Phone)
```

Step 2 — Weak Entity (COPY):
```
COPY(BookID, CopyNo, ShelfLocation)
```
PK = {BookID, CopyNo}; FK: BookID REFERENCES BOOK(BookID) ON DELETE CASCADE.

Step 4 — 1:N Relationship (PUBLISHED_BY):
```
BOOK(BookID, Title, Price, Year, PubID)
```
FK: PubID REFERENCES PUBLISHER(PubID).

Step 5 — M:N Relationships:
```
WRITTEN_BY(BookID, AuthorID, Royalty)
BORROWED(MemberID, BookID, CopyNo, BorrowDate)
```
PK(WRITTEN_BY) = {BookID, AuthorID}; PK(BORROWED) = {MemberID, BookID, CopyNo}.

Step 6 — Multivalued Attribute (Genre):
```
BOOK_GENRE(BookID, Genre)
```
PK = {BookID, Genre}.

**Final Schema (complete):**
```
PUBLISHER(PubID, PubName, Address)
BOOK(BookID, Title, Price, Year, PubID)
AUTHOR(AuthorID, AuthorName, Country)
MEMBER(MemberID, Name, Phone)
COPY(BookID, CopyNo, ShelfLocation)
WRITTEN_BY(BookID, AuthorID, Royalty)
BORROWED(MemberID, BookID, CopyNo, BorrowDate)
BOOK_GENRE(BookID, Genre)
```

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**For 1:N Relationships:** The relationship can be represented by simply **adding a foreign key** to the relation on the N-side, which references the PK of the entity on the 1-side. This is efficient because each N-side entity is associated with exactly one 1-side entity — so storing the FK once per N-tuple fully captures the relationship. No new table is required, no NULL values are introduced, and no join is needed to retrieve the related 1-side entity.

**For M:N Relationships:** A single tuple on one side can relate to **multiple** tuples on the other side and vice versa. If we tried to store multiple FKs in a single attribute, we would violate **First Normal Form (1NF)**. Hence, a **separate junction relation** must be created whose composite primary key is the union of the participating entities' primary keys. This junction relation:
- Avoids 1NF violation (atomic FKs).
- Eliminates redundancy (compared to repeating all attributes).
- Preserves lossless-join via the composite PK.

**Trade-off:** M:N mappings cost more in terms of JOIN operations during query execution, but they are the only information-preserving option.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Question:** Consider a **Company Database** with the following ER constructs:
- **DEPARTMENT** (strong): `DeptID` (PK), `DeptName`, `Location`
- **EMPLOYEE** (strong): `EmpID` (PK), `Name`, `Salary`, `HireDate`
- **PROJECT** (strong): `ProjID` (PK), `ProjName`, `Budget`
- **DEPENDENT** (weak, owner: EMPLOYEE): `DepName` (partial), `Age`, `Relation`
- **Relationship `WORKS_IN`**: N:1 from EMPLOYEE to DEPARTMENT
- **Relationship `MANAGES`**: 1:1 between EMPLOYEE and DEPARTMENT (attribute: `StartDate`)
- **Relationship `WORKS_ON`**: M:N between EMPLOYEE and PROJECT (attribute: `Hours`)
- **Multivalued attribute** of EMPLOYEE: `Skill`
- A **ternary relationship** `SUPERVISES` between EMPLOYEE (supervisor), EMPLOYEE (subordinate), and PROJECT.

**(a)** Map the entire ER schema to relations, identifying all PKs and FKs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how specialization/generalization (ISA) is mapped to relations, and list the three possible strategies with one advantage of each. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Strong Entities:**
```
DEPARTMENT(DeptID, DeptName, Location)
EMPLOYEE(EmpID, Name, Salary, HireDate)
PROJECT(ProjID, ProjName, Budget)
```

**Step 2 — Weak Entity (DEPENDENT):**
```
DEPENDENT(EmpID, DepName, Age, Relation)
```
PK = {EmpID, DepName}; FK: EmpID REFERENCES EMPLOYEE(EmpID) ON DELETE CASCADE.

**Step 3 — 1:1 Relationship (MANAGES):**
Place FK in DEPARTMENT (assuming total participation — every dept has a manager):
```
DEPARTMENT(DeptID, DeptName, Location, MgrEmpID, StartDate)
```
FK: MgrEmpID REFERENCES EMPLOYEE(EmpID).

**Step 4 — N:1 Relationship (WORKS_IN):**
Add FK on N-side (EMPLOYEE):
```
EMPLOYEE(EmpID, Name, Salary, HireDate, DeptID)
```
FK: DeptID REFERENCES DEPARTMENT(DeptID).

**Step 5 — M:N Relationship (WORKS_ON):**
```
WORKS_ON(EmpID, ProjID, Hours)
```
PK = {EmpID, ProjID}.

**Step 6 — Multivalued Attribute (Skill):**
```
EMP_SKILL(EmpID, Skill)
```
PK = {EmpID, Skill}.

**Step 7 — Ternary Relationship (SUPERVISES):**
```
SUPERVISES(SupervisorEmpID, SubordinateEmpID, ProjID)
```
PK = {SupervisorEmpID, SubordinateEmpID, ProjID}; all are FKs.

**Final Schema (Consolidated):**
```
DEPARTMENT(DeptID, DeptName, Location, MgrEmpID, StartDate)
EMPLOYEE(EmpID, Name, Salary, HireDate, DeptID)
PROJECT(ProjID, ProjName, Budget)
DEPENDENT(EmpID, DepName, Age, Relation)
WORKS_ON(EmpID, ProjID, Hours)
EMP_SKILL(EmpID, Skill)
SUPERVISES(SupervisorEmpID, SubordinateEmpID, ProjID)
```

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Specialization/Generalization represents an **IS-A hierarchy** — a subclass is-a superclass entity. Mapping ISA hierarchies to relations involves deciding how to distribute superclass and subclass attributes across tables.

**Three Mapping Strategies (6 marks):**

1. **Top-Down (Multiple Relations — one per subclass):**
   - One relation for the **superclass** (PK + superclass attributes).
   - One relation for **each subclass** (PK = superclass PK, also FK; + subclass-specific attributes).
   - **Advantage:** Avoids NULLs; each subclass table is clean and only contains relevant attributes. Best when subclasses have many distinct attributes and are queried separately.

2. **Bottom-Up (Single Relation with all attributes):**
   - One relation containing **all attributes** of the superclass and all subclasses.
   - Subclass-specific attributes will be NULL for non-applicable tuples.
   - **Advantage:** Single table lookup; no JOIN needed. Best for shallow hierarchies with few subclass-specific attributes.

3. **Hybrid (Single Relation with Type Discriminator):**
   - One relation with all attributes + an extra **"Type"** attribute (discriminator) indicating the subclass.
   - **Advantage:** Single-table queries, supports both general and specific queries efficiently. Best when overlap between subclasses is significant.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Strong Entity → New Table** (PK underlined; all simple attrs; flatten composite attrs).
- **Weak Entity → New Table** (PK = owner_PK + partial_key; ON DELETE CASCADE mandatory).
- **1:1 Relationship → FK on the side with TOTAL participation** (or create new table as fallback).
- **1:N Relationship → FK on the N-side ONLY** (no new table; most efficient mapping).
- **M:N Relationship → ALWAYS create a NEW junction table** (PK = union of participating PKs).
- **Multivalued Attribute → ALWAYS create a SEPARATE table** (PK = entity_PK + multivalued_attr).
- **Derived Attribute → NOT stored** in the relational schema (computed at runtime).
- **N-ary Relationship (n ≥ 3) → New table** with all participating PKs as composite key.
- **Composite Attribute → Flattened** — only its simple sub-parts are included in the relation.
- **Specialization/Generalization (ISA) → 3 strategies:** Top-Down, Bottom-Up, Hybrid (with Type attr).
- **Always show FKs with REFERENCES clause** and underline PKs in your final answer (presentation marks!).
- **Verify lossless-join and dependency-preservation** properties to confirm a correct mapping.

> **End of Notes — Module 2: Relational Database Design Using ER-to-Relational Mapping**

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Functional Dependencies (FDs)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Dependency:** A functional dependency, denoted as $X \rightarrow Y$, between two sets of attributes $X$ and $Y$ that are subsets of a relation schema $R$, is a constraint that specifies that for any two tuples $t_1$ and $t_2$ in any valid relation state $r$ of $R$, if $t_1[X] = t_2[X]$, then $t_1[Y] = t_2[Y]$. We say that **$X$ functionally determines $Y$** in $R$, or **$Y$ is functionally dependent on $X$**.

Formally written as: $X \rightarrow Y$ holds in $R$ if and only if, for every permissible instance of $R$, for every pair of tuples $t_1$ and $t_2$:
$$\forall t_1, t_2 \in r(R): \quad (t_1[X] = t_2[X]) \Rightarrow (t_1[Y] = t_2[Y])$$

### Key Terminology
- **Determinant:** The left-hand side attribute(s) $X$ in $X \rightarrow Y$.
- **Dependent:** The right-hand side attribute(s) $Y$ in $X \rightarrow Y$.
- **Trivial FD:** $X \rightarrow Y$ where $Y \subseteq X$ (always true by definition of set inclusion).
- **Non-trivial FD:** $X \rightarrow Y$ where $Y \not\subseteq X$.
- **Completely Non-trivial FD:** $X \rightarrow Y$ where $X \cap Y = \emptyset$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **functional dependency** like a **governor's office rulebook**: "Given the Governor's ID, the Governor's State is uniquely fixed." No two different states can have the same Governor ID. So `GovernorID → State` is a functional dependency — the left side *functionally determines* the right side. The reverse (`State → GovernorID`) is **not** a functional dependency because two governors could be from the same state (e.g., Kerala has had many governors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Functional dependencies are the *semantic constraints* of a database — they describe what the data *means*, not just what it *contains*. The set of all FDs that hold on $R$ is denoted $F$ (or $F^+$, the closure of $F$).

---

## 1.2 Normalization

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Normalization:** The process of organizing the attributes and relations of a relational schema to reduce data redundancy and eliminate undesirable characteristics like insertion, update, and deletion anomalies. It involves decomposing relations into smaller, well-structured relations based on functional dependencies.

### Why Normalize? (The Three Anomalies)

| Anomaly Type | Real-World Analogy | Description |
|--------------|--------------------|-------------|
| **Insertion Anomaly** | Cannot add a new department unless a student joins it | Cannot insert a tuple without having values for all required attributes |
| **Update Anomaly** | Changing a department name in one row but forgetting in another causes inconsistency | Same data appears in multiple rows; updating one place but not all leads to inconsistency |
| **Deletion Anomaly** | Deleting the last student of a department also deletes the department information | Deleting one fact inadvertently loses another unrelated fact |

### The First Normal Form (1NF)

> [!NOTE]
> **First Normal Form (1NF):** A relation schema $R$ is in 1NF if and only if the domain of every attribute in $R$ contains only **atomic (indivisible) values** and every attribute contains only a single value from that domain (no multi-valued attributes, no repeating groups, no sets).

**Disallowed in 1NF:**
- A cell containing multiple values (e.g., `Phone: {9876543210, 9123456789}`)
- A cell containing a composite value (e.g., `Name: "John Smith"` where Name has sub-parts)
- Repeating groups of columns (e.g., `Phone1, Phone2, Phone3`)

### The Second Normal Form (2NF)

> [!NOTE]
> **Second Normal Form (2NF):** A relation schema $R$ is in 2NF if and only if it is in 1NF and **every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on every candidate key** of $R$. Equivalently, no non-prime attribute is **partially dependent** on a candidate key.

A **partial dependency** occurs when a non-prime attribute is functionally dependent on only *part* of a composite candidate key, not the whole key.

### The Third Normal Form (3NF)

> [!NOTE]
> **Third Normal Form (3NF):** A relation schema $R$ is in 3NF if and only if it is in 2NF and **no non-prime attribute is transitively dependent on any candidate key** of $R$. Equivalently, for every non-trivial FD $X \rightarrow A$ in $R$, either $X$ is a superkey, or $A$ is a prime attribute.

A **transitive dependency** exists when $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow Z$ hold, but $Y \not\rightarrow X$, thereby implying $X \rightarrow Z$ transitively.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Library Card Analogy
Imagine a **messy library card**:
- **1NF violation:** The card stores multiple book titles in one field — like a sticky note with 5 books scribbled on it. Solution: one row per book.
- **2NF violation:** The card uses `(CardID, BookID)` as the key, but stores the CardHolder's name (which depends only on CardID, not BookID). Solution: split into a `Card` table and a `Borrowing` table.
- **3NF violation:** The Card table stores `CardID → DeptID → DeptName`. Department name depends on Department ID, not directly on Card ID. Solution: split into a `Card` table and a `Department` table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Normal Forms:** Every 2NF relation is in 1NF, and every 3NF relation is in 2NF. So: $\text{3NF} \subseteq \text{2NF} \subseteq \text{1NF}$.

---

## 1.3 Transaction Processing — Introduction

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Transaction:** A transaction is a logical unit of database processing that includes one or more database access operations (read and write operations) that must be executed as an **atomic** and **indivisible** unit. It is a logical unit of work that transforms the database from one consistent state to another consistent state.

Formally, a transaction $T$ is a sequence of operations:
$$T = \{O_1, O_2, O_3, \ldots, O_n\}$$
where each $O_i$ is either a `READ` or `WRITE` operation, and the sequence is bounded by `BEGIN_TRANSACTION` and `END_TRANSACTION` (or `COMMIT` / `ROLLBACK`).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Bank ATM Analogy
A **transaction** is like a **money transfer at an ATM**:
1. You insert your card and enter the amount.
2. ATM checks your balance (READ).
3. ATM debits your account (WRITE).
4. ATM credits the recipient (WRITE).
5. ATM prints receipt.

All these steps must happen **together as one unit** — if step 3 succeeds but step 4 fails (network error), you have *lost* your money. The transaction concept says: either **all** steps happen, or **none** happen. The system never leaves the database in a half-finished state.

---

## 1.4 Problems and Failures in Transactions

### Categories of Failures
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The DBMS must guarantee transaction atomicity and durability in the face of the following failures:

| Failure Type | Description | Recovery Strategy |
|--------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **System Crash (Power Failure)** | Hardware, software, or network failure halts processing | Log-based recovery (REDO/UNDO) |
| **Transaction Error** | Logical error in the program causes transaction to abort | Rollback (UNDO) |
| **Local Error / Exception** | Condition detected by transaction itself (e.g., insufficient balance) | Rollback to savepoint |
| **Concurrency Problems** | Multiple transactions interfere with each other | Concurrency control (locks, timestamps) |
| **Disk Failure** | Physical damage to disk storage | Backup + log-based recovery (REDO) |
| **Physical Problems / Catastrophes** | Fire, flood, sabotage | Backup tapes + log |

### Common Concurrency Problems
1. **Lost Update Problem (WW Conflict):** Two transactions read and write the same data item; the second write overwrites the first update, losing it.
2. **Dirty Read Problem (WR Conflict):** A transaction reads data written by an uncommitted concurrent transaction.
3. **Incorrect Summary Problem:** A transaction reads aggregate data while another is updating underlying records.
4. **Unrepeatable Read Problem (RW Conflict):** A transaction reads the same item twice and gets different values due to another transaction's update.

---

## 1.5 Desirable Properties of Transactions — The ACID Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **ACID Properties:** A transaction must possess four essential properties — **Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability** — collectively known as the **ACID properties**. These are the cornerstone guarantees of any robust DBMS.

| Property | Formal Definition | Who Guarantees It | Analogy |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Atomicity** | A transaction is an *all-or-nothing* unit. Either all operations execute successfully and commit, or none take effect. | Transaction Manager (TM) | ATM giving or not giving money — never "half-given" |
| **Consistency** | A transaction transforms the database from one consistent state to another. All integrity constraints are preserved. | Programmer + DBMS | A library card must always have a valid borrower |
| **Isolation** | Concurrent transactions execute as if they were running serially (one after another). Intermediate states are hidden. | Concurrency Control Manager | Each person in a public restroom has privacy |
| **Durability** | Once a transaction commits, its effects are permanent and survive any subsequent failure. | Recovery Manager (RM) | A signed contract cannot vanish overnight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mnemonic:** **ACID = "Always Carefully Isolate Data"** — Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.

---

## 1.6 Characterizing Schedules Based on Recoverability

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Schedule (History):** A schedule $S$ is a sequence (ordered list) of operations from one or more transactions, where the operations of each individual transaction must appear in the same order as in that transaction. It represents the actual chronological execution order.

### Recoverability Classifications

**1. Recoverable Schedule:**
A schedule $S$ is **recoverable** if, for every transaction $T_i$ that commits, $T_i$ reads only values written by transactions $T_j$ such that $T_j$ has already committed. In other words, no transaction commits until all transactions from which it read have committed.

**2. Cascadingless (Cascadeless) Schedule:**
A schedule is **cascadingless** if transactions only read committed values — that is, no transaction $T_i$ reads a value written by an uncommitted transaction $T_j$. Cascading rollbacks are therefore avoided.

**3. Strict Schedule:**
A schedule is **strict** if no transaction may read or write a value written by an uncommitted transaction. This is the strongest recoverability class.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of transactions as **chefs sharing a kitchen**:
- A **recoverable schedule** is one where no chef serves a dish based on raw ingredients from an unconfirmed supplier.
- A **cascadingless schedule** is one where chefs only use ingredients that are already officially delivered.
- A **strict schedule** is one where chefs don't even *touch* the pantry until the previous chef's work is fully signed off.

---

## 1.7 Characterizing Schedules Based on Serializability

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Serializability:** A schedule $S$ is **serializable** if it is *equivalent* to some serial schedule of the same $n$ transactions. It produces the same final result as if the transactions were executed one after another without interleaving.

### Types of Serializability

**1. Conflict Serializability:**
Two operations **conflict** if they belong to different transactions, access the same data item, and at least one is a `WRITE`. A schedule is **conflict serializable** if it is conflict-equivalent to some serial schedule (i.e., its precedence graph is acyclic).

**2. View Serializability:**
A schedule $S$ is **view serializable** if it is view-equivalent to a serial schedule. Two schedules are view-equivalent if:
- They read the same initial values.
- They write the same final values for each data item.
- The transaction that performs the final write on each data item is the same.

### Precedence Graph (Serialization Graph) Method
- **Nodes:** Transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$.
- **Edges:** An edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ exists if $T_i$ must precede $T_j$ in any equivalent serial schedule (i.e., $T_j$ reads a value previously written by $T_i$, or $T_i$ writes a value later read by $T_j$).
- **Rule:** A schedule is conflict-serializable **if and only if** its precedence graph is **acyclic**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Assembly Line Analogy
Imagine two car assembly lines sharing a single robotic arm. A **serial schedule** means Line A finishes entirely, then Line B uses the arm. A **serializable schedule** means the two lines share the arm in pieces, but the final cars look *exactly* as if they were built one at a time. The key is that the cars produced are **indistinguishable** in result.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Dependency Theory — Inference Rules

The **Armstrong's Axioms** (Inference Rules) are used to derive the closure $F^+$ of a set of functional dependencies $F$.

### Primary Inference Rules (Armstrong's Axioms)

| Rule | Notation | Description |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Reflexivity** | If $Y \subseteq X$, then $X \rightarrow Y$ | Trivial FD; always true |
| **Augmentation** | If $X \rightarrow Y$, then $XZ \rightarrow YZ$ | Adding attributes to both sides preserves FD |
| **Transitivity** | If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow Z$ | Chain of dependencies |

### Secondary Inference Rules (Derived from Armstrong's Axioms)

| Rule | Notation | Description |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Union** | If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow YZ$ | Combine right sides |
| **Decomposition** | If $X \rightarrow YZ$, then $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$ | Break right side |
| **Pseudo-transitivity** | If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $WY \rightarrow Z$, then $WX \rightarrow Z$ | Generalized transitivity |
| **Composition** | If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Z \rightarrow W$, then $XZ \rightarrow YW$ | Combine both FDs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Result:** Armstrong's axioms are **sound** (generate only FDs in $F^+$) and **complete** (generate all FDs in $F^+$).

---

## 2.2 Attribute Closure Algorithm

The **closure of an attribute set** $X$ under $F$, denoted $X^+$, is the set of all attributes functionally determined by $X$.

### Algorithm: Computing $X^+$
```
Input: Attribute set X, FD set F
Output: X⁺ (closure of X)

1. Initialize X⁺ = X
2. Repeat:
     For each FD (Y → Z) in F:
         If Y ⊆ X⁺, then:
             X⁺ = X⁺ ∪ Z
   Until X⁺ does not change
3. Return X⁺
```

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Use |
|---------|---------------|-----|
| **Trivial FD** | $X \rightarrow Y$ where $Y \subseteq X$ | Always true; ignored in normal form tests |
| **Closure** | $X^+$ = set of all attributes derivable from $X$ | Test for superkey, compute $F^+$ |
| **Superkey Test** | $X$ is a superkey iff $X^+ \supseteq R$ (all attributes) | Key identification |
| **Candidate Key** | Minimal superkey (no proper subset is a superkey) | Primary key candidate |
| **Extraneous Attribute** | $A$ in $X$ of $X \rightarrow Y$ is extraneous if $(X - \{A\}) \rightarrow Y$ still holds | FD minimization |
| **1NF Condition** | All attribute values must be atomic | Eliminate repeating groups |
| **2NF Condition** | 1NF + no partial dependency on candidate key | Move partial deps to new relation |
| **3NF Condition** | 2NF + no transitive dependency of non-prime attrs | Split transitive chain |
| **ACID** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | Transaction guarantees |
| **Conflict Serializable** | Precedence graph is acyclic | Test for conflict-serializability |
| **Recoverable** | $T_i$ reads only from already-committed $T_j$ | Avoid cascading aborts |
| **Cascadingless** | $T_i$ reads only committed values | No cascading rollbacks |
| **Strict** | No read or write of uncommitted data | Easiest recovery |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Concept | Industry Application |
|---------|----------------------|
| **FD Analysis** | Data warehouse design, schema integration after mergers |
| **Normalization** | Banking systems (no anomaly in account transactions), ERP systems |
| **ACID Transactions** | E-commerce checkout, railway reservation, stock trading platforms |
| **Serializability** | Multi-user databases (hospitals, airlines, e-commerce) |
| **Recovery** | Disaster recovery in cloud databases (AWS RDS, Azure SQL) |
| **Conflict Detection** | Deadlock detection in distributed databases |

---

## 2.4 Transaction States and Operations

A transaction goes through the following states:

| State | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Active** | The initial state; transaction is executing its operations |
| **Partially Committed** | All operations have executed successfully, but changes are still in main memory (not yet flushed to disk) |
| **Committed** | All changes have been permanently recorded in the database |
| **Failed** | Normal execution can no longer proceed; transaction will be rolled back |
| **Aborted** | The database has been rolled back to its state prior to transaction start |

### Operations in a Transaction

| Operation | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `BEGIN_TRANSACTION` | Marks the start of transaction execution |
| `READ` or `GET` | Reads a database item from disk to main memory |
| `WRITE` or `PUT` | Writes a database item from main memory to disk |
| `COMMIT` | Signals successful end of transaction; changes become permanent |
| `ROLLBACK` (or `ABORT`) | Signals unsuccessful end; undo all changes |

---

## 2.5 Recovery System Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Log** | A sequential file of records recording all database changes |
| **Log Record Types** | `[BEGIN, T]`, `[WRITE, T, X, old_value, new_value]`, `[COMMIT, T]`, `[ABORT, T]`, `[CHECKPOINT]` |
| **UNDO** | Reversing changes made by a transaction that did not commit |
| **REDO** | Reapplying changes made by a committed transaction (lost due to crash) |
| **Checkpoint** | A point in the log where the DBMS forces all modified buffers to disk |
| **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** | Rule: log records must be written to disk *before* the actual data changes |

---

## 2.6 Conflict Serializability Test — Algorithm

**Step-by-step procedure:**

1. Create a precedence graph $G$ with one node per transaction.
2. For each pair of conflicting operations $O_i$ in $T_i$ and $O_j$ in $T_j$ (in that order in $S$):
   - If they conflict on data item $X$ (at least one is WRITE), add a directed edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$.
3. If the resulting graph has **no cycles**, the schedule is **conflict-serializable**; the topological order of nodes gives the equivalent serial schedule.
4. If a cycle exists, the schedule is **NOT conflict-serializable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two operations conflict** if they:
> (a) belong to different transactions,
> (b) access the same data item $X$, and
> (c) at least one is a `WRITE` operation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Finding Candidate Keys Using Attribute Closure

### Problem
Given relation $R = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ and the set of functional dependencies:
$$F = \{A \rightarrow BC, \ BC \rightarrow DE, \ B \rightarrow F\}$$

Find all **candidate keys** of $R$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify which attributes never appear on the right side of any FD (i.e., attributes not determined by anyone). These must be part of every candidate key.
- Right-side attributes: $B, C, D, E, F$
- Attributes never on right side: $A$
- Therefore, $A$ **must be in every candidate key**.

**Step 2:** Start with $X = \{A\}$ and compute $A^+$.
- Initialize: $A^+ = \{A\}$
- Apply $A \rightarrow BC$: $A^+ = \{A, B, C\}$
- Apply $B \rightarrow F$: $A^+ = \{A, B, C, F\}$
- Apply $BC \rightarrow DE$: $A^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$
- $A^+ = R$. So $A$ alone is a **superkey**.

**Step 3:** Since $\{A\}$ is a superkey, check if any proper subset of $\{A\}$ is also a superkey. The only proper subset is $\emptyset$, which cannot be a superkey. Therefore, $A$ is a **candidate key**.

**Step 4:** Find more candidate keys by considering combinations.

**Try $A$ with each missing attribute?** Since $A$ is already a key, we don't need to add attributes to it.

**Try sets not containing $A$?** Impossible because $A$ is required.

**Therefore, the only candidate key is $\boxed{A}$.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Decomposition into 2NF

### Problem
Consider the relation:
$$\text{EMP\_DEPT}(\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}, \text{DeptID}, \text{DeptName})$$

with FDs: $\{\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}\} \rightarrow \text{DeptID}, \text{DeptName}$ and $\text{DeptID} \rightarrow \text{DeptName}$.
Primary key: $(\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID})$.

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1:** Identify partial dependencies. Since $\text{DeptID} \rightarrow \text{DeptName}$, and we don't have $\text{DeptID} \rightarrow \text{EmpID}$ or $\text{DeptID} \rightarrow \text{ProjectID}$, this is a **transitive dependency** (not partial), so 2NF is already satisfied? Let's check more carefully.

Actually, the issue is the **transitive dependency** $\{\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}\} \rightarrow \text{DeptID} \rightarrow \text{DeptName}$. This makes it violate 3NF.

**Step 2:** Decompose into 3NF:
- $R_1 = \text{ASSIGN}(\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}, \text{DeptID})$ with FD $\{\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}\} \rightarrow \text{DeptID}$
- $R_2 = \text{DEPT}(\text{DeptID}, \text{DeptName})$ with FD $\text{DeptID} \rightarrow \text{DeptName}$

**Step 3:** Verify each relation.
- $R_1$: Key is $(\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID})$. Only non-prime is $\text{DeptID}$, fully dependent on the key. ✓ 3NF.
- $R_2$: Key is $\text{DeptID}$. Only non-prime is $\text{DeptName}$, fully dependent on the key. ✓ 3NF.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Serializability Test Using Precedence Graph

### Problem
Consider the following schedule $S$:
$$S: r_1(X), r_2(X), w_1(X), r_3(X), w_2(X), w_3(X)$$

Determine if $S$ is conflict-serializable.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify all conflicting pairs (different transactions, same item, at least one WRITE).

| Pair | Operations | Conflict? | Edge |
|------|------------|-----------|------|
| $r_1(X), r_2(X)$ | both READ | No conflict | — |
| $r_2(X), w_1(X)$ | READ then WRITE on $X$ | Conflict (WR) | $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ (since $T_1$ writes after $T_2$ reads) |
| $w_1(X), r_3(X)$ | WRITE then READ on $X$ | Conflict (WR) | $T_1 \rightarrow T_3$ |
| $r_3(X), w_2(X)$ | READ then WRITE | Conflict | $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$ |
| $w_1(X), w_2(X)$ | both WRITE | Conflict (WW) | $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ |
| $w_2(X), w_3(X)$ | both WRITE | Conflict (WW) | $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$ |

**Step 2:** Construct the precedence graph.
- Edges: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$, $T_1 \rightarrow T_3$, $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$.

**Step 3:** Check for cycles. The graph shows $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3$ (linear order). No cycle.

**Step 4:** Conclusion. The schedule is **conflict-serializable**, equivalent to the serial order $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Recoverability Analysis

### Problem
Consider the schedule:
$$S: r_1(X), w_1(X), r_2(X), w_2(Y), c_1, c_2$$

Classify the schedule.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Check recoverability.
- $T_2$ reads $X$ written by $T_1$. Does $T_1$ commit before $T_2$ commits?
- $T_1$ commits ($c_1$) before $T_2$ commits ($c_2$). ✓ **Recoverable**.

**Step 2:** Check cascadingless.
- $T_2$ reads $X$ written by **uncommitted** $T_1$. So this is **NOT cascadingless**.

**Step 3:** Check strict.
- $T_2$ reads $X$ written by uncommitted $T_1$. So this is **NOT strict**.

**Step 4:** Conclusion. The schedule is **Recoverable but NOT Cascadingless and NOT Strict**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Schedule Classification Hierarchy

Let $S$ be a schedule. The following implications hold:

$$\text{Strict} \Rightarrow \text{Cascadingless} \Rightarrow \text{Recoverable} \Rightarrow \text{No cascading aborts}$$

**Conflict Serializable** and **View Serializable** are *separate* properties. The relationship:

$$\text{Conflict Serializable} \subset \text{View Serializable} \subset \text{All Schedules}$$

A schedule can be **view serializable but not conflict serializable** (a rare case called "blind writes" scenario).

### Example: View Serializable but Not Conflict Serializable

| Schedule | Precedence Graph | Result |
|----------|------------------|--------|
| $r_1(X), w_2(X), w_1(X), w_3(X)$ | $T_1 \to T_2, T_2 \to T_1$ → cycle | Not conflict-serializable |
| But view-equivalent to $T_2, T_1, T_3$ | — | View serializable |

---

## 3.6 Detailed Example: ACID in Banking

Consider a money transfer transaction $T$ that transfers ₹500 from Account A to Account B.

$$T: \text{read}(A), \ A = A - 500, \ \text{write}(A), \ \text{read}(B), \ B = B + 500, \ \text{write}(B)$$

### Demonstration of Each ACID Property

**Atomicity:** If a system crash occurs between `write(A)` and `write(B)`, the DBMS must `UNDO` the debit so A is restored to its original value. There is no "half-transfer" state.

**Consistency:** The invariant $\text{sum}(A) + \text{sum}(B) = \text{constant}$ must be preserved. After $T$ completes, the total remains the same. If any constraint is violated, $T$ is rolled back.

**Isolation:** If another transaction $T'$ is reading the balance of A or B concurrently, it sees the **pre-transfer** or **post-transfer** state — never a partial state. Locks (e.g., `LOCK X` on A and B) enforce this.

**Durability:** Once $T$ commits, the new balances survive any subsequent crash. The Write-Ahead Log (WAL) ensures log records are flushed to disk before commit confirmation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** In numerical questions, show the precedence graph with **labeled edges** indicating the data item causing the conflict (e.g., `T1 → T2 [X]`).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Normalization Process Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Unnormalized Relation] --> stepB[Identify Repeating Groups and Multi-valued Attributes]
    stepB --> nf1[First Normal Form 1NF - Atomic Values]

    stepC --> nf2[Second Normal Form 2NF - Remove Partial Dependencies]
    nf2 --> stepD[Identify Transitive Dependencies among Non-Prime Attributes]
    stepD --> nf3[Third Normal Form 3NF - Remove Transitive Dependencies]
    nf3 --> stepE[BCNF - Every Determinant is a Candidate Key]
    stepE --> stepF[4NF - Remove Multi-valued Dependencies]
    stepF --> stepG[5NF - Remove Join Dependencies]
    stepG --> doneX[Optimally Normalized Database]

```

## 4.2 Transaction State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> activeState
    activeState --> partiallyCommitted: All operations executed
    activeState --> failedState: Error or abort initiated
    partiallyCommitted --> committedState: COMMIT successful
    partiallyCommitted --> failedState: Crash before commit
    failedState --> abortedState: ROLLBACK complete
    committedState --> [*]
    abortedState --> [*]
    note right of committedState: All changes permanently saved to disk
    note right of abortedState: Database restored to prior consistent state
```

## 4.3 ACID Properties — Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    acid[Transaction T] --> atom[Atomicity]
    acid --> cons[Consistency]
    acid --> iso[Isolation]
    acid --> dur[Durability]
    atom --> tm[Transaction Manager]
    cons --> prog[Application Logic plus DBMS Constraints]
    iso --> ccm[Concurrency Control Manager - Locks Timestamps]
    dur --> rm[Recovery Manager - Write Ahead Log]
    tm --> logData[Log Files]
    ccm --> lockData[Lock Tables]
    rm --> backupData[Backup plus Checkpoints]

```

## 4.4 Precedence Graph Example for Conflict Serializability Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tx1((T1)) -- r1 then w1 conflict on X --> tx2((T2))
    tx1 -- w1 then w2 conflict on X --> tx2
    tx2 -- r2 then w2 conflict on Y --> tx3((T3))
    tx1 -- w1 then r3 conflict on X --> tx3
    tx2 -- w2 then w3 conflict on Y --> tx3
    note[Schedule: r1X, r2X, w1X, r3X, w2Y, w3Y - ACYCLIC - Conflict Serializable - Order: T1, T2, T3]
    style tx1 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000
    style tx2 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000
    style tx3 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000
```

## 4.5 Schedule Recoverability Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    allSch[All Schedules] --> str[Strict Schedules]
    allSch --> casc[Cascadingless Schedules]
    allSch --> rec[Recoverable Schedules]
    allSch --> unrec[Unrecoverable Schedules - Should be avoided]
    str --> casc
    casc --> rec
    rec --> unrec
    note1[Strict means no read or write of uncommitted data]
    note2[Cascadingless means reads only from committed transactions]
    note3[Recoverable means no T commits before T that it read from commits]
    str -.- note1
    casc -.- note2
    rec -.- note3

```

## 4.6 Functional Dependency Closure Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Input Attribute Set X plus FD Set F] --> init[Initialize X plus equal to X]
    init --> loop[For each FD Y rightarrow Z in F]
    loop --> check{Y is subset of X plus}
    check --> yes[Add Z to X plus]
    check --> no[Skip this FD]
    yes --> moreAny{More FDs to check}
    no --> moreAny
    moreAny --> yes2[Repeat loop]
    moreAny --> no2[No change - Final X plus]
    yes2 --> loop
    no2 --> output[Output X plus - Closure]
    output --> test{Is X plus equal to R}
    test --> yes3[Yes - X is a Superkey]
    test --> no3[No - X is not a Superkey]
    style input fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style output fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000
    style yes3 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000
    style no3 fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
