transactions**, a distinguishing feature over many other NoSQL stores. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes all key formulas, complexity bounds, and engineering metrics that the KTU 2024 examiner expects students to know for the Graph Stores module. | Concept / Metric | Formula / Definition | Notation Explained | Units / Domain | |------------------|----------------------|--------------------|----------------| | Graph definition | $G = (V, E, P)$ | $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges, $P$ = properties | Discrete math | | Graph order (size) | $\vert V \vert = n$ | Number of nodes | Dimensionless | | Graph size (edge count) | $\vert E \vert = m$ | Number of edges | Dimensionless | | Node degree (undirected) | $\deg(v) = \text{number of incident edges}$ | Sum of edges touching $v$ | Dimensionless | | Average degree | $\bar{d} = \dfrac{2m}{n}$ | For undirected graph | Edges/node | | Density | $\rho = \dfrac{2m}{n(n-1)}$ | Fraction of possible edges present | Range $[0, 1]$ | | Adjacency matrix storage | $O(n^2)$ | $n$ nodes, full $n \times n$ matrix | Cells | | Adjacency list storage | $O(n + m)$ | Node list + edge list | Entries | | Traversal cost (per hop) | $O(1)$ | Index-free adjacency | Operations | | $h$-hop traversal cost | $O(k^h)$ | $k$ = avg degree, $h$ = hop count | Operations | | Space complexity (native) | $O(n + m)$ | Nodes + edges | Entries | | Relational JOIN cost (naïve) | $O(n^2)$ | Cartesian product + filter | Rows scanned | | Diameter of graph | $\max_{u,v \in V} d(u,v)$ | Longest shortest path | Hops | | BFS time complexity | $O(n + m)$ | Visits each node/edge once | Operations | | DFS time complexity | $O(n + m)$ | Same as BFS | Operations | | Watts–Strogatz clustering | $C(p) = (1-p)^3 \cdot C_0$ | $p$ = rewiring prob., $C_0$ = initial | Range $[0, 1]$ | | Six-degrees separation | $h \le 6$ | Avg. separation in social nets | Hops | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** The KTU examiner frequently asks students to compare **adjacency matrix** vs. **adjacency list** storage, and to justify *why* graph stores outperform RDBMSs for relationship-heavy queries. Memorize the formulas above verbatim. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Graph Stores are mission-critical in the following engineering domains: * **Social Networks** — Facebook TAO, LinkedIn, Twitter use graph backends to compute "people you may know," "follow recommendations." * **Fraud Detection** — PayPal, Mastercard use Neo4j to detect fraud rings via cycle detection in transaction graphs. * **Knowledge Graphs** — Google Knowledge Graph, Amazon Product Graph, IBM Watson. * **Network & IT Operations** — Dependency graphs for root-cause analysis. * **Recommendation Engines** — Amazon, Netflix, Spotify collaborative filtering. * **Bioinformatics & Drug Discovery** — Protein-protein interaction networks. * **Supply Chain & Logistics** — Route optimization, supplier-dependency mapping. * **Identity & Access Management (IAM)** — Role hierarchies, permission graphs. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Storage Complexity — Adjacency Matrix vs. Adjacency List **Problem:** Compare the space required to store a graph with $n$ nodes and $m$ edges using (a) an adjacency **matrix**, and (b) an adjacency **list**. ### Step 1 — Define the Graph Parameters We are given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with: $$\vert V \vert = n, \quad \vert E \vert = m$$ The maximum possible number of edges in an undirected simple graph is given by the binomial coefficient: $$m_{\max} = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$ ### Step 2 — Derive Adjacency Matrix Storage The adjacency matrix $A$ is a square symmetric matrix of dimension $n \times n$: $$A_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } (v_i, v_j) \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ The total number of cells in the matrix is: $$N_{\text{matrix}} = n \times n = n^2$$ Hence, the **space complexity** is: $$S_{\text{matrix}} = O(n^2)$$ This holds *regardless* of $m$ — even a sparse graph with very few edges still consumes $n^2$ cells. Wasted cells equal: $$W_{\text{matrix}} = n^2 - 2m \quad (\text{since each edge is recorded twice in an undirected matrix})$$ ### Step 3 — Derive Adjacency List Storage The adjacency list stores, for **each** node $v$, a linked list of its neighbours. The total entries equal $2m$ (each edge is listed from both endpoints in an undirected graph): $$N_{\text{list}} = n + 2m$$ Where $n$ accounts for the array of node-head pointers and $2m$ accounts for the edge entries. Hence: $$S_{\text{list}} = O(n + m)$$ ### Step 4 — Comparative Analysis For a **sparse graph** (typical of real-world data, e.g., social networks where $m \ll n^2$): $$\frac{S_{\text{list}}}{S_{\text{matrix}}} = \frac{n + 2m}{n^2} \approx \frac{2m}{n^2} = \rho \ll 1$$ Where $\rho$ is the **graph density**. For example, in a social network with $n = 10^6$ users and average degree $k = 100$: $$m = \frac{n \cdot k}{2} = 5 \times 10^7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{2m}{n(n-1)} \approx 10^{-4}$$ The adjacency list uses only **0.01%** of the storage of the adjacency matrix. ### Step 5 — Conclusion for KTU Examination | Property | Adjacency Matrix | Adjacency List | |----------|------------------|----------------| | Space | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n + m)$ | | Edge existence check | $O(1)$ | $O(\deg(v))$ | | Neighbour iteration | $O(n)$ | $O(\deg(v))$ | | Suitable graph type | Dense | Sparse | | Used by | Some graph algorithms | **Native graph stores (Neo4j)** | > [!NOTE] > **Native graph stores (Neo4j, JanusGraph) use a variant of the adjacency list with index-free adjacency**, giving O(1) per-hop traversal cost. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Graph Store Comparison with Other NoSQL Families The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU comparison table** the examiner frequently demands: | Feature / DB Type | **Graph Store (Neo4j)** | **Document (MongoDB)** | **Key-Value (Redis)** | **Wide-Column (Cassandra)** | **Relational (PostgreSQL)** | |-------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------| | Data model | Nodes + Edges + Props | JSON / BSON docs | Key → Value | Column families (rows × cols) | Tables (rows × cols) | | Schema | Schema-flexible (label-based) | Schema-flexible | Schema-less | Schema-flexible (CF-based) | Rigid, predefined schema | | Query language | **Cypher / GQL** | MQL (MongoDB Query) | GET, SET, GETALL | CQL (CQL = Cassandra QL) | SQL (ISO/IEC 9075) | | ACID support | **Full ACID** (Neo4j 4.x+) | Multi-doc ACID (4.x+) | Single-key atomicity | Tunable / Eventual | Full ACID | | Relationship handling | **Native, O(1) per hop** | Manual (refs + `$lookup`) | None (foreign keys not enforced) | None | JOINs (costly) | | Horizontal scaling | Limited (sharding in 5.x) | Excellent (sharding native) | Excellent (cluster-mode) | **Excellent (masterless)** | Possible (complex) | | Best fit | Highly connected data | Semi-structured docs | Cache, sessions, queues | Time-series, write-heavy | Structured, transactional | | Use case example | Fraud detection, social graphs | Content management, IoT | Session store, leaderboard | IoT telemetry, messaging | Banking, ERP | | CAP emphasis | **CP** (consistency + partition) | CP / AP (configurable) | **AP** (in cluster) | **AP** (masterless) | CA (single-node) → CP | --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Cypher Query Analysis > [!NOTE] > The following Cypher query is for illustration. The KTU examiner may ask you to *interpret* a Cypher query, not write one in a non-CS branch; for CS students (PBCMT404), however, this is a frequent question. **Sample graph:** A person `Anand` lives in `Kerala`, is friends with `Meera`, and `Meera` is friends with `Rahul`. ```cypher MATCH (a:Person {name: "Anand"})-[:FRIEND_OF]->(b:Person)-[:FRIEND_OF]->(c:Person) RETURN a.name, b.name, c.name ``` **Step-by-step interpretation:** 1. `MATCH` — Locate a pattern of three nodes connected by two `:FRIEND_OF` edges. 2. `(a:Person {name: "Anand"})` — Node `a` has label `Person` and property `name = "Anand"`. 3. `-[:FRIEND_OF]->` — Directed edge of type `FRIEND_OF`. 4. The pattern represents a **2-hop traversal** — friends-of-friends. 5. `RETURN` — Output the names of the three nodes. **Cost analysis (per KTU formula sheet):** $$T_{\text{query}} = O(k^2)$$ Where $k$ = average node degree (number of friends per person). For $k = 200$ (typical LinkedIn degree), the engine evaluates up to $200^2 = 40{,}000$ 2-hop paths per starting node. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Property Graph Data Model — Node & Edge Architecture ```mermaid graph LR nodeP["Person Node"]:::personNode nodeM["Movie Node"]:::movieNode nodeG["Genre Node"]:::genreNode edgeA["ACTED_IN Edge"]:::edgeA edgeD["DIRECTED Edge"]:::edgeD edgeH["HAS_GENRE Edge"]:::edgeH nodeP --- edgeA edgeA --- nodeM nodeP --- edgeD edgeD --- nodeM nodeM --- edgeH edgeH --- nodeG classDef personNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef movieNode fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef genreNode fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef edgeA fill:#F4B183,stroke:#C55A11,color:#000 classDef edgeD fill:#B4A7D6,stroke:#7030A0,color:#FFF classDef edgeH fill:#A9D08E,stroke:#548235,color:#000 ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** * The **orange node** (`Person`) holds a label `:Person` and properties like `name`, `born`. * The **blue node** (`Movie`) holds a label `:Movie` and properties like `title`, `released`. * The **green node** (`Genre`) holds a label `:Genre` and a property like `name`. * The **directed edges** (`ACTED_IN`, `DIRECTED`, `HAS_GENRE`) connect the three nodes. Each edge may also carry properties (e.g., `ACTED_IN.role = "Neo"`). --- ## 4.2 Graph Store — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TB clientApp["Client Application"]:::client queryParser["Query Parser
Cypher Gremlin SPARQL"]:::parser queryOptimizer["Query Optimizer
Cost-Based Plan Generator"]:::optimizer executionEngine["Execution Engine
Traversal Planner BFS DFS"]:::engine storageLayer["Storage Layer"]:::storage indexFreeAdj["Index Free Adjacency
Pointer Hopping"]:::adj txManager["Transaction Manager
ACID Compliance"]:::tx persistDisk["Persistent Disk Storage
Native Graph Files"]:::disk clientApp --> queryParser queryParser --> queryOptimizer queryOptimizer --> executionEngine executionEngine --> storageLayer storageLayer --> indexFreeAdj storageLayer --> txManager storageLayer --> persistDisk classDef client fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#2E75B6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef parser fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef optimizer fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C55A11,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef engine fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef storage fill:#EDEDED,stroke:#404040,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef adj fill:#D9E1F2,stroke:#2E75B6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tx fill:#F4B084,stroke:#C55A11,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef disk fill:#A9D08E,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` **Architecture Interpretation (mapped to Neo4j-style implementation):** 1. **Client Application** — Issues queries via Bolt, HTTP, or embedded drivers. 2. **Query Parser** — Parses Cypher/Gremlin/SPARQL into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). 3. **Query Optimizer** — Generates an optimized logical & physical plan using cost estimation. 4. **Execution Engine** — Drives the **traversal planner** which uses BFS/DFS with pruning. 5. **Storage Layer** — Native graph engine containing: * **Index-Free Adjacency** — Each node stores direct pointers to its edges. * **Transaction Manager** — Enforces ACID via write-ahead logging (WAL). 6. **Persistent Disk Storage** — Page-cache-backed native graph files (e.g., Neo4j's `neostore.*`). --- ## 4.3 Traversal Flow — Multi-Hop Pattern Matching ```mermaid graph LR startNode["Start Node A"]:::start level1["Hop 1 Neighbour Set B"]:::level1 level2["Hop 2 Neighbour Set C"]:::level2 resultSet["Filtered Result Set"]:::result startNode -->|"k1 edges"| level1 level1 -->|"k2 edges"| level2 level2 -->|"property filter"| resultSet classDef start fill:#FFD966,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef level1 fill:#A9D08E,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef level2 fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef result fill:#F4B084,stroke:#C55A11,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Traversal Interpretation:** * **Start Node (A):** Entry point of the query (e.g., the user `Anand`). * **Hop 1 Neighbours (B):** All nodes connected to A by one edge (e.g., direct friends). Cardinality = $k_1$ (average degree). * **Hop 2 Neighbours (C):** All nodes reachable from B by one additional edge. Cardinality = $k_1 \cdot k_2 \approx k^2$. * **Filtered Result:** A subset of C satisfying property predicates (e.g., `lives_in = "Kerala"`). This visualizes the **exponential growth of the candidate set with hop depth** — the central performance challenge in graph traversal. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer) > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part A consists of short-answer questions worth 2–3 marks each, requiring direct conceptual answers. ### Q1. Define a graph store. Mention any **two** property-graph query languages. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **graph store** (or graph database) is a NoSQL data store that models data as a **graph** consisting of **nodes (vertices)**, **edges (relationships)**, and **properties**. It is purpose-built for storing and traversing highly connected data with native support for relationships as first-class citizens. Two property-graph query languages are: 1. **Cypher** — declarative pattern-matching language used by Neo4j. 2. **Gremlin** — graph traversal language defined by Apache TinkerPop, used by JanusGraph, Amazon Neptune. *(Alternative valid answer: SPARQL, GQL, GraphQL with graph backends.)* --- ### Q2. Differentiate between **nodes** and **edges** in a property graph. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Property | Nodes (Vertices) | Edges (Relationships) | |----------|------------------|------------------------| | Definition | Discrete entities in the graph | Directed connections between two nodes | | Purpose | Represent real-world objects | Represent associations/relations between objects | | Identification | Unique internal node-ID | Unique edge-ID + start + end node | | Properties | May hold key-value pairs | May also hold key-value pairs | | Examples | `Person`, `Movie`, `City` | `KNOWS`, `ACTED_IN`, `LOCATED_IN` | | Cardinality | $n = \vert V \vert$ | $m = \vert E \vert$ | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** and feature an **internal choice** (i.e., the student answers *either* Q-A *or* Q-B). Each is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **Q-A.(a)** Explain the **graph data model** in detail. Discuss the components — nodes, edges, labels, and properties — with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The **graph data model** is a flexible, schema-optional data model that represents information as a network of interconnected entities. It is one of the four primary NoSQL data models. **1. Nodes (Vertices):** Nodes are the primary data entities. Each node can be assigned one or more **labels** that define its role/type (e.g., `:Person`, `:Movie`). A node carries a unique identifier and may hold multiple **properties** (key-value pairs). **2. Edges (Relationships):** Edges are directed connections between exactly two nodes, representing associations. Each edge has a **type** (e.g., `:KNOWS`, `:WORKS_FOR`) and a **direction**. Edges can also carry **properties** (e.g., `since: 2019`, `weight: 0.85`). **3. Labels:** Categorical markers on nodes used for grouping and fast index lookup. They are analogous to table names in RDBMS but allow a node to carry multiple labels (polymorphism). **4. Properties:** Attribute-value pairs attached to nodes or edges, providing metadata. **5. Example:** Consider a movie database modelled as a graph: * Node: `(:Person {name: "Anand", born: 2002})` * Node: `(:Movie {title: "Inception", released: 2010})` * Node: `(:Genre {name: "Sci-Fi"})` * Edge: `(Anand)-[:ACTED_IN {role: "Neo"}]->(Inception)` * Edge: `(Inception)-[:HAS_GENRE]->(Sci-Fi)` This is a **property graph** because properties are attached to both nodes and edges. **6. Mathematical Form:** The graph can be formally written as $G = (V, E, P)$ with $n = \vert V \vert$ nodes and $m = \vert E \vert$ edges. --- **Q-A.(b)** Compare and contrast **Graph Stores** with **Document Stores**. Discuss the suitability of each for relationship-intensive workloads. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Comparison Axis | Graph Stores (Neo4j) | Document Stores (MongoDB) | |-----------------|----------------------|---------------------------| | **Data model** | Nodes, Edges, Properties (Property Graph) | JSON/BSON documents with nested fields | | **Schema** | Schema-flexible (label-based, no rigid schema) | Schema-flexible (collection-level, no rigid schema) | | **Relationship representation** | **Native edges with O(1) pointer traversal** | Manual references + `$lookup` JOIN (costly) | | **Query language** | Cypher, Gremlin, SPARQL | MQL (MongoDB Query Language), aggregation pipeline | | **Best workload** | **Highly connected, multi-hop queries** (e.g., friends-of-friends) | Semi-structured, document-oriented data (e.g., user profiles, product catalogues) | | **ACID support** | Full ACID (Neo4j 4.x+) | Multi-document ACID (4.x+) | | **Indexing** | Index-free adjacency + label/property indexes | B-tree, text, geospatial indexes | | **Scaling** | Vertical + limited horizontal | **Native horizontal sharding** | | **Traversal cost** | $O(k^h)$ for $h$ hops | $O(n^2)$ for multi-collection joins | **Suitability for Relationship-Intensive Workloads:** For workloads with **deep, multi-hop traversals** (e.g., fraud rings, social-network recommendations, knowledge graphs), **Graph Stores are vastly superior** because: * **Index-free adjacency** enables O(1) per-hop cost. * Relationships are stored physically, not reconstructed via JOIN. * Pattern matching via Cypher is natural and performant. **Document Stores** are preferable when: * Data is largely self-contained within a document. * Relationships are shallow (1–2 levels at most). * Horizontal scalability and write throughput are the primary concerns. **Conclusion:** Graph Stores dominate when **relationships are the data**; Document Stores dominate when **documents are the data** with shallow relations. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **Q-B.(a)** Explain the concept of **index-free adjacency** in native graph stores. How does it give performance advantage over relational JOINs? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** **Index-free adjacency (IFA)** is the storage principle used in **native graph databases** (Neo4j, JanusGraph) where each node stores direct physical (or logical) pointers to all its incident edges, and edges store pointers to their neighbouring nodes. **Mechanism:** * When a graph is loaded, every node $v_i$ maintains a list of pointer references to its edges $E(v_i) = \{e_{i1}, e_{i2}, \ldots, e_{ik_i}\}$. * When a query traverses from $v_i$ to $v_j$, the engine simply **dereferences a pointer** to reach $v_j$ in **O(1) time**. * No index lookup, no hash table, no JOIN re-resolution is required at each step. **Performance Advantage over Relational JOINs:** In a relational DBMS, a JOIN between two tables of sizes $n$ and $m$ costs (with hash join): $$T_{\text{join}} = O(n + m)$$ But for a chain of JOINs representing an $h$-hop traversal: $$T_{\text{multi-join}} = O\left(\prod_{i=1}^{h} n_i\right) \approx O(n^h)$$ In contrast, with IFA, the cost is: $$T_{\text{IFA}} = O(h \cdot k) \approx O(k^h \text{ worst case}, O(h) \text{ per matched path})$$ **Quantitative Illustration (per KTU 2024 sample):** | Query Type | RDBMS Cost | Graph Store (IFA) Cost | |------------|------------|------------------------| | 1-hop neighbour | $O(n)$ scan | $O(1)$ pointer hop | | 2-hop (friends of friends) | $O(n^2)$ join | $O(k^2)$ | | 5-hop with $n = 10^6$, $k = 50$ | $10^{30}$ operations | $50^5 = 3.125 \times 10^8$ operations | **Conclusion:** IFA delivers a **super-exponential performance gain** for connected-data queries, making graph stores the de-facto choice for relationship-intensive domains. --- **Q-B.(b)** Discuss the **CAP theorem** positioning of graph stores. Illustrate with the example of **Neo4j** and explain its consistency model. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **CAP Theorem Recap:** In any distributed data store, only **two of the three** guarantees — **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition Tolerance (P)** — can be fully satisfied at the same time during a network partition. **Positioning of Graph Stores:** Most native graph databases (Neo4j, JanusGraph with Cassandra backend, ArangoDB) lean towards the **CP side**: * **Consistency (C):** They prioritize strong consistency so that traversals yield semantically correct results. * **Partition Tolerance (P):** They support distributed deployments across partitions. * **Availability (A):** During a partition, they may reduce availability to preserve consistency (leader-based or quorum-based writes). **Neo4j Specifics:** * **Single-node / Causal Cluster mode:** Uses a **leader-follower** (core-replica) architecture. * **Raft consensus** is used to replicate the Raft log across core servers. * **Read-your-writes consistency** is guaranteed when clients talk to the leader. * **Write path:** A transaction is committed on a quorum (majority) of core servers before being acknowledged. * **Each transaction** is fully **ACID** with **write-ahead logging (WAL)** and **deadlock detection**. **Consistency Model:** * Neo4j provides **causal consistency**: once a write is acknowledged, all subsequent reads (from the same session) observe that write. * It does **not** offer eventual consistency — this is a deliberate design choice because eventual consistency is unsuitable for graph traversals (a stale neighbour breaks the chain). * During a network partition, the cluster may become **read-only** on the minority side rather than serve stale or partial data. **Comparison with other NoSQL stores:** | Database | CAP Position | Consistency Model | |----------|--------------|-------------------| | **Neo4j (Causal Cluster)** | CP | Causal consistency (ACID) | | **MongoDB (Replica Set)** | CP (default) | Tunable causal / linearizable | | **Cassandra** | **AP** | Eventual / tunable consistency | | **Redis Cluster** | **AP** | Eventual (asynchronous replication) | | **Amazon Neptune** | **AP** (read replicas) | Eventual for replicas | **Conclusion:** Graph stores trade off availability for strong consistency — a justified trade-off because their core value proposition is **trustworthy, relationship-correct traversals**, which would be undermined by stale or missing edges. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS on Graph Store questions:** > > 1. **Confusing Property Graphs with RDF Graphs** — Property graphs (Neo4j) use labelled nodes/typed edges with properties; RDF uses subject-predicate-object triples. They are *not* interchangeable. > 2. **Forgetting the third component (Properties)** — Many students write only "nodes and edges," losing 1–2 marks. Always mention the **triple $(V, E, P)$**. > 3. **Misquoting complexity** — Writing "$O(n)$ for traversal" is wrong. Per-hop cost is **$O(1)$**; $h$-hop is **$O(k^h)$**. Examiners deduct marks for sloppy complexity statements. > 4. **Citing outdated CAP positions** — Neo4j 4.x+ is now **CP with Raft**; do *not* write "Neo4j is AP" (this is a common 2014-era mistake). > 5. **Skipping the analogy/justification** — When asked "why graph stores?", simply saying "they store data as a graph" is insufficient. Mention **index-free adjacency**, **O(1) pointer hops**, and **first-class relationships**. > 6. **Spelling "Cypher" as "Cipher"** — Trivial but examiners do deduct. > 7. **Mixing up BFS/DFS applicability** — BFS finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs; DFS is used for cycle detection, topological sort. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Graph Stores (Module 4)** * **Definition:** A NoSQL store that uses a graph $(V, E, P)$ as its core data model; relationships are *first-class*. * **Three core components:** **Nodes (vertices)**, **Edges (relationships)**, **Properties (key-value attributes)**. * **Property Graph model** = labels on nodes + types on edges + properties on both. * **RDF Graph model** = subject–predicate–object triples, queried via **SPARQL**. * **Index-Free Adjacency (IFA)** = each node stores direct pointers to its edges → O(1) per-hop traversal. * **Adjacency Matrix storage** = $O(n^2)$; **Adjacency List storage** = $O(n + m)$. * **$h$-hop traversal cost** = $O(k^h)$ where $k$ = average node degree. * **CAP positioning** of Neo4j = **CP** (causal consistency via Raft), with full **ACID** transactions. * **Popular graph stores:** **Neo4j**, **Amazon Neptune**, **JanusGraph**, **ArangoDB**, **TigerGraph**, **OrientDB**. * **Query languages:** **Cypher** (Neo4j), **Gremlin** (TinkerPop), **SPARQL** (RDF), **GQL** (ISO/IEC 39075:2024). * **Key applications:** Social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs, recommendation engines, network/IT operations, bioinformatics, IAM. * **Graph metrics to memorize:** $\bar{d} = 2m/n$, $\rho = 2m / n(n-1)$, $S_{\text{matrix}} = O(n^2)$, $S_{\text{list}} = O(n+m)$. * **Native graph advantage:** O(1) per-hop cost vs. $O(n^2)$ JOIN cost in RDBMS for connected data. * **BFS / DFS:** Both $O(n+m)$ time complexity. BFS for shortest path; DFS for cycle / component detection. * **NoSQL CAP matrix recall:** Neo4j = CP; Cassandra = AP; Redis = AP; MongoDB = CP (default). * **Six-degrees of separation:** Empirical observation that social graph average path length is $\le 6$ hops.

topics.title: Column Family Stores and Document Stores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT404 - DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Column Family Stores and Document Stores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The NoSQL Movement — Context for the Topic

Before diving into Column Family Stores and Document Stores, we must understand their place in the database ecosystem. The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4 — *NoSQL Databases*) classifies these systems under the broader umbrella of **NoSQL (Not Only SQL)** databases — distributed, non-relational data stores engineered for horizontal scalability, schema flexibility, and Big Data workloads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Module 4):** *NoSQL databases* are a class of data management systems designed to handle large volumes of structured, semi-structured, or unstructured data by relaxing the strict ACID relational model in favour of **BASE properties** (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency).

The four canonical NoSQL families recognized in the KTU syllabus are:

| NoSQL Family | KTU Canonical Example | Data Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Key-Value Stores** | Redis, DynamoDB | Opaque (key, value) |
| **Column Family Stores** | Apache Cassandra, HBase | Rows of column families |
| **Document Stores** | MongoDB, CouchDB | JSON / BSON documents |
| **Graph Databases** | Neo4j | Nodes, edges, properties |

This note covers the second and third rows of that table.

---

### 1.2 Column Family Stores — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Column-Oriented / Column-Family Store):**
> A **Column Family Store** is a NoSQL data model that organizes data into **rows** identified by a unique **row key**, where each row consists of a sparse, dynamic collection of **columns** grouped into a fixed number of **column families**. It is *not* simply "storing columns instead of rows" — rather, it groups related columns that are typically accessed together, enabling efficient disk seeks and compression on column-wise data blocks.

The two best-known KTU-referenced implementations are:

- **Apache Cassandra** — masterless, peer-to-peer ring topology, AP-oriented (Brewer's CAP).
- **Apache HBase** — master-slave architecture over HDFS, strongly consistent (CP-oriented).

**Conceptual Analogy — The Spreadsheet Filing Cabinet:**
Imagine a hospital that maintains one filing cabinet *per patient* (the row). Inside each patient's folder, instead of one giant pre-printed form with 200 fields, the doctor only files documents that are actually relevant (e.g., "X-Ray Reports", "Blood Test History", "Allergy Notes"). The cabinet has labelled **drawers** (column families like *Diagnostics*, *Prescriptions*). Each drawer holds loose **sheets** (columns) that can be added at any time, even years after the patient was first registered. Two patients can have completely different sets of sheets. This is the essence of a column family store: **sparse, schema-flexible rows with grouped, frequently co-accessed columns.**

---

### 1.3 Document Stores — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Document Store):**
> A **Document Store** is a NoSQL data model that stores semi-structured data as **self-describing documents** (typically encoded in **JSON**, **BSON**, or **XML**), grouped into **collections**. Documents in the same collection may have varying schemas, and they are addressable by a unique **document key** (often a UUID or auto-generated `_id`).

The KTU-canonical implementation is **MongoDB**, with **CouchDB** as a frequent secondary reference.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Recipe Binder:**
Think of a *Document Store* as a recipe binder. Each recipe is on its own index card (the **document**). The card contains the title, ingredients, steps, prep time, and (optionally) a photo and a list of tags — all in one self-contained piece of paper. Some cards are for "Biriyani", some for "Pasta", some for "Payasam". The cook (the *application*) can add a new field like "spice level" only to the Biriyani card; the Pasta card does not need to know about it. All Biriyani-style cards are clipped together in one section of the binder (the **collection**). The whole binder is the **database**. You can flip to a card directly using its recipe name (the **document key**) instead of reading every card sequentially.

---

### 1.4 Why These Two Families? The CAP-Theorem Lens

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The choice between a Column Family Store and a Document Store is fundamentally a **CAP-theorem** and **access-pattern** decision. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why would you pick Cassandra over MongoDB for time-series IoT data?"* The answer hinges on write-throughput, partitioning, and the read-modify-write cost of hierarchical documents.

**Standard Metrics Used in KTU Module 4 Solutions:**

- **Write latency (Cassandra):** Sub-millisecond, often cited as **< 1 ms** at p99.
- **Read latency (MongoDB indexed):** Single-digit milliseconds, typically **< 10 ms**.
- **Typical horizontal scale:** Both scale to **petabytes (PB)** and **thousands of nodes**.

---

### 1.5 At-a-Glance Distinction Table (KTU Favorite)

| Property | Column Family Store | Document Store |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data Unit** | Row of column families | Document (JSON/BSON) |
| **Schema** | Sparse, dynamic per row | Schema-less per document |
| **Primary Access Pattern** | Range scans on row key; OLAP-style column reads | Whole-document fetch; ad hoc field queries |
| **Indexing** | Only on row key (native); secondary indexes limited | Rich secondary indexes on any field |
| **Transactions** | Single-row atomicity (Cassandra LWT) | Multi-document ACID in MongoDB 4.0+ |
| **Best For (KTU)** | Time-series, IoT, messaging, recommendations | Catalogs, content management, user profiles |
| **Canonical Product** | Apache Cassandra, HBase | MongoDB, CouchDB |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Column Family Stores — Anatomy of the Data Model

The data model is hierarchical. Each layer has a precise KTU-exam-friendly name.

**Layer 1 — Keyspace**
The top-level namespace (analogous to a *database* in SQL). Defines the **replication strategy** and **replication factor**.

**Layer 2 — Column Family**
A grouping of related columns that are typically retrieved together. Two flavours exist:

- **Static Column Family:** Fixed schema defined at keyspace creation (older HBase style).
- **Dynamic Column Family:** Columns can be added at write time (Cassandra style — the KTU-canonical model).

**Layer 3 — Row**
Identified by a unique **row key** (string, integer, or composite). All data for one row is stored physically together or close together on disk.

**Layer 4 — Column**
The atomic data unit, represented as a triplet:

$$
\text{Column} = (\text{column\_name}, \text{value}, \text{timestamp})
$$

The **timestamp** enables conflict resolution in eventually-consistent systems (last-write-wins, governed by *snitches* and clocks).

**Layer 5 — Super Column (Optional, HBase / older Cassandra)**
A column whose value is itself a map of sub-columns. KTU 2024 has downplayed super columns in favour of *composite columns* and *clustering columns*.

---

### 2.2 Cassandra — The Architecture (Why It Matters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Cassandra uses a **gossip protocol** for peer-to-peer cluster membership and a **consistent hashing ring** for data partitioning. There is **no single point of failure** — the "masterless" architecture is a frequent 7-mark question.

**How a write happens in Cassandra (logical steps):**

1. Client sends a write to any node in the ring (the *coordinator node*).
2. The coordinator computes the **partition key hash** and identifies the *replica nodes* using the configured replication strategy (e.g., `NetworkTopologyStrategy`).
3. The write is appended to a **commit log** on each replica (durability).
4. The write is written to an in-memory **memtable**.
5. When the memtable is full, it is flushed to an immutable **SSTable (Sorted String Table)** on disk.
6. Periodic **compaction** merges SSTables to reclaim space and tombstones.

**Consistency Level (CL) — KTU 2024 Exam Favorite:**

The client sets a **consistency level** per query:

- `ONE` — wait for 1 replica (fastest, weakest).
- `QUORUM` — wait for a strict majority, i.e., $N/2 + 1$ replicas, where $N$ is the replication factor.
- `ALL` — wait for every replica (slowest, strongest).

**The Quorum Math (mandatory KTU derivation):**

$$
\text{Quorum Write} = \text{CL} = \left\lfloor \frac{RF}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

where **RF** is the *replication factor* (e.g., RF = 3 ⇒ quorum = 2). To guarantee strong consistency, the **read and write quorums** must overlap:

$$
R + W > RF
$$

This is the famous **R + W > N** consistency rule in Dynamo-style stores. KTU examiners commonly give RF = 3 and ask "what CL pair guarantees strong consistency?" — the answer: any $(R, W)$ such that $R + W \ge 4$, e.g., $R=2, W=2$ (quorum reads and writes).

---

### 2.3 Document Stores — Anatomy of the Data Model (MongoDB)

**Layer 1 — Database**
The physical container (one database per application on a server).

**Layer 2 — Collection**
A group of documents. In MongoDB, a collection is *not* required to enforce a uniform schema — documents inside may differ in fields.

**Layer 3 — Document**
The atomic unit. Maximum size in BSON is **16 MB** (a hard KTU favourite). Represented as a JSON-like structure:

```json
{
  "_id": "ObjectId('65a1b2c3d4e5f6...')",
  "name": "Kerala Biriyani",
  "course": "main",
  "ingredients": ["rice", "chicken", "masala"],
  "prep_time_min": 60,
  "tags": ["spicy", "non-veg"]
}
```

**Layer 4 — Field**
A key-value pair inside a document. Values may be primitives, arrays, or **embedded documents** (sub-documents).

**Layer 5 — Embedded Document vs Reference**
A document may either *embed* a related sub-document (denormalization) or store a *reference* (`ObjectId` of another document), which is later resolved via a `$lookup` join in the aggregation pipeline. KTU 2024 emphasizes the *embedding vs referencing* trade-off.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations, constraints, and unit metrics required for the KTU 2024 Module 4 question paper on this topic are consolidated here.

| Concept | Equation / Metric | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cassandra Quorum** | $Q = \lfloor RF/2 \rfloor + 1$ | $Q$ is minimum replicas acknowledged |
| **Strong Consistency Rule** | $R + W > RF$ | $R$=read replicas, $W$=write replicas |
| **Consistent Hashing Replicas** | $N_{\text{replicas}} = RF$ | Per partition key |
| **Partitions on a Node** | $P_{\text{node}} \approx N_{\text{partitions}} \cdot (RF / N_{\text{nodes}})$ | Approximation, uniform hashing assumed |
| **Cassandra Write Latency Target** | $L_{w} < 1 \text{ ms}$ (p99) | Engineering guideline, not a formula |
| **MongoDB Document Max Size** | $S_{\text{doc}} \le 16 \text{ MB}$ | Hard BSON limit |
| **MongoDB Nested Array Limit** | Depth $\le 100$ | Server-enforced |
| **Sharding Chunk Size (MongoDB)** | Default 64 MB, range 1 MB – 1024 MB | Tunable |
| **Bloom Filter False Positive** | $P_{fp} \approx (1 - e^{-k n / m})^{k}$ | $k$=hash fns, $n$=items, $m$=bits |
| **Compaction Throughput** | $T = V_{sstable} / t_{compact}$ | MB/s, system metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety Note:** In the above table, all absolute-value and conditional symbols are written using `\vert` and `\lfloor \rfloor` to prevent markdown table-rendering breakage. Do not type the literal pipe character `|` inside table cells when writing KTU answer sheets in markdown.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Column Family Stores (Cassandra):** Used by Netflix for view-state history, by Instagram for direct-message inboxes, and by Apple for iMessage metadata — anywhere **write-heavy** workloads with **predictable query patterns** dominate.
- **Document Stores (MongoDB):** Used by eBay for catalog metadata, by MetLife for insurance policy management, and by the City of Chicago for the *Divvy* bike-share portal — anywhere **rich, hierarchical, evolving schemas** are the norm.

The engineering utility is *not* "any system can use any database". The KTU 2024 outcome CO4 explicitly requires the engineer to **select** the appropriate NoSQL family for a given workload.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are *worked examples* that the KTU examiner is most likely to set, given the syllabus weightage of this topic in the **2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination)**. Each step is fully written out — no shortcuts.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quorum and Strong-Consistency Math (Cassandra)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 7-mark):**
A Cassandra cluster is configured with a *replication factor* $RF = 5$. A client issues a write at consistency level `QUORUM` and a read at `QUORUM`. Determine the read and write quorum sizes, and prove whether the configuration guarantees strong consistency.

**Step 1 — Define the replication factor.**

The replication factor is given as $RF = 5$. This means every partition key (row) is replicated on $5$ distinct nodes in the cluster.

**Step 2 — Apply the quorum formula.**

The quorum size $Q$ is defined as the smallest integer strictly greater than half of the replicas:

$$
Q = \left\lfloor \frac{RF}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

Substituting $RF = 5$:

$$
Q = \left\lfloor \frac{5}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 2.5 \rfloor + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

So the **write quorum** is $W = 3$ and the **read quorum** is $R = 3$.

**Step 3 — Apply the strong-consistency rule.**

For linearizable (strong) consistency, the read and write quorums must overlap by at least one node. Mathematically:

$$
R + W > RF
$$

Substituting $R = 3$, $W = 3$, $RF = 5$:

$$
R + W = 3 + 3 = 6 \quad \text{and} \quad RF = 5
$$

$$
\therefore 6 > 5 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

The inequality holds; therefore, at least one node is guaranteed to participate in *both* the read and the write. This guarantees that the most recent write is always reflected in any subsequent read, satisfying *linearizability* under the assumption of non-byzantine clocks.

**Step 5 — Edge case the KTU examiner tests.**

Suppose the same cluster uses `R = ONE` and `W = QUORUM`:

$$
R + W = 1 + 3 = 4 \quad < \quad 5 = RF
$$

In this case, the read may hit a stale replica that did not participate in the quorum write, and the system is *not* strongly consistent. The KTU mark is lost if you forget to comment on this contrapositive.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cassandra Partition Distribution on a Ring

**Problem Statement:**
A Cassandra cluster has $N = 8$ nodes and $RF = 3$. The cluster owns $P = 1024$ virtual nodes (vnodes) uniformly distributed. Compute the **expected** number of partitions stored on a single node.

**Step 1 — Total replica copies in the system.**

Each of the $P$ partition keys is stored on $RF$ nodes, so the total stored replicas are:

$$
R_{\text{total}} = P \cdot RF = 1024 \cdot 3 = 3072
$$

**Step 2 — Uniform distribution assumption.**

Under the consistent-hashing-with-vnodes model and a uniform distribution, the expected partitions per node are:

$$
R_{\text{node}} = \frac{R_{\text{total}}}{N} = \frac{P \cdot RF}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate.**

$$
R_{\text{node}} = \frac{1024 \cdot 3}{8} = \frac{3072}{8} = 384
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A typical node will own 384 partition replicas. KTU follow-up: if one node fails, the cluster loses $\approx 12.5\%$ of its capacity; load must be re-distributed, and read-repair or hinted-handoff kicks in.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bloom Filter False-Positive Probability (Cassandra SSTables)

**Problem Statement:**
A Cassandra SSTable uses a Bloom filter of size $m = 10$ bits per element, with $k = 3$ hash functions, containing $n = 100$ SSTable keys. Estimate the probability of a *false positive* on a lookup miss.

**Step 1 — Recall the Bloom filter false-positive formula.**

The probability that a given bit is *still zero* after inserting $n$ items with $k$ hash functions is:

$$
p_{\text{zero}} = \left( 1 - \frac{1}{m} \right)^{k n} \approx e^{-kn/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability of false positive.**

A false positive occurs when all $k$ hashed positions are *already set to 1* by prior insertions. Hence:

$$
P_{fp} = (1 - p_{\text{zero}})^{k} \approx \left( 1 - e^{-kn/m} \right)^{k}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug in $k=3$, $n=100$, $m=10$ (i.e., $m$ scaled per-element so $m_{\text{total}} = 1000$ bits).**

$$
\frac{kn}{m} = \frac{3 \cdot 100}{1000} = 0.3
$$

$$
e^{-0.3} \approx 0.7408
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the false-positive probability.**

$$
P_{fp} \approx (1 - 0.7408)^{3} = (0.2592)^{3} \approx 0.0174
$$

So $P_{fp} \approx 1.74\%$. The KTU point: increasing $k$ or $m$ lowers $P_{fp}$ at the cost of memory and CPU. Cassandra tunes $P_{fp}$ to a default of $\mathbf{1\%}$ for performance.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following analytical matrix is a **high-yield study aid** for the 14-mark KTU questions. The examiner expects you to reason through the *trade-offs*, not just name a product.

| Application Workload | Preferred NoSQL Family | Justification (KTU-Style) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time-series sensor data (millions of writes/sec) | **Column Family** (Cassandra) | High write throughput, append-only pattern, tunable CL |
| Product catalog with rich, evolving attributes | **Document** (MongoDB) | Flexible per-product schema, rich indexing |
| User session cache | **Key-Value** (Redis) | Out of scope here; sub-millisecond GET/SET |
| Social-network friend graph | **Graph** (Neo4j) | Out of scope here; native traversal primitives |
| Messaging / Inbox (write-heavy, range scans) | **Column Family** (Cassandra) | Time-ordered row keys, partition by user_id |
| Content Management System (CMS) | **Document** (MongoDB) | Documents map naturally to articles / pages |
| Audit log (append-only, time-bucket queries) | **Column Family** (HBase) | Sparse wide rows over HDFS, MapReduce friendly |
| Mobile-app user profile store | **Document** (MongoDB) | Variable fields, simple CRUD, easy sharding |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — MongoDB Embedding vs Referencing

**Scenario:** Design a MongoDB schema for a *blog* where each blog has many *comments*, and comments are always loaded with the blog post.

**Step 1 — Embedding Approach (denormalized).**

Embed the comments as a sub-array:

```json
{
  "_id": 1,
  "title": "KTU Notes",
  "comments": [
    { "user": "Anu", "text": "Great!" },
    { "user": "Rahul", "text": "Helpful" }
  ]
}
$$

**Step 2 — Referencing Approach (normalized).**

Store comments in a separate collection with a `post_id` reference:

```json
{ "_id": 1, "title": "KTU Notes" }
{ "_id": 101, "post_id": 1, "user": "Anu", "text": "Great!" }
{ "_id": 102, "post_id": 1, "user": "Rahul", "text": "Helpful" }
$$

**Step 3 — KTU Decision Rule.**

Use **embedding** when:

- The child entity is *always* read with the parent.
- The child array does not grow unbounded (16 MB document cap).
- 1-to-few or 1-to-1 relationships dominate.

Use **referencing** when:

- The child entity is read independently.
- Many-to-many relationships are involved.
- The array could exceed the 16 MB BSON limit.

For a blog, **embedding comments** is the KTU-preferred answer *if* the comment count is bounded; otherwise reference.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams in this section are designed to be KTU-board-renderable: clean ASCII/Mermaid block diagrams that the student can redraw by hand in the answer booklet. Mermaid syntax follows the engine safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs only, no markdown inside labels).

### 4.1 Architecture of a Cassandra Cluster (Masterless Ring)

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1[Client App] --> N1[Node 1 Coordinator]
    C2[Client App 2] --> N3[Node 3 Coordinator]
    C3[Client App 3] --> N6[Node 6 Coordinator]
    N1 -- gossip --> N2
    N1 -- gossip --> N3
    N2 -- gossip --> N4
    N3 -- gossip --> N5
    N4 -- gossip --> N6
    N5 -- gossip --> N7
    N6 -- gossip --> N8
    N7 -- gossip --> N8
    N1 -- replica write --> N2
    N1 -- replica write --> N4
    N3 -- replica write --> N5
    N3 -- replica write --> N7
    N6 -- replica write --> N8
    N6 -- replica write --> N2
```

**Reading the Diagram (KTU 14-mark style):**
- The graph is a logical ring, but for clarity it is drawn as a mesh.
- Each node can act as a **coordinator** for a write or read — no master.
- The `gossip` arrows show the inter-node heartbeat / cluster-membership protocol.
- The `replica write` arrows show that for a partition key with $RF = 3$, the coordinator hands the data to 2 additional replicas (Cassandra itself counts as one).

---

### 4.2 Column Family Data Model — Conceptual Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    KS[Keyspace KTU_DB] --> CF1[Column Family Patient_Info]
    KS --> CF2[Column Family Diagnostics]
    CF1 --> R1[Row patient_id 101]
    CF1 --> R2[Row patient_id 102]
    R1 --> C1A[Column name: 'Anu' value: 'Anu Lakshmi' ts: 100]
    R1 --> C1B[Column name: 'dob' value: '2001-05-12' ts: 105]
    R1 --> C1C[Column name: 'city' value: 'Kochi' ts: 110]
    R2 --> C2A[Column name: 'name' value: 'Rahul' ts: 102]
    R2 --> C2B[Column name: 'city' value: 'Trivandrum' ts: 108]
    CF2 --> R3[Row patient_id 101]
    R3 --> C3A[Column bp value: 120_80 ts: 200]
    R3 --> C3B[Column sugar value: 95 ts: 205]
    R3 --> C3C[Column xray value: normal ts: 210]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- `Keyspace` is the top-level container, equivalent to a database.
- `Column Family` groups related columns, much like a table — but a row can have a *sparse* set of columns.
- Notice that `Row patient_id 102` in `Patient_Info` does *not* have a `dob` column; this is the **sparse** property KTU tests.

---

### 4.3 MongoDB Document Model — Hierarchy and Storage

```mermaid
graph TD
    DB[MongoDB Database university] --> COL1[Collection students]
    DB --> COL2[Collection courses]
    COL1 --> D1[Document id 1]
    COL1 --> D2[Document id 2]
    D1 --> F1[Field name: 'Anu']
    D1 --> F2[Field age: 21]
    D1 --> F3[Field skills array: MongoDB, Cassandra]
    D1 --> F4[Field address subdoc: city, state]
    D2 --> F5[Field name: 'Rahul']
    D2 --> F6[Field age: 22]
    D2 --> F7[Field skills array: Python, SQL]
    F4 --> F4A[Field city: 'Kochi']
    F4 --> F4B[Field state: 'Kerala']
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- A *Database* contains *Collections*, which contain *Documents*, which contain *Fields* (primitives, arrays, or sub-documents).
- The diagram emphasizes **schema flexibility**: Document 2 omits the `address` field, which is permissible.

---

### 4.4 Functional Flow — Cassandra Write Path

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Client sends write to coordinator] --> S2[Step 2: Hash partition key and locate replicas]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Append to commit log on each replica]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Write to memtable in memory]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Memtable flushes to immutable SSTable on disk]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Periodic compaction reclaims space]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Acknowledgement sent to client at configured CL]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Steps 1–2 happen on the *coordinator*.
- Steps 3–5 happen on every *replica* node that owns the partition.
- Step 6 runs in the background, not in the critical write path.
- Step 7 is the moment the client sees a successful write — controlled by the **consistency level**.

---

### 4.5 Cassandra vs MongoDB — Side-by-Side Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Cassandra Block
        CA[Coordinator Node] --> CB[Partition Key Hash]
        CB --> CC[Replica Node Set]
        CC --> CD[Commit Log]
        CC --> CE[Memtable]
        CE --> CF[SSTable on Disk]
    end
    subgraph MongoDB Block
        MA[Mongos Router] --> MB[Config Server Cluster]
        MA --> MC[Shard 1]
        MA --> MD[Shard 2]
        MA --> ME[Shard 3]
        MC --> MF[Primary]
        MC --> MG[Secondary]
    end
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The left block (Cassandra) shows a **peer-to-peer** model — every node is symmetric.
- The right block (MongoDB) shows a **sharded cluster** with a routing tier (`mongos`) and a metadata tier (config servers).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This question bank strictly follows the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination)** pattern. Part A = 3 marks each. Part B = 14 marks each, with **internal choice** (you attempt either Question A or Question B). Bloom's cognitive levels are tagged for KTU outcome alignment.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
*Compare the data model of a Column Family Store (e.g., Cassandra) with that of a Document Store (e.g., MongoDB). Highlight any two structural differences.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Column Family Store** organizes data as rows of column families, where each row is keyed and each column family groups related columns; rows are *sparse* and can have varying column sets, but the column family names are typically fixed at the keyspace level. A **Document Store** organizes data as *self-describing JSON/BSON documents* grouped into collections, where each document can have a *completely independent* schema.

Two structural differences:

1. **Granularity of the data unit** — In Cassandra, the row is a thin set of columns; in MongoDB, the document can contain nested sub-documents and arrays, forming a tree.
2. **Schema strictness** — Cassandra defines column families at keyspace creation (column family names are fixed), though columns inside can be added dynamically. MongoDB imposes *no* structural schema on a collection.

**Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*What is the role of a Bloom filter in Cassandra's read path? Why is it considered a probabilistic structure?*

**Model Answer:**

A **Bloom filter** is a space-efficient probabilistic data structure maintained in-memory for each **SSTable** in Cassandra. On a read, the Bloom filter is first consulted to determine whether a given row key *might* exist in that SSTable. If the Bloom filter returns "definitely no", the entire SSTable can be skipped without any disk I/O, saving substantial latency.

It is called *probabilistic* because:

- It can return a **false positive** — saying a key *might* exist when it does not, costing an extra disk seek.
- It can *never* return a **false negative** — if the filter says the key is absent, the key is truly absent.

The trade-off is tunable: more bits per element lower the false-positive rate at the cost of memory. Cassandra's default targets a $1\%$ false-positive probability.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - When asked "explain the Cassandra write path", students often *omit the commit log* and lose 2 marks. Always include: *commit log → memtable → SSTable → compaction*.
> - When asked "compare Column Family and Document stores", do **not** just list bullet points. You must include the **engineering reason** (write-heavy vs schema-rich) for each choice.
> - For MongoDB questions, the **16 MB document limit** and the **embedding vs referencing** trade-off are worth 2 marks each — do not forget them.
> - Quorum questions are marked using the **R + W > N** rule; you must derive the formula, not just state it.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, describe the **architecture of Apache Cassandra**. Explain the role of the **gossip protocol** and **consistent hashing** in achieving scalability and fault tolerance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Cassandra cluster is configured with **Replication Factor (RF) = 5**. For the following three configurations, determine whether strong consistency is guaranteed and justify your answer using the *R + W > RF* rule. **(7 marks)**

- Case (i): Read CL = `QUORUM`, Write CL = `QUORUM`.
- Case (ii): Read CL = `ONE`, Write CL = `ALL`.
- Case (iii): Read CL = `ALL`, Write CL = `ONE`.

---

**Model Solution for Question A (a):**

The architecture of **Apache Cassandra** is a **peer-to-peer ring** of nodes, with no single point of failure. Every node is identical in capability; any node can act as a *coordinator* for a client request.

**Diagram (reproduced from Section 4.1 for completeness):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    CL[Client] --> CO[Coordinator Node]
    CO --> H[Hash Partition Key]
    H --> R1[Replica Node 1]
    H --> R2[Replica Node 2]
    H --> R3[Replica Node 3]
    R1 --> G1[gossip]
    R2 --> G2[gossip]
    R3 --> G3[gossip]
```

**Role of Consistent Hashing:**
Cassandra uses **consistent hashing** to map partition keys to nodes. Each node is assigned a *token* on a hash ring; the partition key's hash determines its owning node (and replicas are placed on the next $RF - 1$ nodes clockwise). This gives:

- **Horizontal scalability** — adding a node only re-distributes a small fraction of keys.
- **Decentralized partition management** — no central lookup table.
- **Locality-aware replica placement** — using a *snitch* to prefer replicas in the same data centre.

**Role of the Gossip Protocol:**
The **gossip protocol** is a peer-to-peer heartbeat exchanged every second between nodes. It serves to:

- Disseminate **cluster membership** information (node up, node down, node joining).
- Propagate **schema changes** and **token metadata**.
- Enable **failure detection** — if a node misses too many gossips, it is marked *down* and replicas are re-routed.

**Fault tolerance and scalability** therefore emerge from the *combination* of consistent hashing (data placement) and gossip (cluster awareness), with no master, no SPOF, and linear horizontal scaling.

---

**Model Solution for Question A (b):**

Given: $RF = 5$.

Compute the quorum size:

$$
Q = \left\lfloor \frac{5}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 3
$$

**Case (i): $R = 3$, $W = 3$.**

$$
R + W = 6 > 5 = RF
$$

Therefore, the read and write quorums overlap on at least one node, and the system is **strongly consistent**.

**Case (ii): $R = 1$, $W = 5$.**

$$
R + W = 1 + 5 = 6 > 5 = RF
$$

Therefore, the inequality is also satisfied — the configuration is **strongly consistent**. (Note: it is slow on writes because every replica must acknowledge, but the consistency guarantee holds.)

**Case (iii): $R = 5$, $W = 1$.**

$$
R + W = 5 + 1 = 6 > 5 = RF
$$

The inequality holds, so technically the read sees the *latest* write among 5 replicas. However, the practical caveat: a read at `ALL` is expensive and the write at `ONE` is fast. The configuration is **strongly consistent** *if* the write actually reaches the read set, which `R = 5, W = 1` does not guarantee (the write may not have propagated to all replicas by read time). The KTU examiner expects you to note this nuance for full marks.

**Pedagogical Note:** For the *guarantee* to hold, the inequality alone is necessary; in practice one also needs the write to have been propagated before the read begins, which is enforced by a strict consistency level on both sides. The strict, safe KTU answer is: Cases (i) and (ii) are strongly consistent; Case (iii) is borderline because $W = 1$ does not imply quorum write propagation.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **data model of a Document Store** (MongoDB) with a suitable example document. Discuss the **16 MB BSON limit** and its implications on schema design — specifically, when to use **embedding** versus **referencing** of related entities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Apache Cassandra** and **MongoDB** along the following dimensions, providing a reasoned justification for each: (i) data model, (ii) consistency model, (iii) query language, (iv) best-fit workload. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B (a):**

The MongoDB **data model** is hierarchical:

- A *database* contains *collections* of *documents*.
- A *document* is a JSON-like structure, stored in binary form as **BSON**, with a maximum size of **16 MB**.
- A *field* may be a primitive, an array, or an *embedded sub-document*.

**Example document:**

```json
{
  "_id": "ObjectId('507f1f77bcf86cd799439011')",
  "name": "KTU Database Systems",
  "credits": 4,
  "topics": ["ER Model", "Normalization", "NoSQL", "Transactions"],
  "instructor": {
    "name": "Dr. Anu",
    "email": "anu@ktu.edu",
    "office": "Block C, Room 204"
  }
}
$$

**The 16 MB BSON limit and its implications:**

Every MongoDB document is bounded at 16 MB. This is a *hard server-enforced limit*. Practical implications:

1. **Large media (images, videos, PDFs) cannot be embedded** — they must be stored in **GridFS** or an external object store (e.g., S3) and only the URL stored in the document.
2. **Unbounded arrays are dangerous** — if `comments` grows without bound, you may hit the 16 MB ceiling, and MongoDB will refuse the write.
3. **Embedding vs Referencing trade-off:**

| Scenario | Recommended Approach | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Blog with 1-to-few comments (say, < 1000) | **Embedding** | Always read with parent; bounded array |
| User with 1-to-many devices (thousands) | **Referencing** | Unbounded; would exceed 16 MB |
| Order with 1-to-few line items | **Embedding** | Natural ownership; transactional read |
| Students with many-to-many courses | **Referencing** | Many-to-many, requires `$lookup` join |
| Author profile with one address | **Embedding** | Sub-document, one-to-one |

**KTU takeaway:** Prefer embedding for *1-to-1* and *1-to-few*; use referencing for *1-to-many* (unbounded) and *many-to-many* (M:N).

---

**Model Solution for Question B (b):**

| Dimension | Apache Cassandra | MongoDB | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Data Model** | Row of column families; sparse wide rows; row key + column families | JSON/BSON documents in collections; nested sub-documents | Cassandra optimizes for *time-series* and *wide rows*; MongoDB optimizes for *rich hierarchical* objects |
| **(ii) Consistency Model** | Eventual by default, tunable per query (`ONE`, `QUORUM`, `ALL`); AP-leaning | Tunable in 4.0+ with multi-document ACID transactions; CP-leaning with majority write concern | Cassandra sacrifices strong consistency for availability under partition; MongoDB now offers causal consistency in replica sets |
| **(iii) Query Language** | **CQL (Cassandra Query Language)** — SQL-like but no joins, no subqueries; partition key mandatory in `WHERE` | **MQL (MongoDB Query Language)** + Aggregation Pipeline; rich `$match`, `$group`, `$lookup` | CQL is intentionally restricted to enforce *query-first schema design*; MQL is more expressive but allows anti-patterns |
| **(iv) Best-Fit Workload** | Write-heavy time-series, IoT, messaging, recommendation engines; millions of writes per second | Content management, catalogs, user profiles, mobile backends; variable schema with rich querying | Cassandra's append-only SSTable model dominates writes; MongoDB's flexible document tree dominates read-modify-write on rich objects |

**Synthesizing answer (for the 14-mark wrap-up):**
- Choose **Cassandra** when the *query pattern is known at schema design time* and writes dominate.
- Choose **MongoDB** when the *schema is evolving* and reads require *rich secondary indexes* on arbitrary fields.

This is the **CO4 (Module Outcome)** KTU 2024 expects: *apply appropriate NoSQL data models to given problem statements*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 (Column Family & Document Stores):**

- **Column Family Store** = rows of *column families*; rows are sparse; data unit is the **column** = (name, value, timestamp).
- **Document Store** = *collections of JSON/BSON documents*; schemaless; maximum document size is **16 MB**.
- **Cassandra architecture** = masterless ring, gossip protocol, consistent hashing, no SPOF.
- **Write path** = `commit log → memtable → SSTable → compaction` — must appear in any 7-mark answer.
- **Bloom filter** = probabilistic structure in SSTable, used to *avoid disk seeks*; never gives false negatives.
- **Quorum formula** = $Q = \lfloor RF/2 \rfloor + 1$.
- **Strong consistency rule** = $R + W > RF$ (Cassandra/Dynamo-style stores).
- **Tunable consistency levels** in Cassandra: `ONE`, `QUORUM`, `ALL` — write `W`, read `R`, configured per query.
- **MongoDB limits** = 16 MB document, 100 levels of nesting, default chunk size 64 MB.
- **Embedding** is preferred for *1-to-1* and *1-to-few* bounded relationships; **referencing** is preferred for *1-to-many unbounded* and *many-to-many*.
- **CQL** is SQL-like but *no joins, no subqueries* — partition key is mandatory in `WHERE`.
- **MQL** supports rich aggregation pipelines with `$match`, `$group`, `$project`, `$lookup`.
- **Decision rule** = write-heavy, schema-known, range-scans → **Cassandra**; schema-evolving, rich-query, hierarchical → **MongoDB**.
- **CAP-theorem placement** = Cassandra is AP-leaning, MongoDB is CP-leaning (with replica-set majority write concern).
- **Real-world anchors** = Netflix, Instagram, Apple (Cassandra); eBay, MetLife, City of Chicago (MongoDB) — be ready to cite one example each in the 14-mark answer.
- **Avoid the KTU pitfall** = always state the *engineering reason* alongside the product name; never just list features.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Topics: Suggestions and Sample Project Topics (Library Management System, E-commerce Product Catalogue)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT404 - DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Topics: Suggestions and Sample Project Topics (Library Management System, E-commerce Product Catalogue)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 4 — Project Topics: Suggestions and Sample Project Topics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Database Project (KTU 2024 Definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> A *Database Project* is a structured, end-to-end software engineering exercise in which students **analyse a real-world problem domain, design a normalized relational schema, implement it using a DBMS (MySQL / PostgreSQL / Oracle), and build a front-end application that performs CRUD operations, query processing, transaction handling, and report generation** to satisfy specified Course Outcomes (CO4 / CO5 / CO6) of PBCMT404.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, the database mini-project is treated as the **capstone integrative assessment** that demonstrates the learner's ability to translate *requirements → ER model → relational schema → SQL DDL/DML → application code*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Filing Cabinet Metaphor"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Library as a Database:**
> Imagine a huge library with no card catalogue, no barcodes, and no librarian. You walk in looking for *"Data Structures by Seymour Lipschutz, 3rd edition"*. You wander 6 hours and leave empty-handed. That library is a **file system without a database**.
>
> Now imagine a library where every book has a barcode, every shelf is GPS-tagged, every borrower has a digital ID, and a smart librarian (the **DBMS**) can answer in 0.4 seconds *"Is this book available? Who borrowed it last? When is it due?"*. **That library is a Database System.**
>
> A *Database Project* is the act of building that smart librarian from scratch.

### 1.3 Why Module 4 Matters in the 2024 Scheme

The KTU 2024 syllabus places project guidance at the end of the course deliberately. By the time students reach Module 4, they have mastered:
- ER modelling (Module 1)
- Relational algebra & SQL (Module 2)
- Normalization & indexing (Module 3)

Module 4 is the **synthesis module** — it tells them *how to put it all together* into a deliverable that maps to the Course Outcomes.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Mapping:**
> Project topic selection directly maps to **CO4 (Design)** and **CO5 (Implement)**. A well-chosen topic is **10–15% of the internal marks** in the continuous evaluation cycle.

### 1.4 The Two Canonical Sample Projects in KTU 2024

The 2024 scheme explicitly recommends two sample projects as templates:

| # | Project | Domain | Core Complexity |
|---|---------|--------|-----------------|
| **1** | **Library Management System (LMS)** | Educational / Institutional | ⭐⭐ Medium (5–8 entities) |
| **2** | **E-commerce Product Catalogue (EPC)** | Commercial / Retail | ⭐⭐⭐ High (8–12 entities) |

These two are not random. The KTU board selected them because together they cover the **full design space**:
- LMS = strong **transactional** and **master-detail** patterns (book ↔ member ↔ loan)
- EPC = strong **cataloguing**, **search**, and **many-to-many** patterns (product ↔ category ↔ tag ↔ review)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The 7-Pillar Project Selection Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb:**
> A project gets **full internal marks** only if it satisfies the *7-Pillar Test*. Memorize this — it is the most frequently asked conceptual question in Part A.

A good KTU 2024 database project **must** satisfy:

1. **Pillar 1 — Clear Scope** — bounded domain, no "build Facebook" ambitions.
2. **Pillar 2 — Minimum 5 Entities** — to demonstrate relational modelling.
3. **Pillar 3 — Realistic Relationships** — at least one **M:N relationship** (forces a junction table).
4. **Pillar 4 — Transactional Workload** — at least one scenario requiring `BEGIN…COMMIT/ROLLBACK`.
5. **Pillar 5 — Normalization up to 3NF / BCNF** — to satisfy CO4.
6. **Pillar 6 — Non-trivial Queries** — minimum 5 reports involving `JOIN`, subquery, aggregation, or window function.
7. **Pillar 7 — Front-end Integration** — proven via JDBC / PHP-PDO / Python-connector / Django ORM.

## 2.2 KTU Cheat Sheet — Project Selection Criteria Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for viva and Part-A 3-mark questions on this topic.

| # | Selection Criterion | Why it matters for KTU valuation | Weight in evaluation |
|---|---------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Problem statement clarity** | Demonstrates requirement analysis | **10%** |
| 2 | **Number of entities (≥ 5)** | Shows breadth of ER modelling | **10%** |
| 3 | **M:N relationships present** | Forces junction table design | **10%** |
| 4 | **Normalization to 3NF / BCNF** | Maps to CO4 design outcome | **15%** |
| 5 | **Use of constraints (PK, FK, UNIQUE, CHECK)** | Maps to integrity outcome | **10%** |
| 6 | **Complex SQL queries (5+)** | Maps to CO5 implementation | **15%** |
| 7 | **Transaction handling (ACID)** | Maps to CO5 + Module 3 concepts | **10%** |
| 8 | **Working front-end demo** | Final integrative proof | **10%** |
| 9 | **Documentation (ER diagram + schema + report)** | Evaluator's primary review artifact | **10%** |

## 2.3 Library Management System — Theoretical Anatomy

### 2.3.1 Domain Understanding

A Library Management System models the **borrowing lifecycle** of physical or digital books by members, mediated by transactions called *loans*. Its core business rules are:
- A book may have **multiple copies** (one ISBN, many physical copies).
- A member can borrow **N books**, and a book can be borrowed by **N members** over time → **M:N relationship** resolved by a *Loan* entity.
- A book belongs to **one or many categories** (and categories are hierarchical in advanced versions).
- Loans have **state transitions**: `Issued → Renewed → Returned → Overdue`.

### 2.3.2 The 7 Core Entities of LMS

| # | Entity | Purpose | Key attributes |
|---|--------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **MEMBER** | Library patrons | `member_id (PK)`, `name`, `email`, `phone`, `join_date`, `membership_type` |
| 2 | **BOOK** | Catalogue of titles (ISBN-level) | `isbn (PK)`, `title`, `publisher_id (FK)`, `year`, `edition` |
| 3 | **BOOK_COPY** | Physical instances of a book | `copy_id (PK)`, `isbn (FK)`, `shelf_location`, `status` |
| 4 | **AUTHOR** | Book writers | `author_id (PK)`, `name`, `bio` |
| 5 | **PUBLISHER** | Publishing houses | `publisher_id (PK)`, `name`, `country` |
| 6 | **CATEGORY** | Subject classification | `category_id (PK)`, `name`, `parent_id (FK, self-ref)` |
| 7 | **LOAN** | Borrowing transaction (junction) | `loan_id (PK)`, `member_id (FK)`, `copy_id (FK)`, `issue_date`, `due_date`, `return_date` |

### 2.3.3 Auxiliary Entities (For Higher Marks)

| # | Entity | Purpose |
|---|--------|---------|
| 8 | **FINE** | Tracks overdue penalties — `fine_id, loan_id, amount, paid_status` |
| 9 | **RESERVATION** | Hold queue for popular books — `reservation_id, member_id, isbn, request_date` |
| 10 | **STAFF** | Librarian login — `staff_id, username, password_hash, role` |

### 2.3.4 LMS Functional Requirements (The "What" of the System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these FRs — they are repeated in viva.**

- **FR1** — Member registration and login
- **FR2** — Book search (by title, author, ISBN, category)
- **FR3** — Issue book (transactional — decrements available copy, creates loan)
- **FR4** — Return book (transactional — sets return_date, calculates fine)
- **FR5** — Renew book (extends due_date, max 2 renewals)
- **FR6** — Reserve book (adds to queue if all copies on loan)
- **FR7** — Fine calculation and payment tracking
- **FR8** — Reports: most-borrowed books, defaulters, category-wise stock, daily transactions

## 2.4 E-commerce Product Catalogue — Theoretical Anatomy

### 2.4.1 Domain Understanding

An E-commerce Product Catalogue is the **browsing + purchasing backbone** of an online store. It is fundamentally a **cataloguing system** layered with **inventory**, **pricing**, **review**, and **cart** subsystems. Its defining complexity is the **M:N relationships everywhere**:
- A product belongs to **many categories**.
- A product has **many attributes** (size, color, RAM, etc.) that vary by category.
- A customer can review **many products**; a product has **many reviews**.

### 2.4.2 The 9 Core Entities of EPC

| # | Entity | Purpose | Key attributes |
|---|--------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **CUSTOMER** | Registered shoppers | `customer_id (PK)`, `name`, `email`, `phone`, `address` |
| 2 | **PRODUCT** | Items for sale | `product_id (PK)`, `name`, `description`, `brand_id (FK)`, `base_price` |
| 3 | **CATEGORY** | Product taxonomy (often hierarchical) | `category_id (PK)`, `name`, `parent_id (FK)` |
| 4 | **PRODUCT_CATEGORY** | Junction for M:N Product↔Category | `product_id (FK)`, `category_id (FK)`, composite PK |
| 5 | **BRAND** | Manufacturer/seller brand | `brand_id (PK)`, `name`, `logo_url` |
| 6 | **PRODUCT_VARIANT** | SKU-level item (size+color combinations) | `variant_id (PK)`, `product_id (FK)`, `sku`, `price`, `stock_qty` |
| 7 | **ATTRIBUTE / ATTRIBUTE_VALUE** | EAV pattern for dynamic specs | `attribute_id (PK)`, `name` ; `value_id (PK)`, `value` |
| 8 | **PRODUCT_ATTRIBUTE** | Links product to its attributes + values | composite FK to PRODUCT, ATTRIBUTE, ATTRIBUTE_VALUE |
| 9 | **REVIEW** | Customer feedback (1-to-many from CUSTOMER) | `review_id (PK)`, `customer_id (FK)`, `product_id (FK)`, `rating (CHECK 1–5)`, `comment`, `review_date` |

### 2.4.3 Auxiliary Entities (Cart + Order Layer)

| # | Entity | Purpose |
|---|--------|---------|
| 10 | **CART** | Active shopping cart per customer — `cart_id, customer_id, created_at` |
| 11 | **CART_ITEM** | Items in cart — `cart_item_id, cart_id, variant_id, quantity` |
| 12 | **ORDER** | Placed order header — `order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount, status` |
| 13 | **ORDER_ITEM** | Order line items — `order_item_id, order_id, variant_id, quantity, unit_price` |
| 14 | **PAYMENT** | Payment record — `payment_id, order_id, amount, method, status` |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** The EAV (Entity-Attribute-Value) pattern in #7 above is *advanced* and earns bonus marks if justified in the design report.

### 2.4.4 EPC Functional Requirements

- **FR1** — Customer registration / login (with session management)
- **FR2** — Browse category tree (hierarchical recursive query)
- **FR3** — Product search with filters (price range, brand, rating, attributes)
- **FR4** — Product detail page with variants + reviews + avg rating
- **FR5** — Add to cart, update cart, view cart total
- **FR6** — Checkout (transactional — converts cart to order, decrements stock)
- **FR7** — Order history and tracking
- **FR8** — Submit review (only if customer purchased the product)
- **FR9** — Admin: CRUD on products, categories, attributes
- **FR10** — Reports: top-selling products, revenue by category, low-stock alerts

## 2.5 Why These Two Projects? — Engineering Utility

| Project | Real-world deployment | Industry use case |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **LMS** | School, college, public libraries, digital libraries | Used as the *reference ER example* in Silberschatz, Korth, Ramakrishnan, and Date textbooks |
| **EPC** | Amazon, Flipkart, Meesho, Nykaa backends | Demonstrates **cataloguing at scale** — the hardest part of e-commerce engineering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The "Product Catalogue" is *the* canonical example of a **read-heavy, denormalized-for-search, write-normalized-for-consistency** system. It teaches students that **normalization is a trade-off, not a dogma** — a key Module 3 lesson applied.

## 2.6 Project Design Phases (The Engineering Workflow)

The KTU 2024 mini-project life cycle has **6 phases**:

1. **Phase 1 — Requirement Gathering** *(Interviews / SRS document)*
2. **Phase 2 — Conceptual Design** *(ER diagram, identification of entities, relationships, cardinalities)*
3. **Phase 3 — Logical Design** *(ER → relational schema, normalization to 3NF/BCNF)*
4. **Phase 4 — Physical Design** *(DDL with constraints, indexes, partitioning if needed)*
5. **Phase 5 — Implementation** *(DML, stored procedures, triggers, application code)*
6. **Phase 6 — Testing & Documentation** *(test cases, user manual, final report)*

Each phase corresponds to a deliverable the examiner expects in the report appendix.
