# 3. Step-by-Step Schema Design — Library Management System

This section walks through the **complete transformation** of the LMS domain from requirements to a fully-normalized relational schema. Every step is justified.

## 3.1 Step 1 — Entity Identification

> **Conversion Logic:** Read the FRs and underline every *noun* that represents a uniquely identifiable real-world object.

From FRs FR1–FR8, we extract:

$$
\text{Nouns} = \{\text{Member, Book, Copy, Author, Publisher, Category, Loan, Fine, Reservation, Staff}\}
$$

Each noun becomes a candidate entity. We prune:
- **Fine** → kept as separate entity (multiplicity per loan possible: late fee + damage fee).
- **Reservation** → kept as separate entity (queue has identity).

## 3.2 Step 2 — Attribute Assignment

For each entity, we list attributes and mark the **primary key**.

$$
\text{BOOK} = \{\underline{\text{isbn}}, \text{title}, \text{year}, \text{edition}, \text{publisher\_id}\}
$$

$$
\text{BOOK\_COPY} = \{\underline{\text{copy\_id}}, \text{isbn}, \text{shelf\_location}, \text{status}\}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** `isbn` alone is not a key for BOOK_COPY because multiple physical copies share one ISBN. Hence we introduce a surrogate `copy_id`.

## 3.3 Step 3 — Relationship Identification

> **Conversion Logic:** Read the FRs and underline every *verb*. Each verb becomes a candidate relationship.

| Verb in FR | Relationship | Cardinality |
|------------|--------------|-------------|
| *Member issues Book* | MEMBER ↔ BOOK_COPY via LOAN | **M:N** |
| *Book is written by Author* | BOOK ↔ AUTHOR | **M:N** |
| *Book is published by Publisher* | BOOK ↔ PUBLISHER | **M:N : N:1** |
| *Book belongs to Category* | BOOK ↔ CATEGORY | **M:N** |
| *Member reserves Book* | MEMBER ↔ BOOK via RESERVATION | **M:N** |

**Conversion Logic:** M:N relationships **must** be resolved by introducing a junction (associative) entity. Hence LOAN, BOOK_AUTHOR, BOOK_CATEGORY, RESERVATION emerge.

## 3.4 Step 4 — Cardinality & Participation Constraints

Applying **Chen / Crow's Foot** notation:

$$
\text{LOAN} : \text{MEMBER} = N : 1 \quad \text{(partial participation on member side)}
$$

$$
\text{LOAN} : \text{BOOK\_COPY} = N : 1 \quad \text{(total participation — every loan needs a copy)}
$$

$$
\text{BOOK\_COPY} : \text{BOOK} = N : 1 \quad \text{(total — every copy references an ISBN)}
$$

## 3.5 Step 5 — ER → Relational Mapping (The Algorithm)

The KTU board expects students to know the **7-step mapping algorithm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A:} & \quad \text{For each strong entity} \rightarrow \text{create a relation with its simple attributes.}\\
\text{Step B:} & \quad \text{For each weak entity} \rightarrow \text{create a relation with partial key + FK to owner.}\\
\text{Step C:} & \quad \text{For each 1:1 relationship} \rightarrow \text{merge into one table or add FK.}\\
\text{Step D:} & \quad \text{For each 1:N relationship} \rightarrow \text{add FK on the "N" side.}\\
\text{Step E:} & \quad \text{For each M:N relationship} \rightarrow \text{create new junction relation with 2 FKs.}\\
\text{Step F:} & \quad \text{For each multivalued attribute} \rightarrow \text{create separate relation.}\\
\text{Step G:} & \quad \text{Identify and add constraints (PK, FK, UNIQUE, CHECK, NOT NULL).}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Step 6 — The Final LMS Schema (DDL)

```sql
-- Strong entities
CREATE TABLE PUBLISHER (
    publisher_id   INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    name           VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    country        VARCHAR(50)
);

CREATE TABLE AUTHOR (
    author_id      INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    name           VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    bio            TEXT
);

CREATE TABLE CATEGORY (
    category_id    INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    name           VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    parent_id      INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (parent_id) REFERENCES CATEGORY(category_id)  -- self-referencing hierarchy
);

CREATE TABLE MEMBER (
    member_id      INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    name           VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    email          VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    phone          VARCHAR(15),
    join_date      DATE DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    membership_type ENUM('STUDENT','FACULTY','PUBLIC') NOT NULL
);

-- BOOK is weak-ish; depends on PUBLISHER
CREATE TABLE BOOK (
    isbn           CHAR(13) PRIMARY KEY,
    title          VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    publisher_id   INT NOT NULL,
    year           INT CHECK (year BETWEEN 1500 AND 2030),
    edition        INT DEFAULT 1,
    FOREIGN KEY (publisher_id) REFERENCES PUBLISHER(publisher_id)
);

-- M:N: BOOK ↔ AUTHOR resolved as junction
CREATE TABLE BOOK_AUTHOR (
    isbn           CHAR(13),
    author_id      INT,
    PRIMARY KEY (isbn, author_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (isbn) REFERENCES BOOK(isbn) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (author_id) REFERENCES AUTHOR(author_id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);

-- BOOK_COPY: weak entity
CREATE TABLE BOOK_COPY (
    copy_id        INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    isbn           CHAR(13) NOT NULL,
    shelf_location VARCHAR(20),
    status         ENUM('AVAILABLE','ISSUED','RESERVED','DAMAGED') DEFAULT 'AVAILABLE',
    FOREIGN KEY (isbn) REFERENCES BOOK(isbn) ON DELETE CASCADE
);

-- M:N: MEMBER ↔ BOOK_COPY resolved as LOAN
CREATE TABLE LOAN (
    loan_id        INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    member_id      INT NOT NULL,
    copy_id        INT NOT NULL,
    issue_date     DATE NOT NULL,
    due_date       DATE NOT NULL,
    return_date    DATE,
    FOREIGN KEY (member_id) REFERENCES MEMBER(member_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (copy_id)   REFERENCES BOOK_COPY(copy_id),
    CHECK (due_date >= issue_date),
    CHECK (return_date IS NULL OR return_date >= issue_date)
);

-- FINE
CREATE TABLE FINE (
    fine_id        INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    loan_id        INT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    amount         DECIMAL(8,2) CHECK (amount >= 0),
    paid_status    BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    FOREIGN KEY (loan_id) REFERENCES LOAN(loan_id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);
```

## 3.7 Step 7 — Normalization Walkthrough (3NF / BCNF)

We verify each table against the **3NF conditions**:

$$
\forall \text{ table } T : \quad T \in 1NF \land \forall X \rightarrow A, \; X \text{ is a superkey} \Rightarrow T \in 2NF
$$

$$
T \in 3NF \iff T \in 2NF \land \text{no transitive dependencies among non-prime attributes}
$$

| Table | 1NF? | 2NF? | 3NF? | BCNF? | Issue & Resolution |
|-------|------|------|------|-------|--------------------|
| PUBLISHER | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | — |
| AUTHOR | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | — |
| BOOK | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | — |
| BOOK_AUTHOR | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Pure junction, no FD beyond PK |
| BOOK_COPY | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | `status` depends only on `copy_id` |
| LOAN | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | `member_id` and `copy_id` are FKs; no transitive dep |
| FINE | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | `amount` depends on `loan_id` (PK) |
| MEMBER | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | — |
| CATEGORY | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Self-referencing — no FDs violated |

> **Conversion Logic:** A self-referencing FK (`parent_id` in CATEGORY) does **not** violate 3NF because `parent_id` is itself a key (or part of a key) and the dependency is *parent_id → name*, which is a key-to-nonkey dependency — perfectly legal in 3NF/BCNF.

## 3.8 Step 8 — The Five Signature LMS Queries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These five queries collectively demonstrate the **full SQL power spectrum** expected by KTU examiners.

**Q1 — Issue a book (transactional):**
```sql
START TRANSACTION;
UPDATE BOOK_COPY SET status = 'ISSUED' WHERE copy_id = ? AND status = 'AVAILABLE';
INSERT INTO LOAN (member_id, copy_id, issue_date, due_date) VALUES (?, ?, CURDATE(), DATE_ADD(CURDATE(), INTERVAL 14 DAY));
COMMIT;
```

**Q2 — Find top 10 most-borrowed books in a given year:**
```sql
SELECT b.title, COUNT(l.loan_id) AS borrow_count
FROM BOOK b
JOIN BOOK_COPY bc ON b.isbn = bc.isbn
JOIN LOAN l ON bc.copy_id = l.copy_id
WHERE YEAR(l.issue_date) = 2024
GROUP BY b.isbn, b.title
ORDER BY borrow_count DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

**Q3 — List defaulters (members with overdue unreturned books):**
```sql
SELECT m.name, m.email, l.due_date
FROM MEMBER m
JOIN LOAN l ON m.member_id = l.member_id
WHERE l.return_date IS NULL AND l.due_date < CURDATE();
```

**Q4 — Calculate fine amount (₹5 per overdue day):**
```sql
SELECT l.loan_id,
       GREATEST(DATEDIFF(COALESCE(l.return_date, CURDATE()), l.due_date), 0) * 5 AS fine_amount
FROM LOAN l
WHERE l.return_date IS NULL OR l.return_date > l.due_date;
```

**Q5 — Window function: rank members by borrow count (descending):**
```sql
SELECT m.name, COUNT(l.loan_id) AS total_loans,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(l.loan_id) DESC) AS borrower_rank
FROM MEMBER m LEFT JOIN LOAN l ON m.member_id = l.member_id
GROUP BY m.member_id, m.name;
```

## 3.9 Step 9 — E-commerce Product Catalogue Schema (Condensed DDL)

```sql
CREATE TABLE CUSTOMER (
    customer_id  INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    name         VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    email        VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    phone        VARCHAR(15)
);

CREATE TABLE BRAND (
    brand_id     INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    name         VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE CATEGORY (
    category_id  INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    name         VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    parent_id    INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (parent_id) REFERENCES CATEGORY(category_id)
);

CREATE TABLE PRODUCT (
    product_id   INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    name         VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    description  TEXT,
    brand_id     INT,
    base_price   DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (base_price >= 0),
    FOREIGN KEY (brand_id) REFERENCES BRAND(brand_id)
);

-- M:N junction
CREATE TABLE PRODUCT_CATEGORY (
    product_id   INT,
    category_id  INT,
    PRIMARY KEY (product_id, category_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (product_id)  REFERENCES PRODUCT(product_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (category_id) REFERENCES CATEGORY(category_id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);

CREATE TABLE PRODUCT_VARIANT (
    variant_id   INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    product_id   INT NOT NULL,
    sku          VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    price        DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (price >= 0),
    stock_qty    INT DEFAULT 0 CHECK (stock_qty >= 0),
    FOREIGN KEY (product_id) REFERENCES PRODUCT(product_id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);

CREATE TABLE REVIEW (
    review_id    INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    customer_id  INT NOT NULL,
    product_id   INT NOT NULL,
    rating       INT CHECK (rating BETWEEN 1 AND 5),
    comment      TEXT,
    review_date  TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES CUSTOMER(customer_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (product_id)  REFERENCES PRODUCT(product_id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);
```

## 3.10 Step 10 — Normalization Verification (EPC)

| Table | 3NF check | Note |
|-------|-----------|------|
| PRODUCT_VARIANT | ✅ | `price` and `stock_qty` depend only on `variant_id` (PK) |
| PRODUCT_CATEGORY | ✅ | Pure composite-key junction |
| REVIEW | ✅ | `rating` depends on full PK |
| CATEGORY | ✅ | Self-reference, no violation |
| PRODUCT | ✅ | `base_price` depends on `product_id` (PK) — *not* on `brand_id`, hence no transitive dependency |

> **Conversion Logic:** A common student mistake is to keep `brand_name` inside PRODUCT. This violates 3NF because `brand_id → brand_name` is a transitive dependency (brand_id is not a superkey, brand_name is non-prime). The correct design keeps BRAND as a separate table.

## 4.3 Architecture Flow — How an Issue-Book Transaction Flows

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Member submits issue request] --> B[Front-end validates session]
    B --> C{Is copy available?}
    C -- No --> Z[Return error: All copies on loan]
    C -- Yes --> D[BEGIN TRANSACTION]
    D --> E[Lock BOOK_COPY row SELECT FOR UPDATE]
    E --> F[UPDATE BOOK_COPY status to ISSUED]
    F --> G[INSERT new LOAN row]
    G --> H[COMMIT]
    H --> I[Return success to front-end]
    H -. On any failure .-> R[ROLLBACK]
    R --> Z2[Return error to front-end]
```

## 4.4 Module-Level Architecture — How LMS Ties to the Course

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Module 1: ER Modelling] --> P[Project Design]
    M2[Module 2: SQL & RA] --> P
    M3[Module 3: Normalization & Indexing] --> P

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any four criteria that must be satisfied when selecting a database mini-project in the KTU 2024 scheme.**

**Model Answer (4 points, ~60 words):**
A good KTU 2024 database project must satisfy: **(i)** a clearly bounded problem domain with at least 5 entities, **(ii)** inclusion of at least one M:N relationship to justify a junction table, **(iii)** scope for normalization up to 3NF / BCNF, and **(iv)** a transactional workload that demonstrates ACID properties. These four criteria map to Course Outcomes CO4 (Design) and CO5 (Implement).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between strong and weak entities. Give one example of each from the Library Management System.**

**Model Answer (4 points, ~70 words):**

| Property | Strong Entity | Weak Entity |
|----------|---------------|-------------|
| Identifier | Has its own primary key | Depends on owner's PK |
| Existence | Independent | Cannot exist without owner |
| LMS example | `BOOK` (PK = `isbn`) | `BOOK_COPY` (PK = `copy_id` but logically depends on `isbn`) |

In ER notation, weak entities are drawn with **double rectangles** and identifying relationships with **double diamonds**.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not** list entities without justifying why they are entities (loss of 2 marks).
> - **Do not** forget to convert every M:N relationship into a junction table (loss of 3 marks).
> - **Do not** omit constraints (PK, FK, CHECK) in DDL (loss of 2 marks).
> - **Do not** skip the normalization justification (loss of 2 marks).
> - **Do not** write vague functional requirements; number them and map to entities (loss of 2 marks).
> A 14-mark answer without normalization justification rarely scores above **9/14**.

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — Library Management System

> `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

**A.** Design a **Library Management System** database for a college library. Your answer must include:
- **(a) [7 marks]** Functional requirements, ER diagram, and identification of entities, relationships (with cardinalities), and the M:N relationship(s) with resolution.
- **(b) [7 marks]** The relational schema mapped from the ER diagram (with PK, FK marked), normalization verification up to 3NF, and **any two** complex SQL queries demonstrating JOIN, aggregation, and a window function.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Functional Requirements (2 marks):**
1. Register and manage library members (students, faculty, public).
2. Maintain a catalogue of books with multiple physical copies per ISBN.
3. Issue, return, and renew books; calculate overdue fines.
4. Reserve books when all copies are on loan.
5. Search books by title, author, ISBN, or category.
6. Generate reports: most-borrowed books, defaulters, category-wise stock.

**ER Diagram Identification (3 marks):**

| Entity | Type | Reason |
|--------|------|--------|
| MEMBER, BOOK, AUTHOR, PUBLISHER, CATEGORY | Strong | Have independent primary keys |
| BOOK_COPY | Weak | Logically depends on BOOK (ISBN) |
| LOAN, RESERVATION | Associative | Resolves M:N relationships |
| FINE | Strong | Independent existence per loan event |

**Relationships & Cardinalities (2 marks):**

| Relationship | Cardinality | Resolution |
|--------------|-------------|------------|
| MEMBER — LOAN — BOOK_COPY | M:N | LOAN junction entity with `member_id` FK, `copy_id` FK |
| BOOK — BOOK_AUTHOR — AUTHOR | M:N | BOOK_AUTHOR junction table |
| BOOK — CATEGORY | M:N | BOOK_CATEGORY junction |
| PUBLISHER — BOOK | 1:N | `publisher_id` FK in BOOK |

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Relational Schema (3 marks):**

```sql
MEMBER (member_id PK, name, email UNIQUE, phone, join_date, membership_type)
PUBLISHER (publisher_id PK, name, country)
AUTHOR (author_id PK, name, bio)
CATEGORY (category_id PK, name UNIQUE, parent_id FK→CATEGORY)
BOOK (isbn PK, title, year, edition, publisher_id FK→PUBLISHER)
BOOK_COPY (copy_id PK, isbn FK→BOOK, shelf_location, status)
BOOK_AUTHOR (isbn FK, author_id FK, PRIMARY KEY(isbn, author_id))
LOAN (loan_id PK, member_id FK, copy_id FK, issue_date, due_date, return_date)
FINE (fine_id PK, loan_id FK UNIQUE, amount, paid_status)
RESERVATION (reservation_id PK, member_id FK, isbn FK, request_date)
```

**Normalization Justification (2 marks):**
Every relation is in **3NF / BCNF** because:
- All non-key attributes depend on the whole primary key (no partial dependency → 2NF).
- No non-key attribute depends transitively on a non-key attribute (→ 3NF).
- For BOOK_COPY, `status` depends only on `copy_id` (PK), so no transitive dependency exists.
- The self-referencing FK in CATEGORY (`parent_id`) does not violate 3NF because the dependency `parent_id → name` is a key-to-nonkey dependency, which is permitted.

**Two Complex SQL Queries (2 marks):**

*Query 1 — JOIN + Aggregation (top 5 most-borrowed books of 2024):*
```sql
SELECT b.title, COUNT(l.loan_id) AS borrow_count
FROM BOOK b
JOIN BOOK_COPY bc  ON b.isbn = bc.isbn
JOIN LOAN l        ON bc.copy_id = l.copy_id
WHERE YEAR(l.issue_date) = 2024
GROUP BY b.isbn, b.title
ORDER BY borrow_count DESC
LIMIT 5;
```

*Query 2 — Window Function (rank members by number of loans in 2024):*
```sql
SELECT m.name, COUNT(l.loan_id) AS loans_2024,
       DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(l.loan_id) DESC) AS rank_position
FROM MEMBER m
LEFT JOIN LOAN l ON m.member_id = l.member_id AND YEAR(l.issue_date) = 2024
GROUP BY m.member_id, m.name;
```

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — E-commerce Product Catalogue

> `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

**B.** Design an **E-commerce Product Catalogue** system. Your answer must include:
- **(a) [7 marks]** Functional requirements, identification of entities (minimum 8), the M:N relationship between Product and Category with its resolution, and the rationale for separating PRODUCT and PRODUCT_VARIANT.
- **(b) [7 marks]** The relational schema (PK, FK, CHECK constraints marked), the EAV (Entity-Attribute-Value) pattern for dynamic product attributes, and **any two** complex SQL queries — one with a self-join on the hierarchical CATEGORY and one with a correlated subquery.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Functional Requirements (2 marks):**
1. Customer registration and login.
2. Browse hierarchical product categories.
3. Product search with filters (price range, brand, rating, dynamic attributes).
4. View product details with variants, attributes, and reviews.
5. Add to cart, view cart, and checkout.
6. Submit reviews (only for purchased products).
7. Admin CRUD for products, categories, brands, attributes.
8. Reports: top sellers, revenue by category, low-stock alerts.

**Entity Identification (3 marks):**
`CUSTOMER`, `BRAND`, `CATEGORY`, `PRODUCT`, `PRODUCT_VARIANT`, `PRODUCT_CATEGORY`, `REVIEW`, `CART`, `CART_ITEM`, `ORDER`, `ORDER_ITEM`, `PAYMENT` — minimum 8 entities identified.

**M:N Relationship Resolution (1 mark):**
`PRODUCT` and `CATEGORY` are M:N because a product (e.g., a Bluetooth speaker) can belong to *Electronics*, *Audio*, and *Gifts* simultaneously, and a category contains many products. This is resolved by the **PRODUCT_CATEGORY** junction table with composite primary key `(product_id, category_id)`.

**Why separate PRODUCT and PRODUCT_VARIANT? (1 mark):**
PRODUCT represents the **logical** item (e.g., "Nike Air Max 90"), while PRODUCT_VARIANT represents the **physical SKU** (e.g., size 9 + red, size 10 + black). They differ in price and stock, hence must be separate entities to avoid 3NF violation (variant-level attributes would transitively depend on a non-key).

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Relational Schema (3 marks):**

```sql
CUSTOMER (customer_id PK, name, email UNIQUE, phone)
BRAND (brand_id PK, name UNIQUE)
CATEGORY (category_id PK, name, parent_id FK→CATEGORY)
PRODUCT (product_id PK, name, description, brand_id FK, base_price CHECK (base_price >= 0))
PRODUCT_VARIANT (variant_id PK, product_id FK, sku UNIQUE, price CHECK (price >= 0), stock_qty CHECK (stock_qty >= 0))
PRODUCT_CATEGORY (product_id FK, category_id FK, PRIMARY KEY(product_id, category_id))
REVIEW (review_id PK, customer_id FK, product_id FK, rating CHECK (rating BETWEEN 1 AND 5), comment, review_date)
```

**EAV Pattern Justification (2 marks):**
Different categories need different attributes. *Electronics* needs `RAM`, `battery_life`; *Clothing* needs `size`, `material`. Hard-coding columns in PRODUCT leads to nulls and 3NF violations. The EAV pattern resolves this:

```sql
ATTRIBUTE (attribute_id PK, name)
ATTRIBUTE_VALUE (value_id PK, attribute_id FK, value)
PRODUCT_ATTRIBUTE (product_id FK, attribute_id FK, value_id FK,
                  PRIMARY KEY(product_id, attribute_id, value_id))
```

This keeps the schema **sparse, dynamic, and 3NF-compliant**.

**Two Complex SQL Queries (2 marks):**

*Query 1 — Self-join on hierarchical CATEGORY (list parent with immediate children):*
```sql
SELECT p.name AS parent_category, c.name AS child_category
FROM CATEGORY p
JOIN CATEGORY c ON c.parent_id = p.category_id
ORDER BY p.name, c.name;
```

*Query 2 — Correlated subquery (products whose average rating is above the global average):*
```sql
SELECT p.name, p.product_id
FROM PRODUCT p
WHERE (SELECT AVG(r.rating) FROM REVIEW r WHERE r.product_id = p.product_id)
      > (SELECT AVG(rating) FROM REVIEW);
```

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Project Topics)**

- ✅ A KTU 2024 database project must satisfy the **7-Pillar Test**: clear scope, ≥5 entities, M:N relationship, transactional workload, 3NF/BCNF normalization, ≥5 non-trivial queries, and a working front-end.
- ✅ The two canonical sample projects are **Library Management System (LMS)** and **E-commerce Product Catalogue (EPC)** — they together cover the master-detail, M:N, and hierarchical patterns.
- ✅ **LMS Core Entities** = MEMBER, BOOK, BOOK_COPY (weak), AUTHOR, PUBLISHER, CATEGORY (self-ref), LOAN, FINE, RESERVATION.
- ✅ **EPC Core Entities** = CUSTOMER, PRODUCT, PRODUCT_VARIANT, BRAND, CATEGORY (self-ref), PRODUCT_CATEGORY (junction), REVIEW, CART, ORDER, PAYMENT.
- ✅ Always resolve **M:N** with a **junction table** having a composite primary key.
- ✅ Always separate **PRODUCT** and **PRODUCT_VARIANT** in EPC to keep 3NF — a single product table with size/color columns is a **classic 3NF violation**.
- ✅ Use the **EAV pattern** (`ATTRIBUTE`, `ATTRIBUTE_VALUE`, `PRODUCT_ATTRIBUTE`) for dynamic product specs in EPC.
- ✅ The **7-step ER-to-Relational mapping algorithm** is examinable — memorize it verbatim.
- ✅ A good project report must contain: SRS, ER diagram, normalized schema (DDL), ≥5 complex queries, transaction examples, front-end screenshots, test cases, and a bibliography.
- ✅ The 6-phase design lifecycle is: **Requirements → Conceptual → Logical → Physical → Implementation → Testing**.
- ✅ **Window functions** (`RANK`, `DENSE_RANK`, `ROW_NUMBER`, `LAG`, `LEAD`) and **CTEs** (`WITH … AS`) earn bonus marks in query sections.
- ✅ **Common valuation loss points** (avoid these to gain 2–3 extra marks): missing cardinality labels, missing CHECK constraints, no justification of normalization, vague functional requirements, and no M:N resolution explanation.
